1.title: Case study overview
1.content: This case study applies supervised learning and logistic regression to a diabetes risk prediction problem. The aim is not just to fit a model and report performance numbers. The aim is to think like a medical statistician: define the clinical question clearly, understand the outcome, check whether the predictors make sense, evaluate performance properly, and interpret the results in a clinically meaningful way. In this case study, we focus on: . | defining a binary clinical prediction problem; | identifying the outcome and predictors; | splitting data into training and test sets; | fitting a logistic regression model; | evaluating classification performance; | interpreting sensitivity, specificity, ROC AUC, and calibration; | understanding how threshold choice changes clinical interpretation; | understanding the limitations of an educational dataset; | thinking about how this type of model would need to be validated before real use. | .

2.title: Clinical question
2.content: The clinical question is: . Can we predict whether a patient has diabetes using routinely measured clinical characteristics? . This is a binary classification problem because the outcome has two categories: . Diabetes: yes/no . The model estimates the probability that a patient belongs to the diabetes-positive group based on their clinical measurements. In a real-world setting, this type of model could potentially support screening, risk stratification, or identifying patients who may need further testing. However, this example is for teaching only and should not be interpreted as a deployable clinical model.

3.title: Why this case study matters
3.content: Diabetes risk prediction is a useful first case study because it brings together many issues that appear in real medical machine learning. A model may look statistically strong, but that does not automatically mean it is clinically useful. We still need to ask: . | Is the outcome clearly defined? | Are the predictors clinically meaningful? | Are all predictors available at the time of prediction? | Does the model perform well on unseen data? | Is sensitivity high enough for screening? | Is specificity high enough to avoid too many false positives? | Are predicted probabilities well calibrated? | Would the model generalise to another population? | . These questions are more important than simply asking whether the model has high accuracy.

4.title: Dataset
4.content: This case study uses the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset, a commonly used teaching dataset for binary classification. The dataset is used to predict whether a patient has diabetes based on diagnostic measurements, and it includes variables such as pregnancies, glucose, blood pressure, skin thickness, insulin, BMI, diabetes pedigree function, age, and diabetes outcome. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0} . The dataset contains clinical measurements including: . | number of pregnancies; | plasma glucose concentration; | blood pressure; | skinfold thickness; | insulin; | body mass index; | diabetes pedigree function; | age; | diabetes status. | . This dataset is useful for learning the workflow of clinical prediction modelling. However, it should not be treated as a modern, representative, deployable clinical dataset.

5.title: Outcome
5.content: The outcome is: . Diabetes status . The positive class is: . Diabetes = positive . The negative class is: . Diabetes = negative . Defining the positive class clearly is essential because sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value all depend on which group is treated as the event.

6.title: Predictors
6.content: The candidate predictors are: . | Predictor | Clinical interpretation | . | Pregnancies | Number of pregnancies | . | Glucose | Plasma glucose concentration | . | Blood pressure | Diastolic blood pressure | . | Triceps | Skinfold thickness | . | Insulin | Serum insulin | . | BMI / mass | Body mass index | . | Pedigree | Diabetes pedigree function | . | Age | Age in years | . Some predictors, such as glucose, BMI, insulin, and age, have clear clinical relevance. Others may require more careful interpretation. A key principle in medical prediction is that predictors should be available at the time the model is intended to make a prediction.

7.title: Prediction time point
7.content: For this teaching example, we can think of the prediction time point as: . At the time of clinical assessment . This means the model should only use information available at that moment. For example, glucose, BMI, blood pressure, insulin, and age are available at assessment. Future information should not be used. This matters because using information measured after the prediction time point would create data leakage.

8.title: Modelling approach
8.content: We use logistic regression as the baseline model. Logistic regression estimates: . P(diabetes = positive | predictors) . This means the model estimates the probability of diabetes given the patient’s clinical measurements. Logistic regression is a good first model because it is: . | interpretable; | familiar in medical statistics; | suitable for binary outcomes; | able to produce predicted probabilities; | useful as a baseline before more complex machine learning methods. | . A more complex model should only be preferred if it improves clinically meaningful performance after proper validation.

9.title: Analysis workflow
9.content: The case study follows this workflow: . | Load the dataset. | Define the outcome and predictors. | Check the outcome distribution. | Split the data into training and test sets. | Fit logistic regression on the training set. | Predict probabilities in the test set. | Convert probabilities into predicted classes using a threshold. | Calculate classification metrics. | Plot the ROC curve. | Plot a simple calibration curve. | Compare different probability thresholds. | Interpret the results clinically. | .

10.title: Step 1: Outcome distribution
10.content: Before fitting a model, we first examine the outcome distribution. This helps us understand whether the dataset is balanced or imbalanced. If one class is much more common than the other, accuracy can become misleading. For example, if most patients are diabetes negative, a model could achieve apparently high accuracy by predicting negative for most patients. Interpretation . The diabetes-negative group is larger than the diabetes-positive group. In the full dataset used here, there are approximately: . Diabetes negative: 500 patients Diabetes positive: 268 patients . This means the outcome is moderately imbalanced. The imbalance is not extreme, but it is enough that we should not rely only on accuracy. A model could look reasonably accurate while still missing many diabetes-positive patients. Show clinical interpretation The outcome distribution shows why classification metrics need to be interpreted carefully. Because diabetes-negative patients are more common, accuracy alone can give an incomplete picture. For a screening-type model, we especially need to examine sensitivity, because missing true diabetes-positive cases could delay diagnosis or follow-up. This is why the case study later examines sensitivity, specificity, ROC AUC, Brier score, calibration, and threshold behaviour.

11.title: Step 2: Train/test split
11.content: The data are split into: . Training set: used to fit the model Test set: used to evaluate model performance . In this script, the split is: . 80% training 20% testing . The test set is important because it gives a better estimate of performance on unseen patients than training performance alone. In medical prediction, the key question is not: . How well does the model fit the development data? . The key question is: . How well does the model perform for patients not used to train it? .

12.title: Step 3: Logistic regression model
12.content: The logistic regression model uses clinical predictors to estimate the probability of diabetes. Conceptually, the model is: . logit(P(diabetes positive)) = β0 + β1(pregnancies) + β2(glucose) + β3(blood pressure) + β4(triceps) + β5(insulin) + β6(BMI) + β7(pedigree) + β8(age) . The model produces predicted probabilities between 0 and 1. For example: . Predicted probability of diabetes = 0.72 . This means that, based on the model, the patient has an estimated 72% probability of being diabetes positive.

13.title: Step 4: Model coefficients and odds ratios
13.content: The fitted logistic regression model produced the following broad pattern: . | Predictor | Direction in this fitted model | Interpretation | . | Pregnancies | Positive | Higher number of pregnancies was associated with higher predicted diabetes risk | . | Glucose | Positive | Higher glucose was associated with higher predicted diabetes risk | . | Blood pressure | Slightly negative | The fitted coefficient was slightly below zero in this model | . | Triceps | Very close to zero | Little independent contribution in this fitted model | . | Insulin | Very close to zero | Little independent contribution in this fitted model | . | BMI / mass | Positive | Higher BMI was associated with higher predicted diabetes risk | . | Pedigree | Positive | Higher pedigree score was associated with higher predicted diabetes risk | . | Age | Slightly positive | Older age was associated with higher predicted diabetes risk | . In the fitted model output, glucose, BMI, pregnancies, and diabetes pedigree function had stronger evidence of association with diabetes status than some of the other predictors. However, this should not be overinterpreted. This is one train/test split in an educational dataset. The purpose is to understand the modelling workflow, not to make definitive clinical claims. Show statistical interpretation The model output showed, for example: - glucose odds ratio around 1.04 per unit increase; - BMI odds ratio around 1.09 per unit increase; - pedigree odds ratio around 2.18; - pregnancies odds ratio around 1.13. These odds ratios are conditional on the other predictors in the model. An odds ratio above 1 means higher odds of diabetes positivity as the predictor increases, holding the other predictors fixed. An odds ratio below 1 means lower odds, holding the other predictors fixed. For prediction, however, the main focus is not only individual p-values or odds ratios. We also need to evaluate how well the model predicts in test data.

14.title: Step 5: Predicted probabilities and threshold
14.content: Predicted probabilities are converted into predicted classes using a threshold. In this case study, we start with: . Threshold = 0.50 . This means: . Predicted probability >= 0.50 → diabetes positive Predicted probability < 0.50 → diabetes negative . However, a threshold of 0.50 is not automatically clinically appropriate. For screening, a lower threshold may be preferred because it increases sensitivity and reduces missed cases. For confirmatory decisions, a higher threshold may be preferred because it increases specificity and reduces false positives.

15.title: Step 6: Classification results at threshold 0.50
15.content: Using a threshold of 0.50, the confusion matrix was: . |   | Actual negative | Actual positive | . | Predicted negative | 92 | 22 | . | Predicted positive | 10 | 30 | . This means: . | 92 patients were correctly classified as diabetes negative; | 30 patients were correctly classified as diabetes positive; | 10 diabetes-negative patients were incorrectly classified as positive; | 22 diabetes-positive patients were missed by the model. | . The performance metrics were: . | Metric | Value | . | Accuracy | 0.792 | . | Sensitivity | 0.577 | . | Specificity | 0.902 | . | Positive predictive value | 0.750 | . | Negative predictive value | 0.807 | . Interpretation . At the 0.50 threshold, the model is better at identifying diabetes-negative patients than diabetes-positive patients. The specificity is high: . Specificity = 0.902 . This means the model correctly identifies about 90% of diabetes-negative patients. But the sensitivity is lower: . Sensitivity = 0.577 . This means the model detects only about 58% of diabetes-positive patients at this threshold. Show clinical interpretation This is an important clinical point. At the 0.50 threshold, the model misses 22 out of 52 diabetes-positive patients in the test set. For a screening tool, this may be too many missed cases. A screening model usually needs high sensitivity because the cost of missing disease can be serious. However, the model has high specificity, meaning it produces relatively fewer false positives at this threshold. So the default 0.50 threshold may be more conservative. It avoids many false positives, but it misses more true diabetes-positive cases.

16.title: Step 7: ROC curve and AUC
16.content: The ROC curve shows the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity across different thresholds. AUC measures discrimination. Discrimination asks: . Can the model assign higher predicted risks to patients with diabetes than to patients without diabetes? . Interpretation . The test-set AUC was: . AUC = 0.851 . This suggests that the model has useful discrimination in this test set. In plain language, the model is reasonably good at ranking patients by diabetes risk. Patients with diabetes tend to receive higher predicted risks than patients without diabetes. However, AUC does not tell us whether predicted probabilities are accurate. It also does not tell us which threshold should be used in practice. Show clinical interpretation The AUC of 0.851 is encouraging for a teaching example. It suggests that the logistic regression model separates diabetes-positive and diabetes-negative patients reasonably well in the test set. However, the confusion matrix shows that the default threshold of 0.50 gives sensitivity of only 0.577. This means that a good AUC does not automatically mean that the chosen threshold is clinically suitable. Before clinical use, we would need: - threshold-specific evaluation; - calibration assessment; - external validation; - clinical usefulness assessment; - subgroup and fairness checks.

17.title: Step 8: Brier score
17.content: The Brier score measures the average squared difference between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes. In this case study, the Brier score was: . Brier score = 0.149 . Lower Brier scores are better. The Brier score is useful because it evaluates probability prediction, not only classification. A model could classify many patients correctly but still give poorly calibrated probabilities. The Brier score helps us think about the quality of the predicted risks.

18.title: Step 9: Calibration plot
18.content: Calibration assesses whether predicted probabilities agree with observed risks. For example, if a group of patients has an average predicted risk of 30%, then approximately 30% of that group should be diabetes positive if the model is well calibrated. Interpretation . Calibration assesses whether predicted probabilities agree with observed risks. A well-calibrated model should have points close to the diagonal line. If points are above the diagonal line, the observed risk is higher than the predicted risk. This suggests the model may be underestimating risk in that range. If points are below the diagonal line, the observed risk is lower than the predicted risk. This suggests the model may be overestimating risk in that range. In this plot, the overall trend follows the diagonal reasonably, especially at lower and higher risk ranges. However, some middle-risk groups move away from the line. For example: . | Risk group | Mean predicted risk | Observed risk | . | 5 | 0.235 | 0.400 | . | 6 | 0.323 | 0.200 | . | 7 | 0.409 | 0.600 | . | 8 | 0.531 | 0.400 | . This shows that calibration is somewhat unstable in the middle of the risk range. Show clinical interpretation Calibration is especially important when predicted probabilities are used to guide clinical decisions. For example, if a patient is told that their predicted diabetes risk is 40%, that number should be meaningful. In this case study, the calibration plot is useful for teaching, but it should be interpreted cautiously. The test set is small, and each calibration group contains only around 15 or 16 patients. With such small groups, the observed risk can jump around. So this plot should not be read as definitive evidence that the model is well or poorly calibrated. Instead, it teaches the idea that predicted probabilities need to be checked against observed outcomes.

19.title: Step 10: Threshold interpretation
19.content: A threshold converts predicted probabilities into predicted classes. The script compared several thresholds: . | Threshold | Accuracy | Sensitivity | Specificity | . | 0.20 | 0.701 | 0.962 | 0.569 | . | 0.30 | 0.714 | 0.788 | 0.676 | . | 0.40 | 0.766 | 0.673 | 0.814 | . | 0.50 | 0.792 | 0.577 | 0.902 | . | 0.60 | 0.786 | 0.481 | 0.941 | . | 0.70 | 0.773 | 0.404 | 0.961 | . This table shows how strongly the choice of threshold changes clinical behaviour. At threshold 0.20: . Sensitivity = 0.962 Specificity = 0.569 . At threshold 0.50: . Sensitivity = 0.577 Specificity = 0.902 . At threshold 0.70: . Sensitivity = 0.404 Specificity = 0.961 . Interpretation . Lower thresholds detect more diabetes-positive patients, but they also create more false positives. Higher thresholds reduce false positives, but they miss more diabetes-positive patients. Show clinical interpretation If this model were being considered for screening, a threshold lower than 0.50 may be more appropriate because screening usually prioritises sensitivity. For example, at threshold 0.20, sensitivity is very high at 0.962. This means very few diabetes-positive patients are missed. However, specificity falls to 0.569, so many diabetes-negative patients may be flagged for further testing. If the model were used for a more conservative decision, such as deciding who should receive an intervention with costs or risks, a higher threshold may be preferred. This is why there is no universally correct threshold. Threshold choice depends on clinical purpose and the consequences of false positives and false negatives.

20.title: Clinical interpretation
20.content: The model shows useful discrimination, with a test AUC of 0.851. However, the default 0.50 threshold gives a very specific but less sensitive model: . Sensitivity = 0.577 Specificity = 0.902 . This means that at the default threshold, the model is better at ruling out diabetes-negative patients than detecting all diabetes-positive patients. For a screening use case, this may not be ideal because many true diabetes-positive patients could be missed. A lower threshold may be more suitable if the aim is to capture as many possible diabetes-positive cases as possible. A balanced interpretation should consider: . | sensitivity; | specificity; | positive predictive value; | negative predictive value; | ROC AUC; | Brier score; | calibration; | prediction threshold; | intended clinical use. | . The model should not be judged only by accuracy or AUC.

21.title: What could go wrong?
21.content: Several problems can affect this analysis. 1. Accuracy can be misleading . The model has accuracy of 0.792 at the 0.50 threshold. This sounds good, but accuracy alone hides the fact that sensitivity is only 0.577. A model can appear accurate while still missing a clinically important number of diabetes-positive patients. 2. The threshold may not match the clinical purpose . At threshold 0.50, the model is highly specific but less sensitive. For screening, this may not be appropriate. The threshold should be chosen based on clinical consequences, not simply because 0.50 is a common default. 3. The model may not generalise . Performance on one dataset does not guarantee performance in another population. A model developed in one population may perform poorly in another population because of differences in: . | demographics; | healthcare setting; | measurement methods; | disease prevalence; | clinical practice. | . 4. Calibration may be uncertain . The calibration plot uses small groups of patients. Some points deviate from the diagonal, especially in the middle of the risk range. This means predicted probabilities should be interpreted cautiously. 5. Predictors may not be available in practice . A model is only useful if all predictors are available at the time of prediction. If a predictor is expensive, unavailable, or measured inconsistently, the model may not be practical. 6. The dataset may not represent the target population . The Pima dataset is useful for teaching, but it may not represent the population where a real diabetes prediction model would be used. External validation would be essential before considering real-world use.

22.title: Limitations
22.content: This is an educational case study. The model should not be treated as a deployable clinical tool. Before real-world use, a diabetes risk model would require: . | careful data quality assessment; | clinically justified predictor selection; | internal validation; | external validation; | calibration assessment; | threshold selection based on clinical consequences; | decision curve analysis; | fairness and subgroup performance checks; | transparent reporting. | .

23.title: Reflection questions
23.content: . | What is the outcome in this case study? | What is the positive class? | Which predictors are most clinically meaningful? | Why is accuracy alone not enough? | Why is sensitivity important for screening? | Why might specificity matter in clinical follow-up? | What does ROC AUC measure? | What does the Brier score measure? | What does calibration measure? | Why might threshold 0.50 not be appropriate for screening? | Why might a model work poorly in a new population? | What additional validation would be needed before clinical use? | . Show suggested answers 1. The outcome is diabetes status. 2. The positive class is diabetes positive. 3. Clinically meaningful predictors include glucose, BMI, age, blood pressure, insulin, and family-history-related measures. 4. Accuracy alone is not enough because it can hide poor sensitivity or poor specificity. 5. Sensitivity matters because missing true diabetes cases may delay diagnosis or intervention. 6. Specificity matters because false positives can lead to unnecessary follow-up and anxiety. 7. ROC AUC measures discrimination, or the model’s ability to rank diabetes-positive patients higher than diabetes-negative patients. 8. The Brier score measures prediction error for predicted probabilities. 9. Calibration assesses whether predicted probabilities match observed risks. 10. Threshold 0.50 may miss too many diabetes-positive patients if the purpose is screening. 11. A model may work poorly in a new population because of differences in demographics, measurement, disease prevalence, healthcare setting, or clinical practice. 12. External validation, calibration assessment, clinical usefulness evaluation, subgroup performance checks, and transparent reporting would be needed.

24.title: Related course material
24.content: . | Lecture 3: Supervised Learning for Medical Data | Note 3: Supervised Learning for Medical Data | Exercise 3: Supervised Learning for Medical Data . | Lecture 4: Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction | Note 4: Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction | Exercise 4: Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction . | Lecture 5: Classification Metrics in Medicine | Note 5: Classification Metrics in Medicine | Exercise 5: Classification Metrics in Medicine . | Note 8: Calibration and Brier Score | Exercise 8: Calibration and Brier Score | .

25.title: Code
25.content: The R code for this case study is: . r/C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R . After running the script, the figures should be saved in: . figures/ . Expected output files: . figures/diabetes_outcome_distribution.png figures/diabetes_roc_curve.png figures/diabetes_calibration_plot.png .

26.title: Key takeaway
26.content: This logistic regression model has useful discrimination in the test set, with AUC 0.851. However, the default 0.50 threshold gives sensitivity 0.577 and specificity 0.902, meaning the model is more conservative and misses some diabetes-positive patients. The case study shows why medical prediction models must be interpreted using discrimination, calibration, threshold-specific metrics, and clinical context rather than accuracy alone.

28.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
28.title: Lecture 1: What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?

29.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
29.title: Opening scene
29.content: Scene: A classroom inside a hospital data lab Curious Learner: “Everyone keeps talking about machine learning. Is it just fancy algorithms with complicated names?” . Prof Stat: “Not exactly. In biostatistics, machine learning is mainly about learning useful patterns from health data.” . Dr Clinic: “And those patterns must mean something clinically. I cannot use a model just because it looks impressive.” . Leakage Monster: “Unless you accidentally include future information. Then the model looks amazing!” . Prof Stat: “That is exactly why medical machine learning needs careful thinking.” .

30.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
30.title: Today’s big question
30.content: What does machine learning mean in biostatistics and medical research? . In this course, machine learning is not treated as magic. It is treated as a set of statistical and computational tools that help us learn from data and make predictions. In medical research, we may want to predict: . | whether a patient has a disease; | whether a patient will be readmitted to hospital; | whether cancer may recur; | whether a patient may respond to treatment; | whether a patient is at high risk of clinical deterioration; | survival probability over time. | . The goal is not just to build a model. The goal is to build a model that is valid, interpretable, clinically meaningful, and useful on new patients.

31.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
31.title: Intuition first
31.content: Imagine a clinician seeing many patients over time. The clinician starts noticing patterns: . | patients with higher glucose often have higher diabetes risk; | patients with advanced tumour stage may have worse prognosis; | patients with repeated hospital admissions may have higher readmission risk; | some biomarkers may be linked with treatment response. | . Machine learning tries to learn such patterns from data in a structured way. But there is one important rule: . A model is only useful if it performs well on new patients, not just on the patients used to build it.

32.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
32.title: Prediction versus explanation
32.content: Curious Learner: “So machine learning tells us what causes disease?” . Prof Stat: “Sometimes people confuse this. Prediction and causal explanation are different goals.” . Dr Clinic: “So a model can predict risk without proving that a variable causes the disease?” . Prof Stat: “Exactly.” . There are two different types of questions. | Question type | Example | Main aim | . | Inference | Does smoking increase lung cancer risk? | Understand association or causal effect | . | Prediction | Can we predict lung cancer risk? | Predict outcome accurately for new patients | . Both are important, but they are not the same. A prediction model may use variables because they improve prediction, even if they are not causal. A causal or explanatory model focuses more on estimating and interpreting effects.

33.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
33.title: Medical example
33.content: Suppose we build a model to predict diabetes using: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | insulin; | blood pressure. | . The model may estimate: . Predicted diabetes risk = 0.72 . This means the model estimates a 72% probability that the patient is diabetes positive. But before trusting this model, we should ask: . | Was the model tested on unseen patients? | Is the prediction well calibrated? | Is sensitivity high enough? | Is specificity acceptable? | Would it work in another population? | Are the predictors available in real clinical practice? | .

34.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
34.title: Mini conversation: the accuracy trap
34.content: Curious Learner: “My model has 95% accuracy. So it is excellent, right?” . Dr Clinic: “Maybe. But what if only 5% of patients actually have the disease?” . Prof Stat: “Then a model could predict everyone as disease-negative and still appear highly accurate.” . Leakage Monster: “And everyone celebrates the model while it misses all real cases!” . Prof Stat: “That is why medical machine learning needs more than accuracy.” . Accuracy is useful, but it is not enough. In medicine, we often need to examine: . | sensitivity; | specificity; | positive predictive value; | negative predictive value; | ROC AUC; | calibration; | clinical usefulness; | external validation. | .

35.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
35.title: What makes medical machine learning different?
35.content: General machine learning often focuses on predictive performance. Medical machine learning also cares about: . | patient safety; | clinical interpretation; | calibration; | fairness; | generalisability; | reproducibility; | transparent reporting; | external validation; | decision consequences. | . A model can perform well in one dataset but fail in another hospital, population, time period, or healthcare setting. This is why a medical prediction model should not be judged only by whether the algorithm is advanced. It should be judged by whether it is valid and useful in the intended setting.

36.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
36.title: Common mistake
36.content: Common mistake: thinking complex models are automatically better . A complex model is not automatically better than a simpler model. For example, a random forest or boosting model may perform well, but if it is poorly validated or difficult to interpret, it may not be useful clinically. A logistic regression model with good validation, good calibration, and clear interpretation may be more useful than a black-box model with weak reporting.

37.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
37.title: Clinical reality check
37.content: Clinical reality check . If a model predicts: . Predicted risk = 40% . a clinician needs to know: . | Is 40% close to the true observed risk? | What should be done at 40% risk? | What are the harms of false positives? | What are the harms of false negatives? | Has the model been tested externally? | Was the model developed without data leakage? | . A prediction is only useful if it supports a meaningful decision.

38.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
38.title: The role of R in this course
38.content: This course is R-based. R is widely used in medical statistics, epidemiology, clinical prediction modelling, survival analysis, and biomedical data science. In later sections, we will use R to: . | prepare medical datasets; | fit prediction models; | calculate performance metrics; | create ROC curves; | assess calibration; | build reproducible case studies; | interpret results clinically. | . The aim is not just to run R code. The aim is to understand what the results mean in a medical setting.

39.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
39.title: Key takeaway
39.content: Machine learning in biostatistics is not just about algorithms. It is about building, validating, interpreting, and reporting prediction models that may support medical research or clinical decision-making. A useful medical machine learning model should be: . | valid; | calibrated; | interpretable; | reproducible; | clinically meaningful; | tested on unseen data. | .

40.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
40.title: Reflection question
40.content: A model predicts cancer recurrence with very high accuracy in the training dataset. Before trusting it on new patients, what questions would you ask? . Show suggested answer You should ask whether the model was evaluated on unseen data, whether the prediction time point was clearly defined, whether any data leakage occurred, whether the model is calibrated, whether sensitivity and specificity are clinically acceptable, and whether the model has been externally validated in another population.

41.doc: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?
41.title: Lecture 1 - What is Machine Learning in Biostatistics?

42.title: Machine Learning in Biostatistics: Prediction vs Inference

43.title: Learning objectives
43.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain what machine learning means from a statistical learning perspective. | Describe why machine learning has become important in biostatistics, epidemiology, clinical prediction, and biomedical data science. | Distinguish clearly between prediction, inference, association, and causal inference. | Explain why a model that estimates an association well may not necessarily predict well. | Explain why a model that predicts well may not necessarily support causal or mechanistic interpretation. | Understand why medical machine learning must be evaluated using validation, calibration, discrimination, clinical usefulness, and transparent reporting. | Recognise common ways in which prediction and inference are confused in medical research. | .

44.title: 1. Why start with prediction vs inference?
44.content: Machine learning is often introduced through algorithms: linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, neural networks, and boosting. Although algorithms are important, they are not the best starting point for biostatistics. In medical statistics, the first question should not be “which algorithm should I use?” The first question should be “what scientific or clinical question am I trying to answer?” . This is why the distinction between prediction and inference is foundational. In biostatistics, the same dataset can often be used to answer very different questions. For example, a dataset containing smoking status, age, sex, lung cancer diagnosis, and other clinical variables could be used to ask whether smoking is associated with lung cancer. It could also be used to build a model that predicts an individual patient’s future risk of lung cancer. These two analyses may use similar variables and even similar statistical models, but the aims, modelling choices, evaluation criteria, and interpretation are different. James et al. describe statistical learning as a set of tools for modelling and understanding complex datasets (James et al., 2023). That definition is deliberately broad. Some statistical learning methods are used to understand relationships between variables, while others are used primarily to predict outcomes. In medical research, both aims are valuable, but they should not be mixed without careful thought. A major source of poor medical machine learning is not the use of the wrong algorithm. It is the use of the wrong framework: treating a prediction problem like an inference problem, or treating an inference problem like a prediction problem.

45.title: 2. What is machine learning in biostatistics?
45.content: Machine learning can be described as a collection of statistical and computational methods that learn patterns from data and use those patterns for prediction, classification, clustering, or decision support. In a biostatistical context, machine learning usually means using patient-level, population-level, or biomedical data to predict clinically or scientifically meaningful outcomes. Examples include predicting: . | whether a patient has a disease; | whether a patient will be readmitted to hospital; | whether a tumour will recur; | whether a patient will respond to treatment; | whether an individual is at high risk of cardiovascular disease; | a patient’s survival probability at 1, 3, or 5 years; | disease status from gene expression or DNA methylation data. | . The general supervised learning setup can be written as: . Y = f(X) + ε . Here, Y is the outcome, X represents predictors or features, f(X) is the unknown relationship between predictors and outcome, and ε represents random variation or unexplained error. In medical applications, Y might be disease status, mortality, relapse, response to treatment, or survival time. Predictors X might include age, sex, blood pressure, tumour stage, laboratory values, comorbidities, clinical history, imaging features, or molecular markers. This mathematical form looks simple, but it hides several important medical questions. What exactly is the outcome? When is the prediction being made? Are all predictors available at that time? Is the target population clearly defined? Is the model intended for diagnosis, prognosis, screening, monitoring, or treatment decision-making? These questions matter because a model that looks statistically impressive can still be clinically inappropriate. Steyerberg emphasises that prediction models in medicine require careful development, validation, and updating if they are to support clinical decision-making (Steyerberg, 2019). Similarly, TRIPOD+AI provides reporting guidance for clinical prediction models using either regression or machine learning methods, reflecting the idea that machine learning models should be held to transparent reporting standards (Collins et al., 2024).

46.title: 3. Why machine learning matters in modern biostatistics
46.content: Biostatistics has traditionally been strongly associated with study design, estimation, hypothesis testing, regression modelling, survival analysis, causal inference, and uncertainty quantification. These areas remain central. Machine learning does not replace them. Instead, it adds tools for handling prediction, complex patterns, high-dimensional predictors, nonlinearities, interactions, and large-scale biomedical data. Medical datasets are becoming more complex. A modern health data scientist may work with electronic health records, national registry data, clinical trial datasets, imaging-derived features, genomic data, transcriptomic data, methylation arrays, proteomics, metabolomics, wearable-device data, or linked administrative datasets. Some of these datasets contain thousands or millions of variables. Others contain repeated measurements over time. Some include rare outcomes, missing data, censoring, or measurement differences across hospitals. Classical regression models remain extremely useful for many of these problems. Logistic regression, Cox regression, Poisson regression, mixed models, and generalised linear models are not outdated. In fact, they are often the most appropriate starting point. However, machine learning methods such as penalised regression, random forests, gradient boosting, support vector machines, and neural networks can sometimes capture structures that simpler models miss. The central challenge in medical machine learning is that better flexibility can come with higher risk. Flexible models may fit nonlinear relationships and interactions, but they may also overfit, exploit leakage, learn site-specific artefacts, or produce poorly calibrated risk estimates. Therefore, the goal is not simply to use the most advanced algorithm. The goal is to build a model that answers a clearly defined medical question and performs reliably in the target setting.

47.title: 4. Machine learning in medicine is not just algorithm fitting
47.content: A common beginner approach is to load a dataset, try many models, compare accuracy, and choose the model with the highest number. This approach may be acceptable for a simple classroom demonstration, but it is not sufficient for medical statistics. A medical prediction model should be developed around a clinical question. Consider the difference between these two statements: . We trained an XGBoost model and achieved 91% accuracy. and . We developed and internally validated a model to predict 30-day hospital readmission at the time of discharge, using predictors available before discharge, and evaluated discrimination, calibration, and potential clinical utility. The second statement is much stronger because it defines the prediction setting. It tells us when the prediction is made, what outcome is being predicted, what type of validation was performed, and which aspects of performance were assessed. TRIPOD+AI emphasises transparent reporting of the data source, study design, predictors, outcome, model development, validation, performance, and intended use (Collins et al., 2024). PROBAST+AI similarly focuses on risk of bias and applicability in prediction model studies using regression or artificial intelligence methods (Moons et al., 2025). A model in medicine should be assessed using questions such as: . | What is the target population? | What decision or clinical process might the model support? | What is the outcome, and how is it measured? | What is the prediction time point? | Are the predictors available at the prediction time? | Was missing data handled appropriately? | Was the model internally validated? | Was it externally validated? | Does it show good discrimination? | Does it show good calibration? | Could it improve decisions at clinically meaningful thresholds? | Is there risk of bias? | Is it applicable to the intended setting? | . These questions show why machine learning in biostatistics is best viewed as clinical prediction modelling plus responsible statistical learning, not as algorithm competition.

48.title: 5. What is inference?
48.content: Inference is concerned with learning about relationships between variables. In medical statistics, inference often asks whether an exposure, treatment, biomarker, or patient characteristic is associated with an outcome. The aim may be explanatory, etiological, prognostic, or causal. Examples of inference questions include: . | Is smoking associated with lung cancer? | Is maternal smoking during pregnancy associated with offspring DNA methylation? | Is tumour stage independently associated with survival? | Is higher BMI associated with diabetes after adjustment for age and sex? | Does a treatment reduce mortality compared with standard care? | Is a biomarker associated with disease progression? | . In these questions, we usually care about effect estimates, confidence intervals, uncertainty, confounding, model assumptions, and interpretation. If the question is causal, we also care about study design, exchangeability, temporality, selection bias, measurement error, and whether the adjustment set is appropriate. For example, suppose we fit a logistic regression model to estimate the association between smoking and lung cancer. The coefficient for smoking may be transformed into an odds ratio. We might interpret this odds ratio as the adjusted association between smoking and lung cancer, conditional on the covariates in the model. The quality of this analysis depends on whether confounders were appropriately measured and adjusted for, whether the model form is suitable, and whether the study design supports the intended interpretation. In inference, the model is often a tool for estimating a parameter or relationship. Prediction performance may still matter, but it is not the primary target.

49.title: 6. What is prediction?
49.content: Prediction is concerned with estimating an outcome for new individuals. In prediction, the main question is not “what is the effect of this exposure?” but “how accurately and reliably can we estimate this person’s outcome or risk?” . Examples of prediction questions include: . | What is this patient’s 10-year risk of cardiovascular disease? | What is the probability that this patient will be readmitted within 30 days? | What is this cancer patient’s predicted 5-year survival probability? | Which ICU patients are at high risk of death during admission? | Can baseline biomarkers predict response to therapy? | Can methylation profiles classify disease status? | . In prediction, the focus shifts to performance in new data. We care about discrimination, calibration, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, clinical usefulness, and validation. A variable may be useful for prediction even if it is not causal. For example, previous hospital visits may help predict mortality because they summarise illness burden or healthcare use, even if hospital visits themselves are not a biological cause of death. This distinction is very important. A prediction model may include variables that are good predictors but poor intervention targets. Conversely, a causal factor may not improve prediction much if other variables already capture similar information. For example, smoking may be causal for lung cancer, but in a short-term mortality prediction model among patients already diagnosed with advanced cancer, tumour stage and performance status may dominate prediction.

50.title: 7. Prediction, association, and causation are not the same
50.content: Medical students and early researchers often use phrases such as “risk factor,” “predictor,” “associated factor,” and “cause” as if they mean the same thing. They do not. A predictor is a variable that helps forecast an outcome. A risk factor is often used more loosely, sometimes meaning a predictor and sometimes implying a causal factor. An associated factor is statistically related to the outcome. A cause is a factor whose intervention would change the outcome under an appropriate causal framework. Consider the variable “use of oxygen therapy” in hospital data. It may strongly predict mortality. Patients receiving oxygen are often sicker, so oxygen use may be associated with death. But that does not mean oxygen therapy causes death. It may be a marker of severity. If a prediction model includes oxygen use to estimate mortality risk at a given time point, that may be reasonable if oxygen use is available then. But it would be wrong to interpret the coefficient as evidence that oxygen therapy harms patients without considering confounding by indication. Similarly, in an EWAS, a CpG site may be associated with an exposure or disease outcome. That does not automatically mean the methylation difference causes the disease. It may be a consequence of disease, a marker of cell composition, a technical artefact, or a correlate of another exposure. Prediction answers “who is likely to have the outcome?” Causal inference answers “what would happen if we intervened?” These are different scientific questions.

51.title: 8. Side-by-side comparison of prediction and inference
51.content: | Feature | Inference | Prediction | . | Main aim | Estimate or understand a relationship | Predict outcome for new individuals | . | Main question | Is X associated with Y? What is the effect of X? | How well can we predict Y from X? | . | Typical output | Coefficients, odds ratios, hazard ratios, confidence intervals | Predicted risks, risk scores, classes, survival probabilities | . | Main evaluation | Bias, uncertainty, assumptions, confounding, precision | Discrimination, calibration, validation, clinical utility | . | Variable choice | Often guided by scientific question and confounding structure | Often guided by predictive information and availability at prediction time | . | Interpretation | Parameters may have scientific or causal meaning if assumptions hold | Predictors do not necessarily have causal meaning | . | Main danger | Confounding, selection bias, model misspecification | Overfitting, leakage, poor calibration, poor generalisation | . This table is useful because it prevents a common mistake: interpreting a prediction model as if it were a causal model. A random forest variable importance plot may identify variables that improve prediction, but it does not provide causal effects. Similarly, a logistic regression coefficient in a prediction model may not have the same interpretation as a carefully designed etiological analysis.

52.title: 9. Example: breast cancer prognosis
52.content: Suppose we have a dataset of breast cancer patients with age, tumour size, grade, lymph node status, receptor status, treatment information, comorbidities, follow-up time, and death or recurrence outcomes. An inference question might be: . Is lymph node status independently associated with mortality after adjusting for age, tumour size, grade, receptor status, and treatment? . This question focuses on an association. We may use a Cox proportional hazards model and report a hazard ratio with a confidence interval. We would discuss adjustment, model assumptions, proportional hazards, missing data, and possible confounding. A prediction question might be: . Can we predict a newly diagnosed patient’s 5-year survival probability using baseline clinical and tumour characteristics? . This question focuses on predicting individual risk. We may compare Cox regression, penalised Cox regression, random survival forests, or survival boosting. We would evaluate discrimination using the C-index or time-dependent AUC, calibration at 5 years, Brier score, and performance in internal or external validation. Both analyses may use similar variables, but they are not the same analysis. In the inference analysis, the hazard ratio for lymph node status is central. In the prediction analysis, the overall risk estimate for the patient is central.

53.title: 10. Example: EWAS and DNA methylation
53.content: In an epigenome-wide association study, the distinction between prediction and inference becomes especially important. An inference-style EWAS question might be: . Which DNA methylation sites are associated with maternal smoking during pregnancy? . Here, the focus is on identifying CpG sites associated with an exposure. The analysis will likely involve regression models at many CpG sites, covariate adjustment, multiple testing correction, and careful interpretation of biological plausibility. The result may be a list of CpG sites with effect estimates and p-values. A prediction question might be: . Can DNA methylation profiles predict whether an individual was exposed to maternal smoking during pregnancy? . Here, the focus is not on interpreting each CpG site as an exposure-associated locus. The focus is on classification performance in new individuals. The analysis may involve penalised regression, elastic net, random forests, or other machine learning methods. Evaluation should use nested cross-validation or external validation because the number of CpG sites is typically much larger than the number of samples. This distinction matters because feature selection in high-dimensional data can easily create overfitting or leakage if performed before validation. A list of “important” CpGs from a predictive model should not automatically be interpreted as causal or mechanistic.

54.title: 11. Training error and test error
54.content: A prediction model should not be judged only by how well it performs on the data used to train it. Training performance is often optimistic because the model has already seen the data. The real question is whether the model performs well for new patients. Training error is the error measured in the same dataset used to fit the model. Test error is the error measured in data that were not used for fitting. James et al. emphasise that statistical learning is often concerned with estimating performance on unseen test data rather than only describing the training data (James et al., 2023). Consider this example: . Training AUC = 0.95 Internal validation AUC = 0.78 External validation AUC = 0.62 . The training AUC looks excellent. However, the external validation AUC suggests that the model performs only modestly in a new setting. This difference may reflect overfitting, differences between populations, changes in measurement practices, leakage during development, or a model that learned dataset-specific patterns rather than generalisable clinical signal. In medical settings, test performance and external validation are particularly important because models are often intended to support decisions for future patients, not to describe past data.

55.title: 12. Overfitting and why it matters in medicine
55.content: Overfitting occurs when a model learns noise or accidental patterns in the training data rather than generalisable signal. Overfitted models often look impressive during development but fail when applied to new data. This problem is common in medical machine learning because many medical datasets have small sample sizes, rare outcomes, missing data, repeated measurements, many candidate predictors, or high-dimensional biomarkers. Omics datasets are especially vulnerable because the number of predictors may be far larger than the number of observations. For example, if we have 200 patients and 450,000 methylation sites, some CpG sites will appear associated with the outcome purely by chance. If we select those sites using the full dataset and then evaluate the model using cross-validation, performance may be inflated because the test folds have indirectly influenced feature selection. Overfitting is not just a technical inconvenience. In medicine, it can lead to false optimism, wasted validation studies, inappropriate clinical claims, and models that fail when used in real patients.

56.title: 13. Bias-variance trade-off
56.content: The bias-variance trade-off is a useful way to think about model complexity. A very simple model may have high bias because it cannot capture the true relationship between predictors and outcome. For example, a simple linear model may miss a strong nonlinear relationship between age and risk. A very complex model may have high variance because it adapts too closely to the training data and changes substantially if trained on a different sample. A useful way to think about prediction error is: . Prediction error = bias + variance + irreducible error . This is a simplification, but it is conceptually helpful. Bias reflects systematic error from an overly simple model. Variance reflects instability from an overly flexible model. Irreducible error reflects variation that cannot be predicted from the available data. In biostatistics, this trade-off has practical consequences. A complex model such as gradient boosting may outperform logistic regression in some settings, but it may also require larger data, careful tuning, calibration assessment, and external validation. A simpler model may be preferable if it performs similarly and is easier to explain, validate, and implement.

57.title: 14. Discrimination
57.content: Discrimination measures whether the model can separate individuals who experience the outcome from those who do not. In binary classification, discrimination is often assessed using the ROC curve and area under the ROC curve (AUC). In survival analysis, discrimination is often assessed using the C-index or time-dependent AUC. If a mortality model gives higher predicted risks to patients who die than to patients who survive, it has good discrimination. However, discrimination is about ranking, not necessarily about accurate absolute probabilities. For example, a model may correctly rank patients from low to high risk but systematically overestimate the actual risk. It may assign 70% risk to a group whose observed risk is only 30%. Such a model may have reasonable discrimination but poor calibration. Discrimination is important, but it is not enough for clinical prediction.

58.title: 15. Calibration
58.content: Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities agree with observed risks. If a model predicts that 100 patients each have about 20% risk, then approximately 20 of those patients should experience the outcome if the model is well calibrated. Calibration is particularly important in clinical decision-making because decisions are often based on absolute risk thresholds. For example, a clinician may recommend additional monitoring if predicted risk exceeds 20%, or preventive treatment if predicted cardiovascular risk exceeds a guideline threshold. If the model systematically overestimates or underestimates risk, it can lead to overtreatment, undertreatment, patient anxiety, false reassurance, or inefficient use of healthcare resources. Steyerberg places strong emphasis on evaluating both discrimination and calibration when assessing clinical prediction models (Steyerberg, 2019). TRIPOD+AI also expects transparent reporting of model performance, including relevant measures of discrimination and calibration when applicable (Collins et al., 2024).

59.title: 16. Accuracy is not enough
59.content: Accuracy is often the first metric beginners learn, but it can be misleading in medical datasets. Suppose disease prevalence is 5%. A model that predicts “no disease” for everyone will be 95% accurate, but it will identify none of the diseased patients. Such a model is useless for screening. The relevant metric depends on the clinical task. In screening, sensitivity may be prioritised because missing disease is harmful. In confirmatory testing, specificity may be prioritised because false positives may lead to unnecessary invasive procedures. In risk prediction, calibration may be essential because absolute risk estimates guide decisions. In treatment decision-making, decision curve analysis may help assess whether a model offers net benefit at clinically meaningful thresholds. Therefore, medical prediction models should be evaluated using a combination of metrics rather than a single headline number.

60.title: 17. Clinical usefulness
60.content: A model can be statistically impressive but clinically unhelpful. Clinical usefulness depends on whether the model can improve decisions. For example, a model that predicts 30-day readmission may be useful only if the hospital has an intervention that can reduce readmission among high-risk patients. A survival prediction model may be useful if it supports follow-up planning, shared decision-making, or treatment stratification. A disease screening model may be useful if it detects cases earlier without producing excessive false positives. Clinical usefulness depends on the prediction time point, availability of predictors, harms of false positives and false negatives, patient preferences, workflow integration, and threshold probabilities. Decision curve analysis is one approach for evaluating whether a model provides net benefit across a range of clinically relevant thresholds. The key point is that a prediction model is not useful simply because it predicts. It is useful if its predictions can support better decisions.

61.title: 18. Data leakage
61.content: Data leakage occurs when information is used during model development that would not be available when making predictions in real practice. Leakage can make a model appear much better than it really is. Common examples include using variables measured after the prediction time, selecting features using the full dataset before train/test splitting, standardising predictors using the full dataset, imputing missing values using the test data, tuning hyperparameters on the test set, or allowing repeated records from the same patient to appear in both training and test sets. Consider ICU mortality prediction at admission. Appropriate predictors might include age, admission diagnosis, baseline vital signs, and admission laboratory values. Inappropriate predictors would include length of ICU stay, discharge destination, final diagnosis after discharge, or lab values measured shortly before death. These variables may be strongly associated with mortality, but they are not available at admission. Including them would create leakage. Leakage is one of the most serious threats to medical machine learning because it can produce models that look excellent in publications but fail in real clinical use.

62.title: 19. Reporting and risk of bias
62.content: Transparent reporting is essential because readers need to understand how a model was developed, validated, and intended to be used. A prediction model report should describe the data source, study design, target population, inclusion and exclusion criteria, prediction time point, outcome definition, predictor definitions, missing data handling, sample size, model development process, hyperparameter tuning, validation, performance measures, calibration, model specification, and limitations. TRIPOD+AI provides updated reporting guidance for prediction model studies using regression or machine learning methods (Collins et al., 2024). PROBAST+AI provides a structured approach to assessing quality, risk of bias, and applicability of prediction models using regression or artificial intelligence methods (Moons et al., 2025). Risk of bias can arise from unrepresentative participants, poor outcome definitions, predictors unavailable at prediction time, inappropriate missing data handling, small sample size, overfitting, poor validation, selective reporting, or lack of calibration assessment. Applicability asks whether the model is relevant to the intended population and setting.

63.title: 20. Practical framework for medical machine learning
63.content: A responsible medical machine learning workflow should begin with the clinical question, not the algorithm. A useful workflow is: . | Define the clinical prediction question. | Define the target population. | Define the prediction time point. | Define the outcome and outcome window. | Define candidate predictors available at prediction time. | Consider missing data and measurement quality. | Split data or define a resampling strategy. | Perform preprocessing inside the resampling process. | Fit a baseline model before complex models. | Tune models using appropriate validation. | Evaluate discrimination. | Evaluate calibration. | Consider clinical usefulness. | Assess internal validation. | Seek external validation where possible. | Report using TRIPOD+AI principles. | Assess risk of bias and applicability using PROBAST+AI principles. | . This framework keeps the course focused on biostatistics. It ensures that machine learning is not treated as a black-box competition, but as a disciplined modelling process for medical data.

64.title: 21. Summary
64.content: Machine learning in biostatistics is best understood as the development, validation, interpretation, and reporting of prediction models for medical and biomedical data. The most important idea in this note is the distinction between prediction and inference. Inference asks what relationship exists between variables. Prediction asks how accurately outcomes can be estimated for new individuals. A model designed for inference should not automatically be judged by prediction metrics alone, and a model designed for prediction should not automatically be interpreted causally. A good medical machine learning model should be well validated, well calibrated, clinically useful, free from major leakage, appropriate for the target population, transparently reported, and assessed for risk of bias and applicability.

65.title: Revision questions
65.content: . | Why is it important to distinguish prediction from inference before choosing a model? | Give one example of a prediction question and one example of an inference question using the same dataset. | Why can a variable be useful for prediction without being causal? | Why might a causal factor fail to improve prediction? | What is the difference between discrimination and calibration? | Why is accuracy often misleading in medical datasets? | What is data leakage, and why is it dangerous? | Why is external validation important? | Why should interpretability not be confused with causality? | What information should be reported in a clinical prediction model study? | .

66.title: Short exercises
66.content: Exercise 1: Prediction or inference? . Classify each question as prediction, inference, or causal inference. | Does maternal smoking during pregnancy affect offspring DNA methylation? | Can baseline methylation profiles predict asthma status? | Does a new drug reduce 1-year mortality? | Which patients are likely to be readmitted within 30 days? | Is tumour grade associated with survival after adjustment for age and stage? | . Exercise 2: Same dataset, different goals . Imagine a dataset of cancer patients with tumour stage, treatment, biomarkers, survival time, and recurrence status. Write one prediction question and one inference question that could be asked using this dataset. Exercise 3: Calibration interpretation . A model predicts 30% mortality risk for a group of 100 patients, but 50 patients die. | Is the model well calibrated for this group? | Is it overestimating or underestimating risk? | Why could this be clinically harmful? | .

67.title: Key terms
67.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Statistical learning | Methods for modelling and understanding data | . | Prediction | Estimating outcomes for new individuals | . | Inference | Estimating and interpreting relationships between variables | . | Causal inference | Estimating what would happen under an intervention | . | Discrimination | Ability to separate those with and without the outcome | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted and observed risks | . | Data leakage | Use of information not legitimately available during model development or prediction | . | Clinical utility | Whether model predictions improve decision-making | . | TRIPOD+AI | Reporting guidance for prediction models using regression or machine learning | . | PROBAST+AI | Tool for assessing risk of bias and applicability in prediction model studies | .

69.title: Key takeaway
69.content: Prediction and inference are both central to biostatistics, but they answer different questions. Before fitting any model, define whether the aim is to estimate a relationship, estimate a causal effect, or predict outcomes for new individuals. In medical machine learning, responsible prediction modelling requires validation, calibration, clinical interpretation, transparent reporting, and careful avoidance of data leakage.
