78.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
78.title: Case Study 2: Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction

79.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
79.title: Case study overview
79.content: This case study extends the first diabetes risk prediction case study. In Case Study 1, we used ordinary logistic regression to predict diabetes status from routinely measured clinical characteristics. In this case study, we ask a new question: . Can regularised regression methods improve or stabilise diabetes risk prediction compared with ordinary logistic regression? . The aim is not simply to find the model with the highest AUC. The aim is to understand how regularisation changes the modelling process, how ridge, lasso, and elastic net behave differently, and how feature selection should be interpreted in a medical prediction setting. In this case study, we focus on: . | comparing ordinary logistic regression with regularised regression; | understanding coefficient shrinkage; | fitting ridge regression; | fitting lasso regression; | fitting elastic net; | comparing model discrimination; | comparing Brier score; | examining selected predictors; | interpreting selected features cautiously; | understanding why selected predictors are not automatically causal; | recognising the importance of validation and calibration. | .

80.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
80.title: Clinical question
80.content: The clinical question is: . Can we predict whether a patient has diabetes using routinely measured clinical characteristics, and do regularised models provide a useful alternative to ordinary logistic regression? . This remains a binary clinical prediction problem. The outcome has two categories: . Diabetes: yes/no . The model estimates the probability that a patient belongs to the diabetes-positive group based on clinical measurements. This case study is for teaching only. It should not be interpreted as a deployable diabetes diagnosis or screening model.

81.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
81.title: Why this case study matters
81.content: Ordinary logistic regression is a strong baseline model for binary clinical prediction. However, ordinary regression can become unstable when: . | the sample size is limited; | there are many candidate predictors; | predictors are correlated; | some predictors are weak; | there is noise in the data; | the model starts fitting dataset-specific patterns. | . Regularised regression methods try to reduce this problem by shrinking coefficients. This matters in medical prediction because a model should not only fit the development dataset. It should generalise to new patients. This case study introduces three regularised approaches: . | Method | Main idea | . | Ridge regression | Shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors | . | Lasso regression | Shrinks some coefficients exactly to zero | . | Elastic net | Combines ridge and lasso behaviour | . A very important lesson from this case study is that regularisation does not automatically improve performance. Sometimes ordinary logistic regression performs just as well, especially when the number of predictors is modest.

82.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
82.title: Dataset
82.content: This case study uses the same Pima Indians Diabetes dataset used in Case Study 1. The dataset is commonly used for teaching binary classification. It contains clinical measurements such as pregnancies, glucose, blood pressure, skin thickness, insulin, BMI, diabetes pedigree function, age, and diabetes status. Using the same dataset helps learners focus on the difference between modelling approaches rather than learning a completely new dataset. However, this dataset should still be treated as an educational dataset, not as a modern clinical dataset ready for deployment.

83.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
83.title: Outcome
83.content: The outcome is: . Diabetes status . The positive class is: . Diabetes = positive . The negative class is: . Diabetes = negative . Defining the positive class clearly is essential because sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, ROC AUC, and threshold-based interpretation all depend on which class is treated as the event.

84.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
84.title: Predictors
84.content: The candidate predictors are the same as in Case Study 1: . | Predictor | Clinical interpretation | . | Pregnancies | Number of pregnancies | . | Glucose | Plasma glucose concentration | . | Blood pressure | Diastolic blood pressure | . | Triceps | Skinfold thickness | . | Insulin | Serum insulin | . | BMI / mass | Body mass index | . | Pedigree | Diabetes pedigree function | . | Age | Age in years | . Some predictors, such as glucose, BMI, insulin, and age, have clear clinical relevance. Others may be weaker, more variable, or harder to interpret. Regularisation helps us examine whether the model can be stabilised when several predictors are included together.

85.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
85.title: Prediction time point
85.content: For this teaching example, we again define the prediction time point as: . At the time of clinical assessment . This means the model should only use information available at that moment. Predictors measured after the assessment should not be included. This matters because using future information would create data leakage and make model performance look better than it really is.

86.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
86.title: Why regularisation is useful here
86.content: Regularisation is useful because ordinary logistic regression may produce coefficients that are too large or unstable when the dataset is limited or predictors overlap. For example, clinical variables such as BMI, insulin, glucose, and age may contain related information about diabetes risk. Ordinary logistic regression estimates coefficients to fit the training data as closely as possible. Regularised regression adds a penalty. The penalty discourages overly large coefficients. The goal is: . Fit the data well, but avoid an overly complex or unstable model.

87.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
87.title: Models compared
87.content: This case study compares four models. 1. Ordinary logistic regression . Ordinary logistic regression is the baseline model. It estimates: . P(diabetes positive | clinical predictors) . It is interpretable and familiar in medical statistics. However, it does not apply coefficient shrinkage. 2. Ridge regression . Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. It usually keeps all predictors in the model. Ridge is useful when many predictors may each contain some information, especially when predictors are correlated. In this case study, ridge helps us ask: . Can we stabilise the model without removing predictors? . In the script, ridge regression is fitted using: . alpha = 0 . 3. Lasso regression . Lasso regression can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can perform feature selection. In this case study, lasso helps us ask: . Which predictors are retained by a sparse regularised model? . In the script, lasso regression is fitted using: . alpha = 1 . However, selected predictors should not be interpreted as automatically causal or clinically proven. They are predictors selected by the model in this dataset. 4. Elastic net . Elastic net combines ridge and lasso behaviour. It can shrink coefficients and perform some feature selection. It is especially useful when predictors are correlated. In the script, elastic net is fitted using: . alpha = 0.5 . Elastic net helps us ask: . Can we balance coefficient shrinkage and feature selection? .

88.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
88.title: Analysis workflow
88.content: The case study follows this workflow: . | Load the dataset. | Define the outcome and predictors. | Split the data into training and test sets. | Fit ordinary logistic regression. | Fit ridge regression. | Fit lasso regression. | Fit elastic net. | Predict probabilities in the test set. | Compare test AUC. | Compare Brier score. | Examine coefficient shrinkage. | Examine selected predictors from lasso and elastic net. | Compare calibration. | Interpret the models clinically. | Discuss limitations. | .

89.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
89.title: Step 1: Train/test split
89.content: The data are split into: . Training set: used to fit and tune the models Test set: used to evaluate model performance . In this script, the split is: . 80% training 20% testing . The test set should not be used repeatedly to tune models. This matters especially for regularised models because lambda is a tuning parameter. If the test set is used repeatedly to choose tuning settings, the test performance may become optimistic.

90.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
90.title: Step 2: Ordinary logistic regression
90.content: The ordinary logistic regression model is fitted first. This gives us a baseline. Conceptually, the model is: . logit(P(diabetes positive)) = β0 + β1(pregnancies) + β2(glucose) + β3(blood pressure) + β4(triceps) + β5(insulin) + β6(BMI) + β7(pedigree) + β8(age) . The model produces predicted probabilities between 0 and 1. These probabilities can be evaluated using discrimination, calibration, Brier score, and threshold-based metrics.

91.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
91.title: Step 3: Ridge regression
91.content: Ridge regression uses the same predictors as ordinary logistic regression. However, it shrinks coefficients towards zero. The important idea is: . Ridge does not usually remove predictors. Instead, it reduces the size of coefficients. This may help reduce overfitting and improve stability. In this case study, the selected ridge tuning value was: . lambda.min = 0.02263119 .

92.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
92.title: Step 4: Lasso regression
92.content: Lasso regression also applies shrinkage. However, lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means some predictors may be removed from the model. In this case study, the selected lasso tuning value was: . lambda.min = 0.003134162 . The lasso model retained the following non-zero terms: . | Term | Coefficient | . | Intercept | -7.92 | . | Pregnant | 0.112 | . | Glucose | 0.0341 | . | Pressure | -0.0113 | . | Insulin | -0.000693 | . | Mass | 0.0801 | . | Pedigree | 0.701 | . | Age | 0.0142 | . The predictor triceps was not retained by lasso in this fitted model. This does not prove that triceps is clinically unimportant. It only means that, in this specific training split and modelling workflow, lasso shrank its coefficient to zero.

93.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
93.title: Step 5: Elastic net
93.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso behaviour. In this case study, the selected elastic net tuning value was: . lambda.min = 0.01202208 . The elastic net model retained the following non-zero terms: . | Term | Coefficient | . | Intercept | -7.39 | . | Pregnant | 0.101 | . | Glucose | 0.0312 | . | Pressure | -0.00874 | . | Insulin | -0.000300 | . | Mass | 0.0718 | . | Pedigree | 0.605 | . | Age | 0.0136 | . Again, the predictor triceps was not retained. The selected predictors are very similar to the lasso model, but the coefficients are generally slightly more shrunk.

94.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
94.title: Step 6: Model performance comparison
94.content: The models were compared using test-set AUC and Brier score. The results were: . | Model | Test AUC | Brier score | . | Ordinary logistic regression | 0.851 | 0.149 | . | Ridge regression | 0.850 | 0.151 | . | Lasso regression | 0.850 | 0.150 | . | Elastic net | 0.847 | 0.152 | . Interpretation . The ordinary logistic regression model had the highest test AUC and the lowest Brier score in this split. However, the differences are very small. The main interpretation is not: . Ordinary logistic regression is always better. The better interpretation is: . In this modest-sized clinical predictor set, regularisation did not clearly improve test performance over ordinary logistic regression. Show clinical interpretation All four models performed similarly. This is an important teaching result. Regularisation is useful, but it is not magic. If the number of predictors is modest and the signal is reasonably strong, ordinary logistic regression may already perform well. Regularised models become especially useful when there are many predictors, correlated predictors, high-dimensional data, or a stronger risk of overfitting.

95.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
95.title: Step 7: ROC curve comparison
95.content: The ROC curves for the four models were very similar. The AUC values were: . Ordinary logistic regression: 0.851 Ridge regression: 0.850 Lasso regression: 0.850 Elastic net: 0.847 . Interpretation . The four models have almost the same discrimination. This means they are similarly able to rank diabetes-positive patients above diabetes-negative patients. The ROC plot shows that the regularised models do not meaningfully outperform ordinary logistic regression in this particular example. Show clinical interpretation A model with slightly higher AUC in one test split should not automatically be preferred. Here, the differences are too small to justify strong claims. A responsible interpretation should consider: - whether the model is simpler; - whether predicted probabilities are calibrated; - whether selected predictors are stable; - whether the model generalises externally; - whether the model is clinically useful.

96.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
96.title: Step 8: Ridge coefficient shrinkage
96.content: Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. The coefficient comparison was: . | Term | Ordinary coefficient | Ridge coefficient | . | Intercept | -8.21 | -7.12 | . | Pregnant | 0.118 | 0.0979 | . | Glucose | 0.0353 | 0.0290 | . | Pressure | -0.0131 | -0.00933 | . | Triceps | -0.000959 | -0.000679 | . | Insulin | -0.000909 | -0.000342 | . | Mass | 0.0861 | 0.0711 | . | Pedigree | 0.781 | 0.657 | . | Age | 0.0152 | 0.0160 | . Interpretation . Most ridge coefficients are smaller in magnitude than the ordinary logistic regression coefficients. For example: . Pedigree: 0.781 → 0.657 Pregnant: 0.118 → 0.0979 Glucose: 0.0353 → 0.0290 Mass: 0.0861 → 0.0711 . This is coefficient shrinkage. Ridge keeps the predictors but reduces the strength of their coefficients. Show clinical interpretation Ridge regression is useful when we want to reduce overconfident or unstable coefficients without removing predictors entirely. In this case, ridge shrinks the coefficients, but the final test performance is almost the same as ordinary logistic regression. This suggests that ridge changed the model slightly, but did not create a clear performance improvement in this specific dataset split.

97.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
97.title: Step 9: Lasso and elastic net selected features
97.content: Lasso and elastic net can shrink some coefficients to zero. In this fitted model, both lasso and elastic net retained: . pregnant glucose pressure insulin mass pedigree age . Both models removed: . triceps . Interpretation . The selected predictors are prediction features retained by the model. They should not be interpreted as confirmed causal risk factors. For example, lasso retaining glucose, mass, pedigree, and age is clinically sensible, but it does not prove that the selected set is the only important set of predictors. Similarly, removing triceps does not prove that skinfold thickness has no clinical relevance. Show clinical interpretation Feature selection is useful for creating simpler models, but it can be unstable. If we changed the train/test split, lasso and elastic net might select a slightly different set of predictors. This is why selected predictors should be interpreted cautiously, especially in biomedical datasets. The main message is: ```text Selected features are prediction features, not automatic causal discoveries. ``` .

98.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
98.title: Step 10: Calibration comparison
98.content: Regularisation does not remove the need for calibration assessment. Calibration assesses whether predicted probabilities agree with observed risks. A well-calibrated model should have points close to the diagonal line. If points are above the diagonal line, the observed risk is higher than the predicted risk. This suggests underestimation. If points are below the diagonal line, the observed risk is lower than the predicted risk. This suggests overestimation. Interpretation . The calibration plots for the four models are broadly similar. Some points are close to the diagonal line, especially at lower and higher predicted risks. However, there is visible instability in the middle risk groups. This is not surprising because the test set is small and each grouped calibration point contains only a limited number of patients. Show clinical interpretation The calibration plots show why AUC is not enough. Even if models rank patients well, predicted probabilities can still be imperfect. For example, if a group has a mean predicted risk around 0.40 but the observed risk is closer to 0.60, the model is underestimating risk for that group. In real clinical use, this would matter because decisions may depend on absolute predicted risk. Before real-world use, calibration would need to be assessed more carefully, ideally in an external validation dataset.

99.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
99.title: Step 11: Cross-validation plots
99.content: The regularised models use cross-validation to choose the penalty strength. The script produces cross-validation plots for ridge, lasso, and elastic net. Ridge cross-validation . Lasso cross-validation . Elastic net cross-validation . Interpretation . The cross-validation plots show how model fit changes across different values of lambda. The vertical dashed lines show commonly used choices of lambda, including the value that minimises cross-validation error and a more regularised value within one standard error. These plots help us see that regularisation is not a single model. It is a family of models across different penalty strengths. Show clinical interpretation Cross-validation helps choose the penalty strength using the training data. However, cross-validation inside the training data does not remove the need for final evaluation on unseen data. It also does not replace external validation. In clinical prediction, tuning and model development should be kept separate from honest model evaluation.

100.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
100.title: Step 12: What did regularisation add here?
100.content: In this case study, regularisation added three useful learning points. First, ridge showed coefficient shrinkage. It kept the same predictors but reduced most coefficient magnitudes. Second, lasso and elastic net demonstrated feature selection. They removed triceps while retaining several clinically plausible predictors. Third, the model comparison showed that regularisation did not clearly improve test performance in this particular setting. This is a valuable result. A method can be statistically useful in general but not obviously better in every dataset.

101.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
101.title: Clinical interpretation
101.content: In this diabetes risk prediction example, all four models performed similarly. The ordinary logistic regression model had: . AUC = 0.851 Brier score = 0.149 . Ridge and lasso had almost the same AUC: . Ridge AUC = 0.850 Lasso AUC = 0.850 . Elastic net was slightly lower: . Elastic net AUC = 0.847 . The differences are small. This suggests that, for this dataset and these eight predictors, ordinary logistic regression is already a strong baseline. Regularisation still teaches important ideas: . | ridge shrinks coefficients; | lasso can remove predictors; | elastic net combines shrinkage and selection; | selected features are not automatically causal; | calibration still matters; | external validation is still needed. | . A responsible interpretation should consider: . | test-set performance; | calibration; | selected predictors; | feature stability; | clinical plausibility; | predictor availability; | external validation; | intended use. | .

102.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
102.title: What could go wrong?
102.content: Several problems can affect this analysis. 1. Feature selection may be unstable . Lasso and elastic net may select different predictors if the train/test split changes. In this run, both lasso and elastic net removed triceps, but that does not prove the same thing would happen in every split. Selected predictors should not be overinterpreted. 2. Selected predictors are not automatically causal . A selected predictor helped prediction in this dataset. That does not prove that it causes diabetes. Causal interpretation requires different assumptions and study design. 3. Test-set tuning can bias performance . If we repeatedly try different models and choose the one that performs best on the test set, the test set becomes part of model development. This can make the final performance estimate too optimistic. 4. AUC is not enough . A model with high AUC may still be poorly calibrated. Clinical decisions often depend on absolute predicted risk, so calibration remains essential. 5. Regularisation is not always better . Regularisation can reduce overfitting, but it does not automatically improve performance. In this example, ordinary logistic regression performed about as well as the regularised models. This is a useful reminder that modelling choices should be guided by validation, not by assuming that a more advanced method is always better. 6. Educational data may not generalise . The Pima dataset is useful for teaching. However, it may not represent the target population where a real diabetes prediction model would be used. External validation would be essential before real-world use.

103.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
103.title: Limitations
103.content: This is an educational case study. The models should not be treated as deployable clinical tools. Before real-world use, a diabetes prediction model would require: . | careful data quality assessment; | clinically justified predictor selection; | missing data handling; | internal validation; | external validation; | calibration assessment; | threshold analysis; | decision curve analysis; | fairness and subgroup performance checks; | transparent reporting. | .

104.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
104.title: Reflection questions
104.content: . | What is the outcome in this case study? | What is the positive class? | Why might ordinary logistic regression overfit? | What does ridge regression do to coefficients? | Does ridge usually remove predictors? | What does lasso do differently from ridge? | Which predictor was removed by lasso and elastic net in this run? | What does elastic net combine? | Why are selected predictors not automatically causal? | Which model had the highest test AUC in this run? | Which model had the lowest Brier score in this run? | Why is AUC alone not enough? | Why does calibration still matter after regularisation? | Why should the test set not be used repeatedly for tuning? | What additional validation would be needed before clinical use? | . Show suggested answers 1. The outcome is diabetes status. 2. The positive class is diabetes positive. 3. Ordinary logistic regression may overfit when the dataset is small, predictors are correlated, or there are too many predictors relative to the available information. 4. Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. 5. No. Ridge usually keeps predictors in the model. 6. Lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. 7. In this run, `triceps` was removed by lasso and elastic net. 8. Elastic net combines ridge and lasso behaviour. 9. Selected predictors helped prediction in this dataset, but that does not prove causality. 10. Ordinary logistic regression had the highest test AUC in this run, although the difference was very small. 11. Ordinary logistic regression had the lowest Brier score in this run. 12. AUC measures discrimination but not calibration or clinical usefulness. 13. Calibration matters because clinical decisions often depend on absolute predicted risk. 14. Repeated test-set tuning makes the test set part of model development and can bias performance upward. 15. Internal validation, external validation, calibration assessment, threshold analysis, clinical usefulness evaluation, and subgroup checks would be needed.

105.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
105.title: Related course material
105.content: . | Lecture 10: Regularisation and Feature Selection | Note 10: Regularisation and Feature Selection | Exercise 10: Regularisation and Feature Selection . | Lecture 11: Ridge Regression | Note 11: Ridge Regression | Exercise 11: Ridge Regression . | Lecture 12: Lasso Regression | Note 12: Lasso Regression | Exercise 12: Lasso Regression . | Lecture 13: Elastic Net | Note 13: Elastic Net | Exercise 13: Elastic Net . | Lecture 14: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data | Note 14: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data | Exercise 14: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data | .

106.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
106.title: Code
106.content: The R code for this case study is: . r/C2_regularised_diabetes_prediction_case_study.R . This script compares: . ordinary logistic regression ridge regression lasso regression elastic net . After running the script, the figures should be saved in: . figures/ . Expected output files: . figures/regularised_diabetes_model_performance.png figures/regularised_diabetes_ridge_shrinkage.png figures/regularised_diabetes_selected_features.png figures/regularised_diabetes_calibration_comparison.png figures/regularised_diabetes_roc_comparison.png figures/regularised_diabetes_ridge_cv.png figures/regularised_diabetes_lasso_cv.png figures/regularised_diabetes_elastic_net_cv.png .

107.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
107.title: Key takeaway
107.content: Regularised regression is useful for stabilising medical prediction models and reducing overfitting, but it does not automatically improve performance. In this case study, ordinary logistic regression, ridge regression, lasso regression, and elastic net performed very similarly. Ridge demonstrated coefficient shrinkage, while lasso and elastic net demonstrated feature selection by removing triceps. However, selected predictors should be interpreted cautiously, and model performance should be assessed using validation, calibration, Brier score, and clinical reasoning rather than AUC alone.

108.doc: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction
108.title: Case Study 2 - Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction

109.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
109.title: Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage in Medical Machine Learning

110.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
110.title: Learning objectives
110.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why validation is central to machine learning in biostatistics. | Distinguish training performance, apparent performance, internal validation performance, and external validation performance. | Describe overfitting in medical prediction modelling and explain why it is common in clinical and biomedical datasets. | Explain the bias-variance trade-off using medical examples. | Distinguish internal validation from external validation and understand when each is useful. | Recognise common forms of data leakage in medical machine learning. | Explain why leakage can make a model appear clinically useful when it would fail in practice. | Understand why validation, calibration, reporting, and risk-of-bias assessment should be considered together in medical prediction modelling. | .

111.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
111.title: 1. Why this note matters
111.content: In the first note, we introduced the distinction between prediction and inference. That distinction is essential because it changes how we judge a model. If the aim is inference, we may care mainly about an estimated association, a confidence interval, a p-value, or a causal interpretation. If the aim is prediction, we care mainly about how well the model performs for individuals who were not used to build the model. This second note focuses on one of the most important ideas in medical machine learning: a model is only useful if it generalises to new patients. A prediction model can look impressive in the dataset where it was developed, but fail when applied to another hospital, another time period, another ethnic group, another laboratory platform, or another healthcare system. This gap between development performance and real-world performance is one of the major reasons why many medical machine learning models do not translate into clinical practice. In biostatistics, validation is not a cosmetic final step. It is part of the scientific argument that a model may be reliable. If a study reports excellent model performance but does not describe how the model was validated, whether preprocessing was done correctly, whether predictors were available at the intended prediction time, or whether calibration was checked, then the reported performance may not be trustworthy. This is why modern clinical prediction model guidance, including TRIPOD+AI and PROBAST+AI, emphasises transparent reporting, validation, risk of bias, and applicability (Collins et al., 2024; Moons et al., 2025). The practical message of this note is simple: . In medical machine learning, the goal is not to fit the best-looking model on the current dataset. The goal is to build and evaluate a model that can make reliable predictions for future patients in the intended clinical setting.

112.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
112.title: 2. From model fitting to model validation
112.content: When we fit a model, we use data to estimate a relationship between predictors and an outcome. In supervised learning, the outcome is known during training. For example, we may have a dataset of patients with predictors such as age, sex, blood pressure, laboratory values, tumour stage, or biomarkers, and an outcome such as mortality, disease status, readmission, or treatment response. A model may learn patterns from this dataset. Some of these patterns may be clinically meaningful. Others may be accidental. For example, a hospital dataset may contain coding habits that are specific to that hospital. A laboratory dataset may contain batch effects that are related to when samples were processed. A small biomedical dataset may contain chance correlations between thousands of biomarkers and the outcome. A model that is flexible enough may learn all of these patterns, even if they will not hold in future patients. This is why model fitting and model validation are different tasks. Model fitting asks: . What relationship can the model learn from the development data? . Model validation asks: . How well does the fitted model perform when applied to data not used to develop it? . In statistical learning, the central issue is often not whether a model can fit the training data, but whether it can predict well on new observations (James et al., 2023). In medical statistics, this point is even more important because clinical decisions may depend on predicted risks. A model that appears accurate but is not valid can misclassify patients, overestimate or underestimate risk, and potentially support harmful decisions.

113.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
113.title: 3. Training performance, apparent performance, and test performance
113.content: A major source of confusion in machine learning is the difference between performance estimated on the data used to fit the model and performance estimated on independent data. 3.1 Training performance . Training performance is the performance measured on the same data used to fit the model. If a logistic regression, random forest, or XGBoost model is trained on 1,000 patients and then evaluated on those same 1,000 patients, the resulting performance is training performance. Training performance is usually optimistic because the model has already seen the data. This optimism may be small for simple models in large datasets, but it can be severe for flexible models, small datasets, rare outcomes, or high-dimensional predictors. 3.2 Apparent performance . In clinical prediction modelling, performance estimated directly in the development dataset is often called apparent performance. It is the performance the model appears to have before properly correcting for optimism. Apparent performance can be misleading because it includes the benefit of all modelling choices made using the data. These choices may include variable selection, transformation choices, interaction exploration, hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, and model comparison. Even if the final model is evaluated only once, the modelling process may have indirectly used the data many times. 3.3 Test performance . Test performance is estimated using data not used to fit the model. A test set is intended to mimic future patients. If the model performs well on an untouched test set, this provides stronger evidence that the model has learned patterns that generalise beyond the training data. However, not all test sets are equally informative. A random split from the same dataset gives information about performance in similar data from the same source, but it may not tell us whether the model will work in another hospital or cohort. External validation is usually more informative for clinical generalisability.

114.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
114.title: 4. A simple example: why training performance can mislead
114.content: Imagine a team develops a model to predict 30-day mortality after hospital admission. They use 500 patients from one hospital and try many machine learning algorithms. They report the following result: . Training AUC = 0.97 . At first glance, this looks excellent. But we should immediately ask several questions: . | Was the model evaluated on the same patients used to train it? | Were predictors selected before validation? | Were missing values imputed using the whole dataset? | Were any predictors measured after admission? | Was the model tested in another dataset? | Was calibration assessed? | Was the target population clearly defined? | . If the answer is that the model was evaluated only in the training data, then the AUC of 0.97 tells us very little about how the model will perform for future patients. When the same model is tested on a later set of patients, the result might look like this: . Training AUC = 0.97 Internal test AUC = 0.76 External validation AUC = 0.64 . The training AUC suggested near-perfect discrimination, but external validation suggests only modest performance. This pattern is not rare in medical machine learning. It may reflect overfitting, leakage, differences between populations, differences in measurement, poor calibration, or a combination of these issues.

115.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
115.title: 5. Overfitting
115.content: Overfitting occurs when a model learns patterns that are too specific to the training dataset. Instead of learning general relationships that apply to future patients, the model learns noise, accidental correlations, artefacts, or dataset-specific structures. A model that overfits will often show: . High training performance Lower validation performance Poor external performance . In medical statistics, overfitting is not just a technical inconvenience. It can lead to overconfident claims about clinical usefulness. If an overfitted model is reported as highly accurate, clinicians and researchers may believe that it can support decision-making, when in reality it may fail in practice. 5.1 Why overfitting happens . Overfitting becomes more likely when: . | the sample size is small; | the number of outcome events is low; | the number of candidate predictors is large; | many models are tried and only the best result is reported; | hyperparameters are tuned without proper validation; | feature selection is performed using the full dataset; | flexible models are used without enough data; | the dataset contains batch effects, repeated observations, or hidden clusters; | outcome definitions or predictor definitions are inconsistent. | . These conditions are common in medical datasets. Many studies have limited sample sizes because collecting high-quality clinical data is difficult. Outcomes may be rare. Biomedical data may contain tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of predictors. Data may come from one hospital, one assay platform, or one time period. All of these features increase the risk that the model will learn unstable patterns.

116.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
116.title: 6. Overfitting in clinical prediction: an example
116.content: Suppose a researcher wants to predict 1-year mortality in cancer patients using baseline clinical variables and biomarkers. The dataset has: . n = 180 patients Outcome events = 35 deaths Candidate predictors = 120 variables . The researcher fits several models: logistic regression, lasso, random forest, gradient boosting, and support vector machine. They try many tuning settings and choose the model with the highest AUC. The best model has: . Training AUC = 0.99 . This result should be treated with caution. With only 35 outcome events and 120 candidate predictors, a flexible model can easily learn patterns that are specific to the dataset. If the model was not validated properly, the AUC is likely to be optimistic. If the researcher selected predictors before splitting the data, the result may be affected by leakage. If the model was tuned repeatedly on the test set, the test performance may also be optimistic. A more responsible analysis would define the prediction question clearly, restrict predictors to those available at the intended prediction time, use internal validation or resampling correctly, assess calibration, and ideally test the final model in an external cohort. This approach is aligned with the clinical prediction modelling perspective described by Steyerberg (2019) and with modern reporting and risk-of-bias guidance (Collins et al., 2024; Moons et al., 2025).

117.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
117.title: 7. Bias-variance trade-off
117.content: The bias-variance trade-off is a central concept in statistical learning. It helps explain why both overly simple and overly complex models can perform poorly. A useful way to think about expected prediction error is: . Prediction error = bias + variance + irreducible error . This is a conceptual decomposition rather than something we usually calculate directly in routine medical modelling. It is still useful because it explains why model complexity must be controlled. 7.1 Bias . Bias refers to error from overly simplistic assumptions. A high-bias model does not capture the true relationship well. For example, suppose the relationship between age and cardiovascular risk is nonlinear, but we fit a logistic regression model with age only as a linear term. The model may underfit because it cannot represent the true shape of risk across age. Similarly, if treatment response depends on an interaction between tumour subtype and biomarker expression, a model that includes only main effects may miss an important pattern. High-bias models tend to underfit. They may perform poorly both in training data and validation data. 7.2 Variance . Variance refers to error from excessive sensitivity to the training data. A high-variance model changes substantially when trained on a slightly different sample. For example, a deep decision tree may create highly specific rules based on a few patients. It may perform very well on the training dataset but poorly on new patients. A random forest or gradient boosting model can also overfit if the dataset is small, the outcome is rare, or tuning is done incorrectly. High-variance models tend to overfit. They may have excellent training performance but weak validation performance. 7.3 Irreducible error . Irreducible error is the part of the outcome that cannot be predicted from the available data. In medicine, irreducible error may arise from biological variability, unmeasured risk factors, measurement error, random events, incomplete clinical histories, or imperfect outcome definitions. Even a perfectly developed model cannot predict everything. This is important for clinical communication. A prediction model estimates risk; it does not remove uncertainty.

118.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
118.title: 8. Simple models and flexible models in medicine
118.content: A common misconception is that more complex models are always better. In medical machine learning, this is not true. More complex models may improve prediction in some settings, but they also increase the risk of overfitting, poor calibration, and weak interpretability. 8.1 Simple models . Simple models include linear regression, logistic regression, and Cox proportional hazards models. These models are familiar in medical statistics and often provide strong baselines. Their advantages include interpretability, stability, relatively transparent assumptions, and easier reporting. A logistic regression model may perform surprisingly well for tabular clinical data. If the main relationships are approximately linear on the log-odds scale and the number of predictors is moderate, logistic regression may be hard to beat. It also produces predicted probabilities, which are central to clinical prediction. 8.2 Flexible models . Flexible models include decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting, support vector machines, and neural networks. These methods can capture nonlinearities and interactions without requiring all relationships to be specified manually. This can be useful when the data are complex and the sample size is sufficiently large. However, flexible models are not automatically clinically superior. A model should be judged by validated performance, calibration, clinical usefulness, and applicability to the target setting. If a random forest gives only a tiny improvement in AUC over logistic regression but is poorly calibrated and harder to explain, it may not be preferable. A practical principle is: . Use complexity only when it gives a meaningful and validated improvement for the clinical task.

119.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
119.title: 9. Validation as a scientific requirement
119.content: Validation is the process of estimating how well a model performs outside the exact data used to develop it. In clinical prediction modelling, validation is part of the evidence that a model might be useful. A model that has not been validated should be treated as a model hypothesis rather than a reliable clinical tool. It may suggest that prediction is possible, but it does not yet show that the model will work in practice. Validation should answer several questions: . | Does the model discriminate between patients with and without the outcome? | Are the predicted probabilities well calibrated? | Does performance remain acceptable when evaluated in new data? | Is performance consistent across clinically important subgroups? | Is the model useful at clinically relevant decision thresholds? | Does the model apply to the intended target population? | . TRIPOD+AI emphasises transparent reporting of development and validation methods so that readers can judge the reliability and applicability of prediction model studies (Collins et al., 2024). PROBAST+AI further supports structured assessment of risk of bias and applicability (Moons et al., 2025).

120.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
120.title: 10. Internal validation
120.content: Internal validation evaluates model performance using data from the same source as the development data. It estimates how much optimism may exist in the apparent performance and gives a better sense of how the model may perform in similar patients. Common approaches include: . | train/test split; | k-fold cross-validation; | repeated cross-validation; | bootstrap validation. | . Internal validation is essential during model development. It helps avoid being misled by training performance. However, internal validation is not the same as external validation. It does not prove that the model will work in a different hospital, country, time period, or healthcare system.

121.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
121.title: 11. Train/test split
121.content: A train/test split divides the available dataset into two parts. The training set is used to develop the model. The test set is held back and used only for evaluation. A common split is: . Training set: 80% Test set: 20% . For binary classification, the split should often be stratified so that the outcome prevalence is similar in both sets. For example, if 10% of patients have the outcome, a stratified split tries to preserve that 10% proportion in both training and test data. A train/test split is easy to understand, but it has limitations. If the dataset is small, the test set may contain too few outcome events to estimate performance precisely. The result can depend on the random split. For small or moderate datasets, cross-validation or bootstrap validation may provide more efficient internal validation.

122.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
122.title: 12. Cross-validation
122.content: Cross-validation divides the data into several folds. In 5-fold cross-validation, the data are split into five parts. The model is trained on four parts and tested on the remaining part. This is repeated five times so that each part is used once as a validation fold. The performance estimates are then averaged. Cross-validation is useful because it allows each observation to be used for both training and validation at different stages. It is especially helpful when data are limited. However, cross-validation must be done carefully. All steps that learn from data must occur inside the cross-validation loop. This includes imputation, scaling, feature selection, PCA, model tuning, and threshold selection. If these steps are performed before cross-validation using the full dataset, information from the validation folds leaks into training. This is one of the most common mistakes in biomedical machine learning.

123.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
123.title: 13. Bootstrap validation
123.content: Bootstrap validation is often used in clinical prediction modelling. It involves repeatedly sampling from the development dataset with replacement, fitting the model in the bootstrap sample, and evaluating optimism by comparing performance in the bootstrap sample and original data. Bootstrap methods can be particularly useful when the dataset is not large enough to set aside a separate test set. Steyerberg (2019) discusses bootstrap validation as a practical method for estimating and correcting optimism in prediction model performance. The key idea is that bootstrap validation tries to estimate how much the model’s apparent performance is inflated by overfitting. This optimism-corrected estimate is usually more realistic than apparent performance.

124.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
124.title: 14. External validation
124.content: External validation evaluates a model using independent data from a different source. This is one of the strongest tests of a prediction model. Examples include: . | developing a model in Hospital A and testing it in Hospital B; | developing a model in one country and testing it in another; | developing a model using data from 2015–2020 and testing it in 2021–2024; | developing a model in a clinical trial and testing it in real-world registry data; | developing a model in one cohort and testing it in another cohort. | . External validation is important because medical data are highly context-dependent. Disease prevalence, treatment pathways, measurement systems, coding practices, population demographics, and healthcare access may all differ across settings. A model that performs well in one dataset may perform poorly elsewhere. A model without external validation may still be valuable as a development-stage model, but it should not be presented as broadly generalisable.

125.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
125.title: 15. Generalisation and transportability
125.content: Generalisation refers to whether a model performs well in new individuals from the same broad population. Transportability refers to whether a model performs well in a different but related population or setting. For example, a model developed using data from one NHS hospital may generalise reasonably to similar NHS hospitals, but may not transport well to another country with different patient demographics, clinical pathways, laboratory systems, and outcome coding. A model developed in adults may not transport to children. A model developed in trial participants may not transport to routine clinical populations, because trial participants are often more selected. This is why the target population must be defined clearly. A prediction model is not simply “good” or “bad” in isolation. It is good or bad for a specific prediction task, at a specific prediction time, in a specific population, using specific predictors.

126.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
126.title: 16. Calibration and validation
126.content: Validation should not focus only on discrimination. A model may have a good AUC but poorly estimated probabilities. In clinical medicine, predicted probabilities often matter because decisions are based on absolute risk. Calibration measures agreement between predicted risks and observed risks. For example, among patients predicted to have a 20% risk, approximately 20% should experience the outcome if the model is well calibrated in that risk range. Poor calibration can be harmful. If a model overestimates risk, patients may receive unnecessary monitoring, anxiety, or treatment. If it underestimates risk, high-risk patients may be falsely reassured or denied preventive care. Steyerberg (2019) emphasises that prediction models should be assessed using both discrimination and calibration. When validating a model, we should therefore ask: . | Does the model rank patients correctly? | Are predicted probabilities accurate? | Does calibration vary across subgroups? | Does calibration remain acceptable in external data? | Would recalibration be needed before clinical use? | .

127.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
127.title: 17. Data leakage: the hidden threat
127.content: Data leakage occurs when information is used during model development that would not be available in real prediction, or when information from validation/test data is allowed to influence model training. Leakage is dangerous because it can produce unrealistically high performance. A model affected by leakage may appear excellent in a paper, but fail when used in real clinical practice. Leakage can be obvious, such as including discharge destination when predicting mortality at admission. It can also be subtle, such as selecting features using the full dataset before cross-validation. A good practical question is: . Would this information be available, in this form, at the moment the prediction is supposed to be made? . If the answer is no, the variable may cause leakage.

128.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
128.title: 18. Leakage from future information
128.content: Future information leakage occurs when predictors include information measured after the prediction time. Suppose the clinical question is: . Can we predict ICU mortality at the time of ICU admission? . Appropriate predictors might include age, sex, admission diagnosis, blood pressure at admission, oxygen saturation at admission, baseline laboratory values, and comorbidity history. Leakage-risk predictors would include ICU length of stay, discharge destination, final diagnosis after discharge, treatment response after several days, laboratory values measured shortly before death, and interventions performed after deterioration. These variables may be strongly associated with mortality, but they are not available at ICU admission. Including them would make the model unrealistic and inflate performance.

129.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
129.title: 19. Leakage from preprocessing
129.content: Preprocessing leakage occurs when steps such as scaling, imputation, feature selection, or PCA are performed using the full dataset before splitting or cross-validation. For example, suppose a researcher standardises all predictors using the mean and standard deviation from the full dataset, and then performs train/test splitting. The test set has already influenced the preprocessing parameters. This is leakage. The correct approach is: . | Split the data into training and test sets. | Estimate preprocessing parameters using the training set only. | Apply those parameters to the test set. | . In cross-validation, the same principle applies within each fold. Preprocessing must be learned from the training fold and applied to the validation fold. This is why workflow tools are important. In R, tidymodels recipes and workflows help keep preprocessing inside cross-validation, reducing the risk of data leakage.

130.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
130.title: 20. Leakage from feature selection
130.content: Feature selection leakage is one of the most serious problems in omics and high-dimensional biomedical machine learning. Consider a dataset with: . n = 250 participants p = 20,000 gene expression features . An incorrect workflow would be: . | Use the full dataset to select the 100 genes most associated with disease status. | Perform cross-validation using only those 100 selected genes. | Report the cross-validated AUC. | . This workflow is biased because the validation folds contributed to feature selection. The selected features were chosen partly because of their relationship with outcomes in the validation data. Therefore, the cross-validation result is no longer an honest estimate of performance. The correct workflow is: . | Split the data into cross-validation folds. | Within each training fold, perform feature selection using only that training fold. | Fit the model using the selected features from that training fold. | Evaluate on the held-out validation fold. | Repeat for each fold. | . For a final model, feature selection should be repeated using the training data only, and performance should be evaluated on an untouched test set or external dataset.

131.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
131.title: 21. Leakage from duplicate or related observations
131.content: Medical datasets often contain repeated or related observations. A patient may have multiple admissions, multiple clinic visits, multiple images, multiple tissue samples, or repeated biomarker measurements. If rows are split randomly, the same patient may appear in both training and test sets. This creates leakage because the model may recognise patient-specific patterns rather than general disease patterns. For example, if a dataset contains multiple images per patient and images are randomly split, images from the same patient may appear in both training and test sets. The model may perform well because it has effectively seen the patient before. The correct approach is to split by patient, not by row. In clustered data, splitting may need to account for hospital, family, batch, centre, or other grouping structures.

132.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
132.title: 22. Leakage from outcome proxies
132.content: Some variables are not the outcome itself but are very close proxies for it. These variables may create unrealistic performance if they would not be available in real prediction or if they encode clinical knowledge that occurs after the prediction point. Examples include: . | palliative care referral when predicting short-term mortality; | discharge code when predicting diagnosis at admission; | treatment escalation when predicting deterioration; | “number of days in ICU” when predicting ICU mortality; | clinician risk category assigned after reviewing the full case; | follow-up imaging results when predicting baseline prognosis. | . These variables may seem legitimate because they are present in the dataset. But prediction modelling is not about using every available column. It is about using predictors that would be available and appropriate at the intended prediction time.

133.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
133.title: 23. Leakage in survival prediction
133.content: Survival prediction has additional leakage risks because time is central. Suppose we want to predict 5-year survival at cancer diagnosis. Predictors should be available at or near diagnosis. Appropriate predictors may include age at diagnosis, tumour stage, tumour grade, receptor status, baseline comorbidities, and baseline biomarkers. Potential leakage variables include recurrence during follow-up, treatment response after chemotherapy, follow-up imaging, later metastasis status, end-of-life care variables, and survival time itself. A survival model should respect the temporal ordering of information. If a variable is measured after the prediction time, it should not be used for baseline prediction.

134.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
134.title: 24. Leakage in EWAS and omics prediction
134.content: In EWAS, gene expression, methylation, proteomics, and metabolomics studies, leakage can be particularly subtle. The data are high-dimensional, preprocessing is complex, and batch effects are common. Potential leakage sources include: . | selecting CpG sites using the full dataset before validation; | performing PCA using all samples before splitting; | carrying out batch correction using outcome information; | normalising using the full dataset before cross-validation; | allowing duplicate samples from the same participant in different folds; | using technical processing variables that are confounded with outcome status; | tuning the model repeatedly using the test set. | . A careful omics machine learning workflow should define the split first, carry out preprocessing within resampling, perform feature selection inside the training folds, and reserve an untouched test set or external dataset for final evaluation. This is especially important when p >> n.

135.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
135.title: 25. Warning signs in medical machine learning papers
135.content: When reading a medical machine learning paper, be cautious if you see any of the following: . | extremely high accuracy from a small dataset; | performance reported only on training data; | no internal validation; | no external validation; | feature selection before splitting; | unclear prediction time point; | predictors measured after the outcome; | no calibration assessment; | only AUC reported; | no missing data strategy; | no model specification; | no description of hyperparameter tuning; | no code or reproducibility information; | no assessment of applicability to the target population. | . These issues do not automatically mean the study is useless, but they reduce confidence in the reported performance. PROBAST+AI is designed to help evaluate these kinds of bias and applicability concerns systematically (Moons et al., 2025).

136.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
136.title: 26. Practical checklist before fitting a medical ML model
136.content: Before fitting a model, ask: . | What is the clinical prediction question? | What is the target population? | What is the exact prediction time point? | What is the outcome and how is it measured? | Are all predictors available at prediction time? | Are there predictors that encode future information? | Are there repeated observations per patient? | Is the outcome rare? | Is the sample size adequate for the number of predictors? | Will preprocessing be performed inside resampling? | Will feature selection be performed inside resampling? | How will discrimination be assessed? | How will calibration be assessed? | Is external validation possible? | How will risk of bias and applicability be discussed? | How will the model be reported transparently? | . This checklist should become a habit. It prevents many of the most common mistakes in medical machine learning.

137.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
137.title: 27. Worked example: hospital readmission prediction
137.content: Suppose we want to predict 30-day hospital readmission at the time of discharge. The prediction time is discharge. Therefore, predictors available up to discharge may be allowed, but predictors after discharge should not be used. Appropriate predictors may include age, sex, admission diagnosis, comorbidities, length of stay, discharge medications, laboratory values before discharge, previous admissions, and discharge destination if it is genuinely known at discharge and part of the decision context. Leakage-risk predictors may include readmission scheduling information, follow-up events after discharge, post-discharge emergency visits, or variables updated after the readmission occurs. Validation should ideally assess whether the model works in later time periods or another hospital. Calibration is important because the model may be used to identify patients who need additional support. A model that overestimates readmission risk may waste resources; a model that underestimates risk may miss patients who would benefit from intervention.

138.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
138.title: 28. Worked example: baseline cancer prognosis
138.content: Suppose the aim is to predict 5-year survival for patients newly diagnosed with breast cancer. The prediction time is diagnosis or baseline assessment. Predictors should be measured at or before that time. These might include age, tumour size, grade, stage, receptor status, baseline comorbidities, and baseline biomarkers. If the dataset contains variables such as recurrence during follow-up, response to chemotherapy, later metastasis, or palliative care referral, these should not be used for baseline prediction. They occur after the prediction time and would create leakage. A useful validation strategy might include internal validation using bootstrap or cross-validation, followed by external validation in another cancer registry or cohort. Performance should include discrimination, calibration at relevant time points, and possibly decision curve analysis if the model is intended to guide treatment or follow-up intensity.

139.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
139.title: 29. Worked example: methylation-based disease classification
139.content: Suppose the aim is to classify disease status using DNA methylation data. The dataset contains 300 participants and 450,000 CpG sites. This is a high-dimensional problem. The risk of overfitting is high because there are far more predictors than observations. A naive analysis may identify CpG sites that appear strongly associated with disease purely by chance. A poor workflow would select CpG sites using the full dataset, then cross-validate a classifier. This leaks outcome information into the validation folds. A better workflow would define cross-validation folds first, perform feature filtering and model fitting within each training fold, and evaluate on the held-out fold. If a final test set is available, it should remain untouched until the end. External validation in another cohort would be even stronger. The biological interpretation should also be cautious. A feature selected by a prediction model is not automatically causal. It may be a proxy, a batch-related artefact, or unstable across cohorts.

140.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
140.title: 30. Summary
140.content: Validation, overfitting, and leakage are core concepts in machine learning for biostatistics. They determine whether reported model performance is believable. A model can look excellent in the development dataset but fail in new patients. This may happen because of overfitting, high variance, leakage, population differences, measurement differences, or poor calibration. The key principles are: . | Training performance is not enough. | Apparent performance is usually optimistic. | Internal validation is essential during development. | External validation is important for clinical generalisability. | Preprocessing and feature selection must occur inside resampling. | Predictors must be available at the intended prediction time. | Calibration must be assessed, not only discrimination. | Risk of bias and applicability should be considered from the start. | . In medical machine learning, validation is not a technical afterthought. It is part of responsible scientific practice.

141.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
141.title: Revision questions
141.content: . | What is the difference between training performance and test performance? | Why is apparent performance usually optimistic? | What is overfitting? | Why is overfitting common in medical datasets? | What is the bias-variance trade-off? | Why can flexible models overfit small clinical datasets? | What is internal validation? | What is external validation? | Why is external validation especially important in clinical prediction modelling? | What is data leakage? | Give three examples of leakage from future information. | Why is feature selection before cross-validation problematic? | Why should preprocessing be performed inside cross-validation? | Why should repeated patient observations not be split randomly by row? | Why is calibration important during validation? | Why might a model with good AUC still be clinically unsafe? | Why is omics machine learning particularly vulnerable to leakage? | What warning signs should you look for in a medical machine learning paper? | How do TRIPOD+AI and PROBAST+AI relate to validation and reporting? | What checks should be done before fitting a medical prediction model? | .

142.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
142.title: Key terms
142.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Training performance | Performance measured on the data used to fit the model | . | Apparent performance | Development-data performance before optimism correction | . | Test performance | Performance measured on data not used for model fitting | . | Overfitting | Learning noise or dataset-specific patterns rather than generalisable signal | . | Bias | Error due to overly simplistic modelling assumptions | . | Variance | Error due to excessive sensitivity to the training data | . | Internal validation | Validation using data from the same source as model development | . | External validation | Validation using independent data from another setting, time, or population | . | Generalisation | Performance in new data from a similar target population | . | Transportability | Performance in a related but different setting or population | . | Data leakage | Use of information that would not legitimately be available during model training or prediction | . | Feature-selection leakage | Selecting predictors using the full dataset before validation | . | Future information leakage | Using variables measured after the prediction time | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted risks and observed risks | . | Optimism | Inflation of performance due to model development choices and overfitting | .
