144.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
144.title: Key takeaway
144.content: A medical machine learning model is not credible simply because it performs well on the development dataset. It becomes credible only when it is developed without leakage, validated appropriately, assessed for calibration and clinical usefulness, and reported transparently enough for others to judge its risk of bias and applicability.

145.doc: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
145.title: Note 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage

146.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
146.title: Lecture 2: Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage

147.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
147.title: Opening scene
147.content: Scene: The model performance announcement Curious Learner: “My model has 99% accuracy on the training data!” . Prof Stat: “That sounds impressive. What is the test accuracy?” . Curious Learner: “Test accuracy? Around 52%.” . Captain Overfit: “Excellent work! I helped the model memorise the training data perfectly.” . Leakage Monster: “And maybe I slipped some information from the future into the dataset.” . Prof Stat: “This is exactly why validation matters.” .

148.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
148.title: Today’s big question
148.content: Why can a model look excellent during development but fail on new patients? . In medical machine learning, a model may appear strong for the wrong reasons. This often happens because of: . | overfitting; | poor validation; | data leakage; | using training performance as if it represents future performance. | . A model is not useful just because it performs well on the data used to build it. A useful model should perform well on new, unseen patients.

149.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
149.title: Intuition first
149.content: Imagine two students preparing for an exam. One student memorises the exact answers from last year’s paper. The other student understands the topic properly. If the same questions appear again, the first student looks excellent. But if new questions appear, the first student struggles. A machine learning model can behave in the same way. It may memorise the training data instead of learning patterns that generalise. That is overfitting. A model should learn useful patterns, not memorise the development dataset.

150.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
150.title: Training performance versus test performance
150.content: In prediction modelling, we usually separate model development from model evaluation. | Performance type | Meaning | . | Training performance | How well the model performs on data used to fit it | . | Test performance | How well the model performs on new unseen data | . Training performance is usually too optimistic because the model has already seen those patients. Test performance is more realistic because it evaluates the model on patients not used during model fitting.

151.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
151.title: Mini conversation: why training accuracy is not enough
151.content: Curious Learner: “If my model performs well on the training data, why is that not enough?” . Prof Stat: “Because the model has already seen those patients.” . Dr Clinic: “I need to know whether it works for my next patient.” . Captain Overfit: “But training data is my kingdom. I memorise every tiny detail.” . Prof Stat: “And that is exactly the problem.” . In medicine, the real question is not: . How well did the model fit the development data? . The real question is: . How well will the model perform on future patients? .

152.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
152.title: What is overfitting?
152.content: Overfitting happens when a model learns patterns that are too specific to the training data. These patterns may include: . | random noise; | small sample quirks; | accidental associations; | measurement artefacts; | hospital-specific patterns; | time-period-specific patterns. | . An overfitted model may look excellent during development but perform poorly when applied to new patients.

153.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
153.title: Medical example: hospital readmission prediction
153.content: Suppose we build a model to predict 30-day hospital readmission. The model uses many predictors: . | age; | diagnosis; | previous admissions; | medications; | lab results; | ward; | discharge notes; | follow-up appointments. | . If the dataset is small and the model is too flexible, it may learn patterns that are specific to one hospital. For example, it may learn that a particular ward has high readmission risk because of local workflow, not because the pattern generalises clinically. When the model is used in another hospital, performance may collapse.

154.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
154.title: What is validation?
154.content: Validation means checking whether the model performs well beyond the data used to develop it. There are different levels of validation. | Validation type | Meaning | . | Apparent performance | Performance on the same data used to fit the model | . | Internal validation | Evaluation within the same dataset using splitting, cross-validation, or bootstrap | . | External validation | Evaluation in a different dataset, hospital, time period, or population | . Apparent performance is usually too optimistic. Internal validation is better. External validation is strongest because it asks whether the model works outside the original development setting.

155.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
155.title: Internal validation
155.content: Internal validation estimates how well the model might perform on new patients from a similar population. Common methods include: . | train/test split; | cross-validation; | bootstrap validation. | . A train/test split is simple and easy to understand. Cross-validation and bootstrap validation can be more efficient, especially when the dataset is not very large. But all validation methods must be done carefully to avoid leakage.

156.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
156.title: External validation
156.content: External validation evaluates the model in a separate dataset. This could mean: . | another hospital; | another country; | another time period; | another clinical system; | another patient subgroup. | . External validation is very important in medical prediction because patient populations and clinical practice can vary widely. Clinical reality check . A model developed in one hospital may not work equally well in another hospital. Differences in patients, measurement systems, clinical workflows, and disease prevalence can all affect model performance.

157.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
157.title: Enter Leakage Monster
157.content: Leakage Monster: “I make models look better than they really are.” . Curious Learner: “How?” . Leakage Monster: “I let the model see information it would not have in real life.” . Prof Stat: “That is called data leakage. It is one of the most dangerous mistakes in medical machine learning.” . Data leakage happens when information enters the model that would not be available at the proper prediction time point. This can make performance look artificially strong.

158.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
158.title: Examples of data leakage
158.content: Data leakage can happen when: . | test data are used during feature selection; | preprocessing is done before the train/test split; | future outcomes are included as predictors; | variables measured after diagnosis are used to predict diagnosis; | repeated measurements from the same patient are split incorrectly; | information from treatment decisions after the prediction time point enters the model; | outcome-related variables are accidentally included as predictors. | .

159.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
159.title: Medical example of leakage
159.content: Suppose we want to predict whether a patient will be admitted to ICU. The prediction time point is: . At hospital admission . A bad predictor would be: . ICU discharge medication . Why? . Because ICU discharge medication is only known after ICU admission. Using this variable would leak future information into the model. The model may look excellent during evaluation, but it would be useless at the real prediction time point.

160.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
160.title: Prediction time point
160.content: The prediction time point answers: . When is the model supposed to make the prediction? . Examples: . At hospital admission At diagnosis Before treatment starts At discharge At first antenatal visit . Once the prediction time point is defined, only information available before or at that time should be used. This is one of the simplest ways to reduce leakage.

161.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
161.title: Structured R workflows
161.content: Structured R workflows help reduce mistakes. A good R workflow should: . | split data before final model evaluation; | estimate preprocessing steps only on the training data; | apply the same preprocessing to the test data; | avoid using outcome information in predictor construction; | keep feature selection inside resampling when using cross-validation; | document the prediction time point clearly. | . In later practical sessions, we will use R to build workflows that separate training and evaluation properly.

162.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
162.title: Common mistake 1
162.content: Common mistake: selecting predictors before splitting the data . Suppose you use the full dataset to select the strongest predictors. Then you split the data into training and test sets. This is a problem because the test set has already influenced the model-building process. As a result, test performance may be too optimistic.

163.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
163.title: Common mistake 2
163.content: Common mistake: preprocessing before train/test split . Suppose you standardise variables using the mean and standard deviation from the full dataset. Then you split into training and test sets. This leaks information from the test data into the training process. A safer workflow is: . | split the data; | estimate preprocessing steps using only the training data; | apply those steps to the test data. | .

164.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
164.title: Mini conversation: Captain Overfit meets Leakage Monster
164.content: Captain Overfit: “I make the model memorise random noise.” . Leakage Monster: “And I give the model information from the future.” . Curious Learner: “So both of you make performance look better than it really is?” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. That is why careful validation is essential.” . Overfitting and leakage are different problems, but both can lead to misleading performance estimates.

165.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
165.title: Clinical reality check
165.content: Clinical reality check . If a model is overfitted or built with leakage, it may fail when used on real patients. That could mean: . | missed diagnoses; | unnecessary follow-up tests; | unsafe reassurance; | poor risk communication; | unfair performance across patient groups; | wasted clinical resources. | . In medical machine learning, validation is not just a technical detail. It is part of patient safety.

166.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
166.title: Key takeaway
166.content: A prediction model should not be judged by training performance alone. A reliable medical prediction model needs: . | a clearly defined prediction time point; | proper separation of training and evaluation; | protection against data leakage; | assessment on unseen data; | careful interpretation of performance; | ideally, external validation. | .

167.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
167.title: Reflection question
167.content: You are building a model to predict 30-day hospital readmission at the time of discharge. Which variables might be unsafe to include because they are only available after discharge or during the 30-day follow-up period? . Show suggested answer Unsafe variables may include information recorded after discharge, follow-up appointment outcomes, emergency department visits during the 30-day period, readmission-related notes, post-discharge medication changes, or any variable directly created using the future readmission outcome. Safe predictors should be available at or before discharge, such as age, diagnosis, previous admissions, comorbidities, length of stay, discharge medications, and discharge lab values if available at that time.

168.doc: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
168.title: Lecture 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage

169.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
169.title: Exercise 2: Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage

170.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
170.title: Exercise 1: Training error vs test error
170.content: A model for predicting hospital mortality gives the following results: . Training AUC = 0.96 Test AUC = 0.69 . Answer: . | Which performance estimate is more relevant for future patients? | What does the large difference suggest? | Give two possible reasons for this pattern. | . Show answer The test AUC is more relevant because it estimates performance on data not used to train the model. The large drop from training to test performance suggests overfitting or possible leakage. It may also happen if the test population differs from the training population or if the training dataset was too small.

171.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
171.title: Exercise 2: Identify overfitting
171.content: Which of the following are signs of possible overfitting? . | Scenario | Possible overfitting? | . | Training accuracy 99%, test accuracy 65% |   | . | Training AUC 0.80, test AUC 0.78 |   | . | Very complex model fitted to 80 patients with 500 predictors |   | . | Model performance reported only on training data |   | . | External validation performance similar to internal validation |   | . Show answer Possible overfitting: training accuracy 99% with test accuracy 65%; very complex model with 80 patients and 500 predictors; reporting only training performance. Similar internal and external validation performance is reassuring, though still needs careful interpretation.

172.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
172.title: Exercise 3: Bias or variance?
172.content: Classify each situation as mainly high bias or high variance. | Situation | High bias or high variance? | . | A simple linear model misses a strong nonlinear effect |   | . | A very deep decision tree performs perfectly on training data but poorly on test data |   | . | A logistic regression model underfits a complex image-based problem |   | . | A model changes substantially when trained on a slightly different sample |   | . Show answer A simple linear model missing nonlinear effects is high bias. A deep tree performing well on training data but poorly on test data is high variance. Logistic regression underfitting an image-based problem is high bias. A model that changes substantially between samples is high variance.

173.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
173.title: Exercise 4: Internal or external validation?
173.content: Classify each validation approach. | Validation approach | Internal or external validation? | . | 5-fold cross-validation in the development dataset |   | . | Bootstrap validation in the same cohort |   | . | Testing the model in another hospital |   | . | Testing the model in the same hospital but later calendar years |   | . | Testing the model in another country |   | . Show answer 5-fold cross-validation and bootstrap validation are internal validation. Testing in another hospital or country is external validation. Testing in the same hospital but later years may be considered temporal external validation or temporal validation, depending on the study design.

174.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
174.title: Exercise 5: Leakage from future information
174.content: You are building a model to predict ICU mortality at admission. Classify each variable as appropriate or leakage risk. | Variable | Appropriate or leakage risk? | . | Age at ICU admission |   | . | Blood pressure at ICU admission |   | . | Oxygen saturation at ICU admission |   | . | ICU length of stay |   | . | Discharge destination |   | . | Lab values measured after deterioration |   | . | Comorbidity history before admission |   | . | Final diagnosis recorded after discharge |   | . Show answer Appropriate: age at ICU admission, blood pressure at ICU admission, oxygen saturation at ICU admission, comorbidity history before admission. Leakage risk: ICU length of stay, discharge destination, lab values measured after deterioration, final diagnosis recorded after discharge.

175.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
175.title: Exercise 6: Leakage from preprocessing
175.content: A researcher does the following: . | Uses the full dataset to standardise all predictors. | Splits the data into training and test sets. | Fits a model on the training set. | Evaluates performance on the test set. | . Question: . | What is wrong with this workflow? | What is the correct workflow? | . Show answer The researcher used information from the test set during standardisation. This causes data leakage. The correct workflow is to split the data first, estimate standardisation parameters using the training data only, and then apply those parameters to the test data.

176.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
176.title: Exercise 7: Leakage from feature selection
176.content: A researcher has 10,000 gene expression predictors and 300 patients. Workflow: . | Selects the 100 genes most associated with outcome using the full dataset. | Performs 10-fold cross-validation using only those 100 genes. | Reports high cross-validated AUC. | . Question: . | Why is this problematic? | What should have been done instead? | . Show answer This is problematic because the feature selection used outcome information from the full dataset before cross-validation. The cross-validation folds are no longer independent. Feature selection should be performed inside each training fold, and performance should be evaluated on held-out folds that were not used for feature selection.

177.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
177.title: Exercise 8: Prediction time point
177.content: For each prediction problem, state the prediction time point. | Problem | Prediction time point | . | Predict 30-day readmission after hospital discharge |   | . | Predict 5-year survival after cancer diagnosis |   | . | Predict ICU mortality at ICU admission |   | . | Predict treatment response before starting therapy |   | . Show answer 30-day readmission: at hospital discharge. 5-year survival after cancer diagnosis: at diagnosis or immediately after baseline assessment. ICU mortality: at ICU admission. Treatment response: before starting therapy.

178.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
178.title: Exercise 9: Calibration problem
178.content: A model predicts 10-year cardiovascular risk. Among patients predicted to have 30% risk, the observed event rate is 12%. Answer: . | Is the model well calibrated in this risk group? | Is the model overestimating or underestimating risk? | Why could this be clinically harmful? | . Show answer The model is not well calibrated in this group. It is overestimating risk because predicted risk is 30% but observed risk is 12%. This could lead to unnecessary treatment, anxiety, additional testing, or inappropriate risk communication.

179.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
179.title: Exercise 10: Short answer questions
179.content: Write 3–5 sentences for each question. | Why is apparent performance usually optimistic? | Why is external validation important in medical machine learning? | Why can overfitting be especially severe in omics data? | Why should preprocessing be performed inside cross-validation? | Why is it dangerous to include predictors measured after the prediction time? | .

180.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
180.title: Exercise 11: Mini case study
180.content: A team develops a model to predict 1-year mortality after cancer diagnosis. They report: . Sample size: 150 patients Predictors: 2,000 biomarkers Model: random forest Training AUC: 0.99 No test set No cross-validation No calibration plot No external validation . Answer: . | List at least five concerns. | What validation approach would you recommend? | What additional reporting would you request? | . Show answer Concerns include small sample size, many predictors, high risk of overfitting, no internal validation, no external validation, no calibration assessment, training AUC only, and unclear feature selection. At minimum, cross-validation or bootstrap validation should be used, ideally with feature selection inside resampling. External validation in an independent cohort would be preferred. Reporting should include outcome definition, predictor definitions, missing data handling, model tuning, calibration, discrimination, and limitations.

181.doc: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage
181.title: Exercise 2 - Validation, Overfitting and Data Leakage

182.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
182.title: Lecture 3: Supervised Learning for Medical Data

183.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
183.title: Opening scene
183.content: Scene: A patient dataset arrives in the lab Curious Learner: “I have a dataset with patient characteristics and disease status. Is this machine learning now?” . Prof Stat: “If each patient has an observed outcome, then yes, this is a supervised learning problem.” . Dr Clinic: “So the model learns from previous patients and then predicts outcomes for future patients?” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. But first, we must define the outcome, predictors, and prediction time point clearly.” . Leakage Monster: “Or just throw every variable into the model. Future variables are very powerful!” . Prof Stat: “And very dangerous. That is how leakage begins.” .

184.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
184.title: Today’s big question
184.content: What is supervised learning, and how do we use it correctly with medical data? . Supervised learning means learning a relationship between predictors and an observed outcome. The data usually look like this: . Patient characteristics → observed outcome . The model uses past examples to learn patterns that may help predict outcomes for future patients.

185.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
185.title: Intuition first
185.content: Think of supervised learning like clinical teaching. A trainee clinician learns from previous cases: . | symptoms; | test results; | diagnosis; | treatment history; | outcome. | . Over time, the trainee starts recognising patterns. A supervised learning model does something similar, but using data. Supervised learning uses examples with known outcomes to learn how predictors relate to the outcome.

186.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
186.title: Outcomes and predictors
186.content: Every supervised learning problem needs two main ingredients. | Term | Meaning | . | Outcome | What we want to predict | . | Predictors | Variables used to make the prediction | . For example: . | Clinical task | Outcome | Predictors | . | Diabetes prediction | Diabetes yes/no | Glucose, BMI, age | . | Hospital readmission prediction | Readmission yes/no | Previous admissions, diagnosis, length of stay | . | Cancer recurrence prediction | Recurrence yes/no | Tumour stage, treatment, biomarkers | . | Survival prediction | Time to event | Age, tumour stage, molecular markers | . The model learns from the relationship between predictors and outcome.

187.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
187.title: Mini conversation: can we use every variable?
187.content: Curious Learner: “So should I include every variable in the dataset?” . Dr Clinic: “Not necessarily. Some variables may not be available when the model is supposed to make the prediction.” . Leakage Monster: “But post-outcome variables make the model look amazing.” . Prof Stat: “That is exactly the problem. A predictor must be available at the prediction time point.” . In medical machine learning, predictor availability matters. A model is not useful if it needs information that would not be available in real practice.

188.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
188.title: Prediction time point
188.content: The prediction time point answers: . When is the model supposed to make the prediction? . Examples: . At hospital admission At diagnosis Before treatment starts At discharge At first antenatal visit . Once the prediction time point is defined, only information available at or before that time should be used. This protects the analysis from data leakage.

189.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
189.title: Medical example: diabetes prediction
189.content: Suppose we want to predict whether a patient has diabetes at clinical assessment. Possible predictors: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | insulin; | blood pressure; | number of pregnancies. | . Outcome: . Diabetes status: positive or negative . Prediction time point: . At clinical assessment . This is a supervised learning problem because we have both predictors and known diabetes status in the dataset.

190.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
190.title: Types of supervised learning problems
190.content: Supervised learning includes different types of prediction problems. Regression . Used when the outcome is continuous. Example: . Predict systolic blood pressure . Classification . Used when the outcome is categorical. Example: . Predict diabetes positive or negative . Survival prediction . Used when the outcome is time-to-event. Example: . Predict time to cancer recurrence . Medical data often involve all three types.

191.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
191.title: Binary classification
191.content: Binary classification is very common in medical prediction. Examples: . | disease yes/no; | hospital readmission yes/no; | treatment response yes/no; | recurrence yes/no; | ICU admission yes/no; | mortality yes/no. | . The model usually estimates: . P(outcome = positive | predictors) . For example: . P(diabetes = positive | glucose, BMI, age) = 0.72 . This means the model estimates a 72% probability that the patient belongs to the diabetes-positive group.

192.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
192.title: Common mistake
192.content: Common mistake: unclear outcome definition . A prediction model is only as clear as its outcome. For example, “diabetes” could mean: . | self-reported diabetes; | doctor-diagnosed diabetes; | medication use; | glucose threshold; | HbA1c threshold; | hospital record diagnosis. | . These are not always equivalent. If the outcome is unclear, the model interpretation becomes unclear.

193.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
193.title: Clinical reality check
193.content: Clinical reality check . Before fitting a supervised learning model, ask: . | What exactly is the outcome? | What is the positive class? | When is the prediction made? | Are all predictors available at that time? | What decision could the prediction support? | What are the consequences of wrong predictions? | . Prediction modelling is not just a technical exercise. It must match a real clinical question.

194.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
194.title: The supervised learning workflow
194.content: A simple supervised learning workflow is: . | Define the clinical question. | Define the outcome. | Define candidate predictors. | Define the prediction time point. | Split data into training and test sets. | Fit the model on the training data. | Evaluate performance on the test data. | Interpret performance clinically. | Check limitations. | Consider external validation. | . This workflow will be used throughout the course.

195.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
195.title: Mini conversation: model first or question first?
195.content: Curious Learner: “Should I start by choosing the model?” . Prof Stat: “No. Start with the clinical question.” . Dr Clinic: “If the clinical question is unclear, the model cannot rescue the analysis.” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. The method should follow the question, not the other way around.” .

196.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
196.title: Key takeaway
196.content: Supervised learning in medical data starts with: . | a clear outcome; | clinically sensible predictors; | a defined prediction time point; | proper model validation; | careful clinical interpretation. | . Before fitting any model, ask: . What are we predicting? For whom? At what time? Using what information? For what decision? .

197.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
197.title: Reflection question
197.content: Suppose you want to predict cancer recurrence after diagnosis. What would be: . | the outcome; | the predictors; | the prediction time point; | the clinical use of the prediction? | . Show suggested answer The outcome could be recurrence within a defined time period, such as recurrence within 5 years. Predictors could include tumour stage, grade, treatment information available at diagnosis, age, biomarkers, and baseline clinical characteristics. The prediction time point might be at diagnosis or after surgery, depending on the intended use. The prediction could support follow-up planning, risk stratification, or treatment discussion, but it would need proper validation before clinical use.

198.doc: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
198.title: Lecture 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data

199.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
199.title: Supervised Learning for Medical Data

200.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
200.title: Learning objectives
200.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Define supervised learning in the context of biostatistics. | Identify outcomes and predictors in medical datasets. | Distinguish regression, classification, and survival prediction problems. | Explain why the prediction time point matters in medical machine learning. | Understand train/test splitting and why test-set performance matters. | Explain why logistic regression is often used as a baseline prediction model. | Interpret the confusion matrix, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, ROC curve, and AUC. | Understand why classification metrics must be interpreted in the clinical context. | .

201.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
201.title: 1. Introduction
201.content: Supervised learning is one of the most important areas of machine learning for biostatistics and medical statistics. Many medical prediction problems are supervised learning problems because we usually have a dataset containing patient characteristics and known outcomes. For example, we may have data on patients’ age, sex, blood pressure, glucose level, body mass index, treatment history, tumour stage, or biomarker values. We may also know whether each patient developed diabetes, experienced cancer recurrence, died within a certain time period, responded to treatment, or was readmitted to hospital. The aim of supervised learning is to use the available predictors to learn a relationship with the outcome, and then use that learned relationship to make predictions for new patients. In simple notation, we often write this as: . Y = f(X) + error . where: . | Y is the outcome; | X represents the predictors or features; | f(X) is the function learned from the data; | error represents unexplained variation. | . This notation appears simple, but it captures the core idea behind supervised learning. We use observed examples to learn a mapping between predictors and outcomes. The model is then assessed by how well it performs on new individuals, not simply by how well it fits the training dataset. This distinction is especially important in biostatistics. In medicine, a model may appear impressive in the development dataset but fail when used in another hospital, another cohort, or another patient population. Therefore, supervised learning in medical statistics must always be connected to validation, calibration, clinical usefulness, and transparent reporting (Steyerberg, 2019; Collins et al., 2024).

202.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
202.title: 2. What makes learning “supervised”?
202.content: The term supervised learning means that the algorithm learns from examples where the correct outcome is already known. For each individual in the dataset, we observe: . Predictors + Outcome . For example: . | Patient | Age | BMI | HbA1c | Blood pressure | Diabetes status | . | 1 | 52 | 31.2 | 6.8 | 142 | Yes | . | 2 | 39 | 24.8 | 5.1 | 118 | No | . | 3 | 61 | 29.4 | 7.2 | 151 | Yes | . Here, diabetes status is the known outcome. Age, BMI, HbA1c, and blood pressure are predictors. The model uses these examples to learn patterns. Once trained, the model can be given a new patient’s predictors and return a predicted probability of diabetes. This is different from unsupervised learning, where there is no labelled outcome. In unsupervised learning, the aim may be to identify clusters, reduce dimensionality, or discover structure in the data. Examples include clustering patients into subgroups or using principal component analysis to summarise high-dimensional omics data. In medical statistics, both supervised and unsupervised learning are useful, but clinical prediction modelling is usually a supervised learning task.

203.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
203.title: 3. Supervised learning in biostatistics
203.content: Supervised learning is central to many medical and biomedical prediction problems. Examples include: . | Medical question | Outcome | Predictors | . | Can we predict diabetes within 10 years? | Diabetes yes/no | Age, BMI, glucose, HbA1c, family history | . | Can we predict 30-day readmission? | Readmission yes/no | Diagnosis, comorbidities, admission history, labs | . | Can we predict cancer recurrence? | Recurrence yes/no or time to recurrence | Tumour stage, grade, treatment, biomarkers | . | Can we predict treatment response? | Responder/non-responder | Clinical characteristics, molecular markers | . | Can we predict survival? | Time to death or relapse | Age, disease stage, biomarkers, treatment | . | Can we classify disease status from methylation data? | Case/control | CpG methylation markers | . These examples show that supervised learning is not a single method. It is a framework. The specific model depends on the outcome type, sample size, predictors, missing data, and scientific question. A logistic regression model, random forest, XGBoost model, penalised Cox model, and neural network can all be supervised learning methods. The key feature is that they are trained using known outcomes.

204.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
204.title: 4. Outcome variable
204.content: The outcome is the variable we want to predict. In medical statistics, defining the outcome carefully is one of the most important steps. A vague or poorly defined outcome can make even a technically sophisticated model clinically meaningless. Examples of outcomes include: . | Outcome | Type | . | Systolic blood pressure | Continuous | . | Diabetes status | Binary | . | Hospital readmission within 30 days | Binary | . | Cancer subtype | Multiclass | . | Treatment response | Binary or ordinal | . | Time to death | Time-to-event | . | Time to cancer recurrence | Time-to-event | . | DNA methylation level at a CpG site | Continuous | . A good outcome definition should specify: . | what event or measurement is being predicted; | how it is measured; | over what time horizon it is observed; | whether censoring is present; | whether the outcome is clinically meaningful; | whether the outcome is available consistently across patients. | . For example, “mortality” is not specific enough. A better outcome definition would be: . All-cause mortality within 1 year of hospital admission. Similarly, “readmission” should specify whether it means any readmission, emergency readmission, disease-specific readmission, and the time window such as 30 days or 90 days.

205.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
205.title: 5. Predictors
205.content: Predictors are variables used to make predictions. In medical datasets, predictors may include: . | demographic characteristics; | symptoms; | clinical measurements; | laboratory values; | imaging features; | comorbidities; | medication history; | tumour characteristics; | genetic or molecular markers; | DNA methylation sites; | gene expression values; | healthcare utilisation history. | . The most important rule is: . Predictors must be available at the intended prediction time. For example, if we want to predict ICU mortality at ICU admission, we can use variables measured at or before ICU admission. We should not use ICU length of stay, discharge destination, or lab values measured after clinical deterioration because those variables would not be available at the time the prediction is made. This rule is essential for avoiding data leakage.

206.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
206.title: 6. Prediction time point
206.content: The prediction time point is the moment at which the model is intended to make a prediction. This concept is often ignored in poor medical machine learning studies, but it is central to good biostatistical modelling. Examples: . | Prediction problem | Prediction time point | . | Predict ICU mortality | At ICU admission | . | Predict 30-day readmission | At hospital discharge | . | Predict 5-year cancer survival | At diagnosis or treatment start | . | Predict treatment response | Before treatment begins | . | Predict relapse after surgery | At surgery or shortly after surgery | . | Predict disease using methylation | At sample collection | . The prediction time point determines which predictors are legitimate. If a variable is measured after the prediction time point, it should usually not be used. For example, suppose we want to predict 30-day hospital readmission at discharge. It may be reasonable to use length of stay because it is known at discharge. But if we were predicting deterioration at admission, length of stay would not be available and would cause leakage. This is why clinical prediction models must clearly state when prediction is being made (Steyerberg, 2019; Collins et al., 2024).

207.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
207.title: 7. Regression, classification, and survival prediction
207.content: Supervised learning problems are often grouped according to the outcome type. Regression . Regression is used when the outcome is continuous. Examples: . | predicting blood pressure; | predicting cholesterol level; | predicting biomarker concentration; | predicting length of stay; | predicting methylation beta values. | . A regression model produces a numerical prediction. Example: . Predicted systolic blood pressure = 132 mmHg . Common regression methods include linear regression, ridge regression, lasso regression, regression trees, random forests, and gradient boosting. Classification . Classification is used when the outcome is categorical. Binary classification has two categories. Examples: . | disease yes/no; | death yes/no within a fixed period; | readmission yes/no; | responder/non-responder. | . Multiclass classification has more than two categories. Examples: . | cancer subtype; | disease stage; | pathogen type; | low/intermediate/high risk group. | . A classification model often produces a predicted probability for each class. Example: . Predicted probability of disease = 0.72 . The probability can then be converted into a class label using a threshold. Survival prediction . Survival prediction is used when the outcome is time-to-event and censoring is present. Examples: . | time to death; | time to relapse; | time to recurrence; | time to disease progression; | time to hospital readmission. | . Survival prediction differs from ordinary regression or classification because not every patient has complete follow-up. Some patients may be censored, meaning the event has not occurred by the end of follow-up or the patient is lost to follow-up. Methods such as Cox proportional hazards models, penalised Cox models, random survival forests, and survival boosting are used for survival prediction. Survival machine learning will be covered later in the course.

208.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
208.title: 8. Binary classification in medical prediction
208.content: Binary classification is one of the most common supervised learning tasks in medical statistics. Examples include: . Disease: yes/no Death within 1 year: yes/no Readmission within 30 days: yes/no Treatment response: yes/no Cancer recurrence within 5 years: yes/no . In binary classification, the model usually estimates a probability. Example: . Predicted probability of 30-day readmission = 0.35 . This means that, based on the model and available predictors, the patient is estimated to have a 35% probability of readmission within 30 days. A threshold can be used to convert the predicted probability into a classification. Example: . If predicted probability >= 0.50, classify as high risk. If predicted probability < 0.50, classify as low risk. However, the threshold of 0.50 is not automatically appropriate in medicine. The choice of threshold should depend on the clinical context, outcome prevalence, harms of false positives, harms of false negatives, and intended use of the model.

209.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
209.title: 9. Train/test split
209.content: To estimate how well a model performs on new patients, we usually need to evaluate it on data that were not used to train it. A simple approach is the train/test split. Training set: used to fit the model Test set: used to evaluate model performance . A common split is: . 80% training 20% testing . The training data are used for model development. The test data are held back and should only be used for final evaluation. This gives a more realistic estimate of how the model may perform on unseen patients. However, the test set must be handled carefully. If we use the test set repeatedly to choose variables, tune hyperparameters, or decide between many models, it is no longer an honest test set.

210.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
210.title: 10. Stratified splitting
210.content: In medical classification problems, outcomes are often imbalanced. For example: . Disease prevalence = 10% No disease = 90% . If we randomly split the data, it is possible that the training and test sets will have different outcome proportions, especially when the sample size is small. Stratified splitting preserves the proportion of outcome classes in both the training and test sets. For example, if 10% of patients have the outcome in the full dataset, stratified splitting aims to keep approximately 10% outcome cases in both the training and test datasets. This is useful because model training and evaluation can be unstable when too few outcome cases appear in either set.

211.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
211.title: 11. Logistic regression as a baseline model
211.content: Logistic regression is one of the most important models for binary clinical prediction. Although it is a classical statistical model, it can also be understood as a supervised learning method because it learns a relationship between predictors and a binary outcome. Logistic regression estimates: . P(Y = 1 | X) . This is the probability of the outcome given the predictors. The model uses the logit link: . logit(p) = log(p / (1 - p)) . where p is the probability of the outcome. Logistic regression is often a strong baseline model in medical prediction because it is: . | interpretable; | relatively stable; | familiar to clinicians and statisticians; | able to produce predicted probabilities; | suitable for tabular clinical data; | easy to validate and calibrate; | extendable through penalisation such as ridge, lasso, and elastic net. | . Before using more complex models such as random forests or gradient boosting, it is usually sensible to compare them with logistic regression. A complex model is not automatically better. If a simpler model performs similarly and is easier to interpret, validate, and communicate, it may be preferable in a medical setting.

212.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
212.title: 12. From probabilities to classes
212.content: Many binary classifiers produce probabilities. Example: . | Patient | Predicted probability of disease | . | A | 0.82 | . | B | 0.61 | . | C | 0.44 | . | D | 0.12 | . To create class labels, we choose a threshold. If the threshold is 0.50: . | Patient | Probability | Predicted class | . | A | 0.82 | Disease | . | B | 0.61 | Disease | . | C | 0.44 | No disease | . | D | 0.12 | No disease | . But in medicine, the threshold should be chosen carefully. For screening, we may use a lower threshold to identify more possible cases. This increases sensitivity but may also increase false positives. For treatment decisions with serious side effects, we may use a higher threshold to avoid overtreatment. This increases specificity but may miss some true cases. Therefore, classification is not only a statistical problem. It is also a clinical decision problem.

213.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
213.title: 13. Confusion matrix
213.content: A confusion matrix compares predicted classes with observed outcomes. |   | Predicted positive | Predicted negative | . | Actual positive | True positive | False negative | . | Actual negative | False positive | True negative | . Definitions: . | True positive: the model predicts positive and the outcome is positive. | False positive: the model predicts positive but the outcome is negative. | True negative: the model predicts negative and the outcome is negative. | False negative: the model predicts negative but the outcome is positive. | . In medicine, the clinical consequences of false positives and false negatives are often very different. For example, in cancer screening, a false negative may delay diagnosis. A false positive may cause anxiety and additional testing. Both matter, but they matter differently.

214.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
214.title: 14. Accuracy
214.content: Accuracy is the proportion of all patients correctly classified. Accuracy = (True positives + True negatives) / Total . Accuracy is simple, but it can be misleading. Suppose a disease has prevalence 2%. A model that predicts “no disease” for everyone will be correct for 98% of patients. Accuracy = 98% . This looks excellent, but the model detects no disease cases. It has no value as a screening tool. This is why accuracy should rarely be the only metric reported in medical classification problems.

215.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
215.title: 15. Sensitivity
215.content: Sensitivity is the proportion of actual positive cases correctly identified. Sensitivity = True positives / (True positives + False negatives) . Sensitivity answers: . Among patients who truly have the condition, how many did the model detect? . High sensitivity is important when missing a case is harmful. Examples include: . | cancer screening; | sepsis alerts; | identifying high-risk ICU patients; | infectious disease screening; | detecting adverse drug reactions. | . A model with low sensitivity may miss patients who need care.

216.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
216.title: 16. Specificity
216.content: Specificity is the proportion of actual negative cases correctly identified. Specificity = True negatives / (True negatives + False positives) . Specificity answers: . Among patients who truly do not have the condition, how many did the model correctly classify as negative? . High specificity is important when false positives have serious consequences. Examples include: . | avoiding unnecessary invasive tests; | avoiding unnecessary treatment; | preventing unnecessary anxiety; | avoiding inappropriate allocation of limited resources. | . A model with low specificity may over-identify patients as high risk.

217.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
217.title: 17. Positive predictive value
217.content: Positive predictive value, or PPV, is the proportion of predicted positives who truly have the outcome. PPV = True positives / (True positives + False positives) . PPV answers: . Among patients predicted to be positive, how many truly have the outcome? . PPV is strongly affected by prevalence. If the disease is rare, even a model with good sensitivity and specificity may have a low PPV. This is important in screening, where many positive predictions may be false positives if the condition is rare.

218.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
218.title: 18. Negative predictive value
218.content: Negative predictive value, or NPV, is the proportion of predicted negatives who truly do not have the outcome. NPV = True negatives / (True negatives + False negatives) . NPV answers: . Among patients predicted to be negative, how many truly do not have the outcome? . Like PPV, NPV depends on prevalence. When a disease is rare, NPV may be high even for a weak model. Therefore, NPV should be interpreted alongside sensitivity, specificity, prevalence, and clinical context.

219.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
219.title: 19. ROC curve and AUC
219.content: The ROC curve shows how sensitivity and specificity change across different classification thresholds. It plots: . Sensitivity vs 1 - Specificity . The area under the ROC curve is called AUC. AUC measures discrimination: the ability of the model to assign higher risk scores to patients who experience the outcome than to those who do not. Interpretation: . AUC = 0.5: no discrimination AUC = 1.0: perfect discrimination . AUC is useful, but it is not enough. A model can have a good AUC but still be poorly calibrated. It may rank patients well but give risk probabilities that are too high or too low. For clinical risk prediction, calibration must also be assessed.

220.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
220.title: 20. Clinical interpretation of metrics
220.content: No single metric is best for all medical problems. The choice of metric depends on the clinical purpose. | Clinical task | Important considerations | . | Screening | High sensitivity may be important | . | Confirmatory diagnosis | High specificity may be important | . | Risk prediction | Discrimination and calibration are both important | . | Rare disease prediction | PPV and sensitivity need careful interpretation | . | Treatment decision-making | Clinical utility and net benefit matter | . | Patient reassurance | NPV may be important, but prevalence matters | . The key point is that statistical metrics must be interpreted in relation to clinical consequences. For example, a false negative in sepsis prediction may be more harmful than a false positive. But a false positive in a model recommending toxic treatment may also be harmful. Medical prediction modelling requires both statistical evaluation and clinical judgement.

221.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
221.title: 21. Common mistakes in supervised medical learning
221.content: Common mistakes include: . | Defining the outcome vaguely. | Including predictors that are not available at prediction time. | Splitting rows instead of patients when there are repeated records. | Evaluating only training performance. | Using accuracy alone in imbalanced data. | Ignoring calibration. | Choosing thresholds without clinical justification. | Trying complex models without a simple baseline. | Performing preprocessing before train/test splitting. | Reporting results without enough detail for reproducibility. | . These mistakes are common in medical machine learning studies and can lead to overly optimistic or clinically misleading conclusions.

222.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
222.title: 22. Practical workflow for a first supervised learning analysis
222.content: A basic supervised learning workflow for a binary medical outcome should include: . | Define the clinical prediction question. | Define the prediction time point. | Define the outcome clearly. | Identify candidate predictors available at prediction time. | Explore missingness and data quality. | Split data into training and test sets. | Fit a baseline logistic regression model. | Generate predicted probabilities. | Choose or examine classification thresholds. | Evaluate confusion matrix-based metrics. | Evaluate discrimination using ROC AUC. | Assess calibration. | Interpret results clinically. | Report limitations clearly. | . This workflow will be used in the first applied R examples.

223.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
223.title: 23. Summary
223.content: Supervised learning is the foundation of many medical machine learning applications. In supervised learning, the model learns from examples where both predictors and outcomes are known. In biostatistics, this framework is commonly used for clinical prediction tasks such as disease classification, readmission prediction, treatment response prediction, cancer recurrence prediction, and survival prediction. The most important point is that supervised learning in medicine is not just about fitting a model. It requires careful definition of the clinical question, outcome, prediction time point, and candidate predictors. For binary classification, logistic regression is often a useful baseline model. Model performance should be interpreted using clinically meaningful metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, ROC AUC, and calibration. The goal is not simply to maximise accuracy. The goal is to develop models that are valid, interpretable, clinically useful, and generalisable to new patients.

224.doc: Note 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
224.title: Revision questions
224.content: . | What is supervised learning? | Why is supervised learning important in biostatistics? | What is the difference between an outcome and a predictor? | Why is the prediction time point important? | What is the difference between regression and classification? | What makes survival prediction different from ordinary classification? | Why is logistic regression a good baseline model for binary clinical prediction? | What is a confusion matrix? | What is sensitivity? | What is specificity? | Why can accuracy be misleading in imbalanced medical datasets? | Why do PPV and NPV depend on disease prevalence? | What does ROC AUC measure? | Why is AUC not enough for clinical prediction? | Why should thresholds be chosen with clinical context in mind? | .

227.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
227.title: Exercise 3: Supervised Learning for Medical Data

228.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
228.title: Exercise 1: Identify outcome and predictors
228.content: For each clinical prediction problem, identify: . | the outcome; | possible predictors; | the prediction time point; | the target population. | . A. Diabetes prediction . Question: . Can we predict whether a person will develop diabetes within 10 years? . Outcome: . Predictors: . Prediction time point: . Target population: . Show answer Outcome: diabetes within 10 years. Possible predictors: age, sex, BMI, family history, fasting glucose, HbA1c, blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking status, physical activity, comorbidities. Prediction time point: baseline health assessment. Target population: people without diabetes at baseline who are being assessed for future diabetes risk. B. Hospital readmission prediction . Question: . Can we predict whether a patient will be readmitted within 30 days after hospital discharge? . Outcome: . Predictors: . Prediction time point: . Target population: . Show answer Outcome: unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge. Possible predictors: age, diagnosis, comorbidities, previous admissions, length of stay, discharge medications, lab values before discharge, social factors, discharge destination. Prediction time point: hospital discharge. Target population: patients being discharged from hospital. C. Breast cancer recurrence prediction . Question: . Can we predict whether a breast cancer patient will experience recurrence within 5 years? . Outcome: . Predictors: . Prediction time point: . Target population: . Show answer Outcome: cancer recurrence within 5 years. Possible predictors: age at diagnosis, tumour size, tumour grade, lymph node status, receptor status, treatment, comorbidities, biomarkers, gene expression signatures. Prediction time point: diagnosis or after initial treatment planning, depending on the intended model use. Target population: breast cancer patients at baseline or treatment initiation.

229.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
229.title: Exercise 2: Regression, classification, or survival prediction?
229.content: Classify each task as: . | regression; | binary classification; | multiclass classification; | survival prediction. | . | Task | Type | . | Predict systolic blood pressure |   | . | Predict diabetes yes/no |   | . | Predict cancer subtype |   | . | Predict time to cancer recurrence |   | . | Predict hospital readmission yes/no |   | . | Predict cholesterol level |   | . | Predict low/intermediate/high risk group |   | . | Predict time to death after diagnosis |   | . | Predict treatment responder/non-responder |   | . Show answer | Task | Type |---|---| Predict systolic blood pressure | Regression | Predict diabetes yes/no | Binary classification | Predict cancer subtype | Multiclass classification | Predict time to cancer recurrence | Survival prediction | Predict hospital readmission yes/no | Binary classification | Predict cholesterol level | Regression | Predict low/intermediate/high risk group | Multiclass classification or ordinal classification | Predict time to death after diagnosis | Survival prediction | Predict treatment responder/non-responder | Binary classification | .

230.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
230.title: Exercise 3: Prediction time point
230.content: For each problem, state the most appropriate prediction time point. | Problem | Prediction time point | . | Predict ICU mortality |   | . | Predict 30-day readmission |   | . | Predict 5-year cancer survival |   | . | Predict response to chemotherapy |   | . | Predict recurrence after surgery |   | . Show answer | Problem | Prediction time point |---|---| Predict ICU mortality | At ICU admission | Predict 30-day readmission | At hospital discharge | Predict 5-year cancer survival | At diagnosis or treatment start | Predict response to chemotherapy | Before chemotherapy begins | Predict recurrence after surgery | At surgery or shortly after surgery | .

231.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
231.title: Exercise 4: Appropriate predictors
231.content: You are building a model to predict mortality at hospital admission. Classify each variable as appropriate or leakage risk. | Variable | Appropriate or leakage risk? | . | Age at admission |   | . | Sex |   | . | Blood pressure at admission |   | . | Discharge destination |   | . | ICU length of stay |   | . | Comorbidity history |   | . | Lab values at admission |   | . | Lab values after clinical deterioration |   | . | Final diagnosis after discharge |   | . Show answer | Variable | Appropriate or leakage risk? |---|---| Age at admission | Appropriate | Sex | Appropriate | Blood pressure at admission | Appropriate | Discharge destination | Leakage risk | ICU length of stay | Leakage risk | Comorbidity history | Appropriate | Lab values at admission | Appropriate | Lab values after clinical deterioration | Leakage risk | Final diagnosis after discharge | Leakage risk | .

232.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
232.title: Exercise 5: Confusion matrix calculation
232.content: A model predicts disease status for 100 patients. Results: . True positives = 30 False positives = 10 True negatives = 50 False negatives = 10 . Calculate: . | Accuracy | Sensitivity | Specificity | Positive predictive value | Negative predictive value | . Show answer Total = 30 + 10 + 50 + 10 = 100 Accuracy: ```text (TP + TN) / Total = (30 + 50) / 100 = 0.80 ``` Sensitivity: ```text TP / (TP + FN) = 30 / (30 + 10) = 0.75 ``` Specificity: ```text TN / (TN + FP) = 50 / (50 + 10) = 0.833 ``` Positive predictive value: ```text TP / (TP + FP) = 30 / (30 + 10) = 0.75 ``` Negative predictive value: ```text TN / (TN + FN) = 50 / (50 + 10) = 0.833 ``` .

233.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
233.title: Exercise 6: Accuracy problem
233.content: A disease has prevalence 2%. A model predicts every patient as disease negative. Answer: . | What is the accuracy? | What is the sensitivity? | Why is this model clinically useless? | Which metrics would be more informative? | . Show answer Accuracy is 98%, because 98% of patients do not have the disease. Sensitivity is 0%, because the model detects none of the true disease cases. The model is clinically useless for identifying disease because it classifies every patient as negative. More informative metrics include sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, ROC AUC, precision-recall curve, and calibration. In screening, sensitivity is especially important.

234.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
234.title: Exercise 7: Threshold choice
234.content: A model gives predicted probability of disease. For screening, should we usually prefer: . | a lower threshold with higher sensitivity, or | a higher threshold with higher specificity? | . Explain your answer. Show answer For screening, we often prefer a lower threshold with higher sensitivity because the goal is to avoid missing true cases. However, this usually increases false positives. Whether this is acceptable depends on the harm, cost, and burden of follow-up testing.

235.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
235.title: Exercise 8: PPV and prevalence
235.content: Two populations are screened using the same model. The model has the same sensitivity and specificity in both populations. Population A has disease prevalence 20%. Population B has disease prevalence 2%. Question: . In which population will the positive predictive value usually be higher? . Show answer The positive predictive value will usually be higher in Population A because disease prevalence is higher. PPV depends strongly on prevalence. When disease is rare, many positive predictions may be false positives even if sensitivity and specificity are reasonable.

236.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
236.title: Exercise 9: AUC interpretation
236.content: A model has: . AUC = 0.82 . Answer: . | What does this tell us? | Does this tell us whether predicted probabilities are well calibrated? | What else should be assessed? | . Show answer An AUC of 0.82 suggests the model has reasonably good discrimination. It can rank patients with the outcome higher than patients without the outcome fairly well. AUC does not tell us whether predicted probabilities are calibrated. Calibration should also be assessed, along with clinical usefulness, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and performance in relevant subgroups.

237.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
237.title: Exercise 10: Logistic regression as baseline
237.content: Why is logistic regression often a good first model for binary clinical prediction? . Write 4–6 sentences. Show answer Logistic regression is a good baseline model because it is interpretable, familiar in medical statistics, and produces predicted probabilities. It works well for many tabular clinical datasets and can be assessed using standard prediction metrics. It is also easier to validate and report than many complex models. More complex machine learning models should usually be compared against logistic regression to show whether they provide meaningful improvement.

238.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
238.title: Exercise 11: Short answer questions
238.content: Write 3–5 sentences for each question. | Why is the prediction time point important? | Why should predictors be available at prediction time? | Why is accuracy not enough in medical classification? | Why can PPV be low for rare diseases? | Why should thresholds be chosen based on clinical context? | .

239.doc: Exercise 3 - Supervised Learning for Medical Data
239.title: Exercise 12: Mini case study
239.content: A team develops a model to predict 30-day readmission. They use the following variables: . Age Sex Admission diagnosis Comorbidities Length of stay Discharge medications Number of previous admissions Discharge destination Readmission status . The model is intended to be used at hospital discharge. Answer: . | Which variables are appropriate? | Which variable should not be used as a predictor? | Is length of stay appropriate in this case? | Would your answer change if the prediction time point was hospital admission? | . Show answer Appropriate predictors include age, sex, admission diagnosis, comorbidities, length of stay, discharge medications, number of previous admissions, and discharge destination, assuming the prediction is made at discharge. Readmission status should not be used as a predictor because it is the outcome. Length of stay is appropriate if the prediction is made at discharge because it is known at that time. If the prediction time point was hospital admission, length of stay, discharge medications, and discharge destination would not be available and would become leakage-risk variables.
