286.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
286.title: Exercise 1: Probability to odds

287.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
287.title: Exercise 2: Odds to probability

288.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
288.title: Exercise 3: Interpreting coefficients
288.content: A logistic regression model gives the following coefficient for age: . β_age = 0.05 . Answer: . | Is age associated with higher or lower odds of the outcome? | What is the odds ratio? | Interpret the odds ratio. | . Show answer The coefficient is positive, so higher age is associated with higher odds of the outcome. The odds ratio is: ```text exp(0.05) = 1.051 ``` This means each one-year increase in age is associated with approximately 5.1% higher odds of the outcome, holding other predictors constant.

289.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
289.title: Exercise 4: Interpreting an odds ratio
289.content: A logistic regression model for diabetes gives: . Odds ratio for BMI = 1.12 . Answer: . | What does this mean? | Does this mean the risk increases by 12%? | Why should odds ratios be interpreted carefully? | . Show answer An odds ratio of 1.12 means each one-unit increase in BMI is associated with 12% higher odds of diabetes, holding other variables constant. It does not necessarily mean the risk increases by 12%. Odds and risk are different, especially when the outcome is common. Odds ratios should be interpreted carefully because they can exaggerate the impression of effect size compared with risk ratios when outcomes are common.

290.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
290.title: Exercise 5: Inference or prediction?
290.content: Classify each logistic regression use as inference or prediction. | Question | Inference or prediction? | . | Is BMI associated with diabetes after adjustment for age and sex? |   | . | Can we predict 10-year diabetes risk? |   | . | Is smoking associated with lung cancer? |   | . | Can we predict 30-day hospital readmission? |   | . | Is tumour stage independently associated with survival? |   | . | Can we predict recurrence within 5 years? |   | . Show answer | Question | Inference or prediction? |---|---| Is BMI associated with diabetes after adjustment for age and sex? | Inference | Can we predict 10-year diabetes risk? | Prediction | Is smoking associated with lung cancer? | Inference | Can we predict 30-day hospital readmission? | Prediction | Is tumour stage independently associated with survival? | Inference/prognostic association | Can we predict recurrence within 5 years? | Prediction | .

291.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
291.title: Exercise 6: Appropriate predictors
291.content: You are building a logistic regression model to predict 30-day hospital readmission at discharge. Classify each variable as appropriate or leakage risk. | Variable | Appropriate or leakage risk? | . | Age |   | . | Sex |   | . | Admission diagnosis |   | . | Previous admissions |   | . | Length of stay |   | . | Discharge medications |   | . | Readmission status |   | . | Lab values after readmission |   | . Show answer Appropriate predictors: age, sex, admission diagnosis, previous admissions, length of stay, discharge medications. Leakage risk: readmission status and lab values after readmission. Because the prediction is made at discharge, length of stay and discharge medications are available and may be appropriate.

292.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
292.title: Exercise 7: Breast cancer prediction
292.content: Suppose you want to predict whether a breast cancer patient will experience recurrence within 5 years. Answer: . | What is the binary outcome? | List five possible predictors. | What is the prediction time point? | Why might survival analysis sometimes be better than logistic regression? | . Show answer The binary outcome is recurrence within 5 years: yes/no. Possible predictors include age at diagnosis, tumour size, tumour grade, lymph node status, receptor status, treatment, comorbidities, and baseline biomarkers. The prediction time point could be diagnosis or treatment initiation. Survival analysis may be better if patients have different follow-up times or censoring. Logistic regression ignores timing and may discard information when the outcome is time-to-event.

293.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
293.title: Exercise 8: Methylation prediction
293.content: Suppose you want to predict disease status using DNA methylation data. You have: . n = 500 participants p = 450,000 CpG sites . Answer: . | Why is ordinary logistic regression problematic? | What type of logistic regression may be more suitable? | What validation issue is especially important? | . Show answer Ordinary logistic regression is problematic because the number of predictors is much larger than the number of participants. This creates severe overfitting risk and may make the model impossible to estimate. Penalised logistic regression, such as lasso or elastic net, may be more suitable. Feature selection and preprocessing must be done inside resampling to avoid data leakage.

294.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
294.title: Exercise 9: Calibration interpretation
294.content: A logistic regression model predicts 40% risk for a group of patients. Observed event rate in this group is 20%. Answer: . | Is the model well calibrated in this group? | Is it overestimating or underestimating risk? | Why could this be clinically harmful? | . Show answer The model is not well calibrated in this group. It is overestimating risk because predicted risk is 40% but observed risk is 20%. This could lead to unnecessary treatment, unnecessary monitoring, patient anxiety, or inefficient use of clinical resources.

295.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
295.title: Exercise 10: Separation
295.content: A dataset contains 80 patients. All 10 patients with a particular biomarker value experienced the outcome. None of the 70 patients without that biomarker value experienced the outcome. Answer: . | What problem might occur in logistic regression? | Why is this a problem? | What could be done? | . Show answer This is a separation problem. The predictor perfectly separates the outcome groups, which can lead to unstable or infinite coefficient estimates. Possible solutions include penalised logistic regression, Firth correction, reducing model complexity, or collecting more data.

296.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
296.title: Exercise 11: Short answer questions
296.content: Write 3–5 sentences for each question. | Why is logistic regression suitable for binary outcomes? | Why is ordinary linear regression not ideal for binary outcomes? | What is the difference between probability and odds? | Why are odds ratios easy to misinterpret? | Why is logistic regression a useful baseline model? | Why is calibration important for logistic regression prediction models? | Why should complex machine learning models be compared with logistic regression? | .

297.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
297.title: Exercise 12: Mini case study
297.content: A researcher builds a logistic regression model to predict 1-year mortality after cancer diagnosis. They include: . Age at diagnosis Tumour stage Tumour grade Treatment type Baseline biomarker level Recurrence status during follow-up End-of-life care referral . Answer: . | Which predictors are appropriate? | Which predictors are leakage risks? | Why are they leakage risks? | How would you improve the predictor list? | . Show answer Appropriate predictors may include age at diagnosis, tumour stage, tumour grade, treatment type if known at prediction time, and baseline biomarker level. Leakage risks include recurrence status during follow-up and end-of-life care referral. These occur after baseline and are closely related to the outcome. They are leakage risks because they would not be available when predicting 1-year mortality at diagnosis. The predictor list should include only variables available at the defined prediction time point. The model should clearly state whether prediction is made at diagnosis, after treatment planning, or at another time.

298.doc: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction
298.title: Exercise 4 - Logistic Regression for Clinical Prediction

300.title: Opening scene
300.content: Scene: The model performance celebration Curious Learner: “My model has 90% accuracy. I think it is ready!” . Dr Clinic: “Does it detect the patients who actually have the disease?” . Curious Learner: “I did not check that.” . Prof Stat: “Then we need to talk about sensitivity, specificity, ROC AUC, and calibration.” . Ms Calibration: “And please do not forget whether the predicted probabilities are meaningful.” . Confusion Bug: “I thought accuracy, AUC, and calibration were all the same thing.” . Prof Stat: “That is exactly why this lecture exists.” .

301.title: Today’s big question
301.content: Why is accuracy not enough in medical prediction? . Accuracy is simple: . Accuracy = proportion of all patients correctly classified . But in medicine, different errors have different consequences. Missing a true disease case is not the same as sending a healthy person for extra testing. That is why medical classification models need more than accuracy.

302.title: Confusion matrix
302.content: For a binary outcome, predictions can be summarised using a confusion matrix. |   | Actually positive | Actually negative | . | Predicted positive | True positive | False positive | . | Predicted negative | False negative | True negative | . This table is the foundation for many classification metrics.

303.title: Mini conversation: what does the confusion matrix tell us?
303.content: Curious Learner: “So the confusion matrix tells me where the model is right and wrong?” . Prof Stat: “Yes. It separates correct positives, correct negatives, false positives, and false negatives.” . Dr Clinic: “And those errors have different clinical consequences.” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. That is why one number is rarely enough.” .

304.title: Accuracy
304.content: Accuracy answers: . What proportion of all patients did the model classify correctly? . It is calculated as: . Accuracy = (true positives + true negatives) / all patients . Accuracy can be useful, but it can be misleading when the outcome is imbalanced. For example, if only 5% of patients have a disease, a model could predict everyone as disease-negative and still achieve 95% accuracy. That model would be clinically poor for detecting disease.

305.title: Sensitivity
305.content: Sensitivity answers: . Among patients who truly have the disease, how many did the model detect? . Sensitivity = true positives / all actual positives . High sensitivity is important when missing disease is harmful. Examples: . | screening for cancer; | detecting sepsis; | identifying high-risk pregnancy; | flagging patients at risk of deterioration; | screening for diabetes. | .

306.title: Specificity
306.content: Specificity answers: . Among patients who truly do not have the disease, how many did the model correctly identify? . Specificity = true negatives / all actual negatives . High specificity is important when false positives cause harm, anxiety, or unnecessary cost. Examples: . | avoiding unnecessary invasive testing; | reducing false alarms; | preventing overtreatment; | avoiding unnecessary referrals; | reducing patient anxiety. | .

307.title: Mini conversation: sensitivity versus specificity
307.content: Curious Learner: “Which is more important: sensitivity or specificity?” . Dr Clinic: “It depends on the clinical purpose.” . Prof Stat: “For screening, sensitivity is often very important. For confirmatory decisions, specificity may matter more.” . Confusion Bug: “So there is no universally best metric?” . Prof Stat: “Correct. The best metric depends on the decision context.” .

308.title: PPV and NPV
308.content: Positive predictive value answers: . Among patients predicted positive, how many are truly positive? . Negative predictive value answers: . Among patients predicted negative, how many are truly negative? . These values depend strongly on disease prevalence. A model may have the same sensitivity and specificity but different PPV in different populations. This matters in medical practice because the same model can feel more or less useful depending on how common the disease is in the target population.

309.title: ROC curve and AUC
309.content: The ROC curve shows how sensitivity and specificity change across thresholds. AUC measures discrimination. Discrimination asks: . Does the model assign higher predicted risk to patients with the outcome than to patients without the outcome? . A higher AUC means better ranking ability. But AUC does not tell us everything. AUC tells us about ranking, not whether predicted probabilities are accurate.

310.title: Enter Ms Calibration
310.content: Ms Calibration: “AUC tells us whether the model ranks patients well. But I care about whether the predicted risks are believable.” . Curious Learner: “What does believable mean?” . Ms Calibration: “If the model says 30% risk, then around 30% of similar patients should actually have the outcome.” . Prof Stat: “That is calibration.” . Calibration asks: . Do predicted probabilities agree with observed risks? . For example, if 100 patients are given an average predicted risk of 30%, then about 30 of them should have the outcome if the model is well calibrated.

311.title: Discrimination versus calibration
311.content: Discrimination and calibration are different. | Concept | Question | . | Discrimination | Can the model rank high-risk patients above low-risk patients? | . | Calibration | Are the predicted probabilities close to observed risks? | . A model can have good discrimination but poor calibration. For clinical decision-making, both matter.

312.title: Common mistake 1
312.content: Common mistake: thinking high accuracy means clinical usefulness . High accuracy can be misleading when the disease is rare or when one class dominates. A model may be accurate overall but still fail to detect the patients who need attention. Always check sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, ROC AUC, and calibration.

313.title: Common mistake 2
313.content: Common mistake: thinking high AUC means good risk prediction . A model can have a high AUC but still give risk estimates that are too high or too low. This matters because clinical decisions are often based on absolute risk. For example, if a patient is told they have 40% risk, that number should be meaningful.

314.title: Threshold choice
314.content: A threshold converts predicted probabilities into predicted classes. For example: . Predicted risk ≥ 0.50 → positive Predicted risk < 0.50 → negative . But threshold choice should depend on clinical context. For screening, we may choose a lower threshold to increase sensitivity. For confirmatory decisions, we may choose a higher threshold to increase specificity.

315.title: Clinical reality check
315.content: Clinical reality check . In medicine, model errors have consequences. A false negative may mean: . | missed diagnosis; | delayed treatment; | unsafe reassurance. | . A false positive may mean: . | unnecessary testing; | anxiety; | cost; | overtreatment. | . A poorly calibrated risk may mean: . | misleading risk communication; | wrong treatment threshold; | poor shared decision-making. | . This is why classification metrics must be interpreted together.

316.title: Which metric should we report?
316.content: There is no single best metric for every situation. A transparent report should usually include: . | outcome definition; | positive class; | threshold used; | confusion matrix; | sensitivity; | specificity; | PPV; | NPV; | ROC AUC; | calibration assessment; | validation method. | . The final interpretation should connect the numbers to the clinical decision.

317.title: Mini conversation: final performance check
317.content: Curious Learner: “So I should not just report accuracy?” . Prof Stat: “Correct.” . Dr Clinic: “I need to know what errors the model makes and whether the risk estimates are useful.” . Ms Calibration: “And whether predicted probabilities match observed risks.” . Confusion Bug: “Fine. I will stop calling every metric accuracy.” .

318.title: Key takeaway
318.content: A medical classification model should not be judged only by accuracy. A proper evaluation should consider: . | confusion matrix; | sensitivity; | specificity; | PPV; | NPV; | ROC AUC; | calibration; | threshold choice; | clinical consequences. | . A model is useful only if its performance supports the intended medical decision.

319.title: Reflection question
319.content: For a diabetes screening model, which is more important: sensitivity or specificity? . How would your answer change if the model were used to decide on an expensive or risky treatment? . Show suggested answer For diabetes screening, sensitivity may be especially important because missing true cases could delay diagnosis and intervention. However, specificity also matters because too many false positives could lead to unnecessary follow-up testing and anxiety. If the model were used to decide on an expensive or risky treatment, specificity and positive predictive value may become more important, because unnecessary treatment could cause harm or waste resources.

322.title: Learning objectives
322.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why classification metrics are essential in medical machine learning. | Interpret accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value. | Understand why accuracy can be misleading in imbalanced medical datasets. | Explain how disease prevalence affects positive and negative predictive values. | Interpret ROC curves and AUC in clinical prediction. | Understand the difference between discrimination and calibration. | Explain why threshold choice should depend on clinical context. | Understand why one metric is never enough in medical prediction. | Apply classification metrics to examples from clinical prediction, breast cancer research, and methylation-based biomedical prediction. | .

323.title: 1. Introduction
323.content: After building a classification model, the next question is not simply: . Did the model run successfully? . The more important question is: . Is the model useful, reliable, and clinically meaningful? . In medical machine learning, classification models are often used to predict binary outcomes such as disease status, hospital readmission, treatment response, recurrence, or mortality within a fixed time period. These models usually produce either a predicted class or a predicted probability. For example, a model may predict: . Disease: yes/no Readmission within 30 days: yes/no Treatment response: responder/non-responder Cancer recurrence within 5 years: yes/no Malignant tumour: yes/no Exposure status: exposed/unexposed . To evaluate such models, we need performance metrics. However, in medicine, performance metrics must be interpreted carefully. A model with high accuracy may still be clinically useless. A model with high AUC may still give poorly calibrated probabilities. A model with high sensitivity may produce many false positives. A model with high specificity may miss many true cases. This is why classification metrics in medicine are not just mathematical calculations. They are tools for understanding clinical trade-offs. A good medical prediction model should be assessed using a combination of metrics, including discrimination, calibration, threshold-based performance, and clinical usefulness (Steyerberg, 2019; James et al., 2023).

324.title: 2. Binary classification in medical prediction
324.content: In binary classification, the outcome has two categories. Examples include: . | Medical problem | Positive class | Negative class | . | Diabetes prediction | Diabetes | No diabetes | . | Cancer diagnosis | Malignant | Benign | . | Hospital readmission | Readmitted | Not readmitted | . | Treatment response | Responder | Non-responder | . | 5-year recurrence | Recurrence | No recurrence | . | Exposure prediction from methylation | Exposed | Unexposed | . The “positive” class does not always mean something good. In medical prediction, the positive class usually means the event or condition of interest. For example: . Positive = disease present Negative = disease absent . or: . Positive = cancer recurrence Negative = no recurrence . Before calculating metrics, it is essential to define the positive class clearly. If the positive class is coded incorrectly, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV may be interpreted incorrectly.

325.title: 3. Predicted probabilities and predicted classes
325.content: Many classification models produce predicted probabilities. For example: . | Patient | Predicted probability of disease | . | A | 0.82 | . | B | 0.61 | . | C | 0.44 | . | D | 0.12 | . These probabilities can be converted into predicted classes using a threshold. If the threshold is 0.50: . Predicted probability ≥ 0.50 → positive Predicted probability < 0.50 → negative . Then: . | Patient | Predicted probability | Predicted class | . | A | 0.82 | Positive | . | B | 0.61 | Positive | . | C | 0.44 | Negative | . | D | 0.12 | Negative | . However, a threshold of 0.50 is not automatically appropriate in medicine. For some clinical problems, a lower threshold may be preferred because missing a true case is dangerous. For other problems, a higher threshold may be preferred because false positives may lead to unnecessary invasive testing or harmful treatment. This means classification is not only a statistical problem. It is also a clinical decision problem.

326.title: 4. The confusion matrix
326.content: Most threshold-based classification metrics are derived from the confusion matrix. A confusion matrix compares the model’s predicted class with the observed outcome. |   | Predicted positive | Predicted negative | . | Actual positive | True positive | False negative | . | Actual negative | False positive | True negative | . The four cells are: . | True positive (TP): the model predicts positive and the patient truly has the outcome. | False positive (FP): the model predicts positive but the patient does not have the outcome. | True negative (TN): the model predicts negative and the patient truly does not have the outcome. | False negative (FN): the model predicts negative but the patient truly has the outcome. | . In medicine, false positives and false negatives often have very different consequences. For example, in cancer screening: . | a false negative may delay diagnosis; | a false positive may cause anxiety, unnecessary imaging, biopsy, or follow-up. | . Both errors matter, but they matter differently.

327.title: 5. Accuracy
327.content: Accuracy is the proportion of all patients correctly classified. Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (TP + FP + TN + FN) . Accuracy answers: . Out of all patients, how many did the model classify correctly? . For example: . TP = 30 FP = 10 TN = 50 FN = 10 . Then: . Accuracy = (30 + 50) / (30 + 10 + 50 + 10) = 80 / 100 = 0.80 . So the model has 80% accuracy. Accuracy is simple and easy to understand, but it can be misleading in medical datasets, especially when the outcome is rare.

328.title: 6. Why accuracy can be misleading
328.content: Suppose a disease has prevalence 2%. This means: . 2% have disease 98% do not have disease . Now imagine a model that predicts “no disease” for every patient. This model will be correct for 98% of patients. Accuracy = 98% . At first glance, this sounds excellent. But the model detects no disease cases. Its sensitivity is: . Sensitivity = 0% . This model is clinically useless for screening because it fails to identify every patient with disease. This example shows why accuracy alone should not be used to evaluate medical classification models. In imbalanced datasets, high accuracy can simply reflect the majority class. This is especially important in medicine because many clinically important outcomes are uncommon: . | rare diseases; | hospital mortality; | severe adverse events; | treatment toxicity; | cancer recurrence in low-risk groups; | specific molecular subtypes. | . In these settings, accuracy can give a false sense of model performance.

329.title: 7. Sensitivity
329.content: Sensitivity is the proportion of actual positive cases correctly identified by the model. Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN) . Sensitivity answers: . Among patients who truly have the outcome, how many did the model detect? . Sensitivity is also called: . True positive rate Recall . For example: . TP = 30 FN = 10 . Then: . Sensitivity = 30 / (30 + 10) = 30 / 40 = 0.75 . The model detects 75% of true positive cases. When is sensitivity important? . Sensitivity is especially important when missing a case is harmful. Examples: . | cancer screening; | sepsis alerts; | identifying high-risk ICU patients; | infectious disease screening; | detecting serious adverse drug reactions; | identifying patients at high risk of suicide or self-harm; | screening for disease in pregnancy. | . In these settings, a false negative may delay diagnosis or treatment. Therefore, a model with low sensitivity may be unacceptable even if its accuracy is high.

330.title: 8. Specificity
330.content: Specificity is the proportion of actual negative cases correctly identified by the model. Specificity = TN / (TN + FP) . Specificity answers: . Among patients who truly do not have the outcome, how many did the model correctly classify as negative? . Specificity is also called: . True negative rate . For example: . TN = 50 FP = 10 . Then: . Specificity = 50 / (50 + 10) = 50 / 60 = 0.833 . The model correctly identifies 83.3% of true negative cases. When is specificity important? . Specificity is especially important when false positives are harmful. Examples: . | avoiding unnecessary invasive diagnostic tests; | avoiding unnecessary chemotherapy or surgery; | avoiding unnecessary isolation or treatment; | avoiding patient anxiety; | avoiding overuse of limited healthcare resources. | . For example, if a prediction model recommends a treatment with serious side effects, low specificity may lead to overtreatment.

331.title: 9. Sensitivity-specificity trade-off
331.content: Sensitivity and specificity often move in opposite directions. If we lower the classification threshold, more patients are classified as positive. This usually: . increases sensitivity decreases specificity . If we raise the classification threshold, fewer patients are classified as positive. This usually: . decreases sensitivity increases specificity . For example, suppose a model predicts probability of disease. At threshold 0.30: . Sensitivity = 0.90 Specificity = 0.60 . At threshold 0.70: . Sensitivity = 0.55 Specificity = 0.92 . Neither threshold is automatically correct. The better threshold depends on the clinical purpose. In screening, we may prefer the first threshold because we want to detect most true cases. In confirmatory decision-making, we may prefer the second threshold because we want fewer false positives.

332.title: 10. Positive predictive value
332.content: Positive predictive value, or PPV, is the proportion of predicted positive cases who truly have the outcome. PPV = TP / (TP + FP) . PPV answers: . Among patients predicted to be positive, how many truly have the outcome? . For example: . TP = 30 FP = 10 . Then: . PPV = 30 / (30 + 10) = 30 / 40 = 0.75 . This means 75% of patients predicted positive truly have the outcome. PPV is especially important when a positive prediction leads to action. For example: . | referral for further testing; | additional imaging; | biopsy; | treatment; | monitoring; | clinical review; | genetic counselling. | . If PPV is low, many patients flagged by the model may not actually have the outcome.

333.title: 11. Negative predictive value
333.content: Negative predictive value, or NPV, is the proportion of predicted negative cases who truly do not have the outcome. NPV = TN / (TN + FN) . NPV answers: . Among patients predicted to be negative, how many truly do not have the outcome? . For example: . TN = 50 FN = 10 . Then: . NPV = 50 / (50 + 10) = 50 / 60 = 0.833 . This means 83.3% of patients predicted negative truly do not have the outcome. NPV is important when a negative prediction is used for reassurance or de-escalation. Examples: . | ruling out disease; | deciding not to refer; | reducing monitoring intensity; | avoiding unnecessary treatment. | . However, NPV can be high simply because the disease is rare. Therefore, NPV should be interpreted alongside prevalence, sensitivity, and specificity.

334.title: 12. Prevalence affects PPV and NPV
334.content: Sensitivity and specificity are often described as test characteristics. They are not completely fixed in all settings, but they are less directly dependent on prevalence than PPV and NPV. PPV and NPV depend strongly on disease prevalence. If disease prevalence is high, PPV tends to be higher. If disease prevalence is low, PPV tends to be lower. This is important in screening. Suppose a disease is rare. Even if the model has good sensitivity and specificity, many positive predictions may be false positives because true cases are uncommon. This is why a model may perform differently in different populations. For example: . Population A: disease prevalence = 20% Population B: disease prevalence = 2% . Using the same model, PPV will usually be higher in Population A than in Population B. This matters when applying a prediction model to a new hospital, country, age group, or screening population. A model developed in a high-risk population may not have the same PPV in a lower-risk population.

335.title: 13. Worked example: confusion matrix metrics
335.content: Suppose a model predicts disease status in 200 patients. True positives = 40 False positives = 20 True negatives = 120 False negatives = 20 . The confusion matrix is: . |   | Predicted positive | Predicted negative | . | Actual positive | 40 | 20 | . | Actual negative | 20 | 120 | . Accuracy . Accuracy = (TP + TN) / Total = (40 + 120) / 200 = 160 / 200 = 0.80 . Sensitivity . Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN) = 40 / (40 + 20) = 40 / 60 = 0.667 . Specificity . Specificity = TN / (TN + FP) = 120 / (120 + 20) = 120 / 140 = 0.857 . PPV . PPV = TP / (TP + FP) = 40 / (40 + 20) = 40 / 60 = 0.667 . NPV . NPV = TN / (TN + FN) = 120 / (120 + 20) = 120 / 140 = 0.857 . This model is better at identifying negative cases than positive cases. Whether this is acceptable depends on the clinical task.

336.title: 14. ROC curve
336.content: The ROC curve shows how sensitivity and specificity change across all possible classification thresholds. It plots: . Sensitivity . against: . 1 - Specificity . where 1 - Specificity is the false positive rate. At each threshold, we get a different pair of sensitivity and specificity values. The ROC curve summarises the model’s ability to rank patients by risk across thresholds. A model with better discrimination will have a ROC curve closer to the top-left corner. A model with no discrimination will lie near the diagonal line.

337.title: 15. AUC
337.content: AUC means area under the ROC curve. AUC is a measure of discrimination. Discrimination asks: . Can the model assign higher predicted risks to patients who experience the outcome than to patients who do not? . Interpretation: . AUC = 0.5: no discrimination AUC = 1.0: perfect discrimination . An AUC of 0.80 suggests that the model has reasonably good ability to rank patients by risk. A common interpretation is: . If we randomly choose one patient who experienced the outcome and one patient who did not, the model will assign a higher predicted risk to the outcome patient 80% of the time. AUC is useful, but it does not tell us everything.

338.title: 16. Limitations of AUC
338.content: AUC has several limitations in medical prediction. First, AUC does not measure calibration. A model can have good AUC but produce predicted probabilities that are too high or too low. For example: . Predicted risk = 40% Observed risk = 15% . This model may rank patients well but overestimate absolute risk. Second, AUC does not tell us which threshold should be used. A model with AUC 0.85 may still perform poorly at the threshold that matters clinically. Third, AUC can be insensitive to clinically important improvements. A new biomarker may improve decision-making in a high-risk subgroup but only slightly change the overall AUC. Fourth, AUC may look acceptable even when the model is not clinically useful. Therefore, AUC should be reported with other metrics such as calibration, sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, and clinical utility (Steyerberg, 2019).

339.title: 17. Precision-recall curve
339.content: The precision-recall curve is another way to evaluate classification models, especially when the positive outcome is rare. In this context: . Precision = PPV Recall = Sensitivity . A precision-recall curve plots: . PPV against sensitivity . This can be more informative than the ROC curve when the outcome is rare. For example, in rare disease prediction, the ROC AUC may look high even when the PPV is poor. The precision-recall curve focuses more directly on the performance among predicted positives. This is useful when the main concern is: . Among patients flagged by the model, how many are truly positive? . Precision-recall curves are particularly relevant in: . | rare disease screening; | adverse event prediction; | high-dimensional biomarker classification; | case-finding algorithms in electronic health records. | .

340.title: 18. Threshold choice in medicine
340.content: A classification threshold converts predicted probabilities into predicted classes. For example: . Predicted risk ≥ 0.20 → high risk Predicted risk < 0.20 → low risk . The threshold should not be chosen only because it gives the highest accuracy. Instead, threshold choice should reflect the clinical setting. Questions to ask: . | What happens if we miss a true case? | What happens if we falsely label someone high risk? | Is the model used for screening, diagnosis, treatment, or monitoring? | What is the cost of follow-up testing? | Is the treatment harmful, expensive, or scarce? | What risk level would justify clinical action? | . For example, in a sepsis alert system, we may accept more false positives to avoid missing true cases. In a model recommending chemotherapy, false positives may be more harmful because treatment has serious side effects. This is why threshold selection should involve clinical reasoning, not only statistical optimisation.

341.title: 19. Discrimination versus calibration
341.content: Two central concepts in clinical prediction are: . Discrimination Calibration . Discrimination . Discrimination asks: . Can the model separate patients who experience the outcome from those who do not? . Metrics include: . | ROC AUC; | C-statistic; | sensitivity and specificity at selected thresholds. | . Calibration . Calibration asks: . Do predicted probabilities agree with observed risks? . For example, if 100 patients are each predicted to have 20% risk, then approximately 20 of them should experience the outcome if the model is well calibrated. A model can have good discrimination but poor calibration. Example: . AUC = 0.85 Predicted average risk = 40% Observed average risk = 15% . This model ranks patients well but overestimates risk. In medicine, calibration is essential because clinical decisions often depend on absolute risk, not just ranking.

342.title: 20. Brier score
342.content: The Brier score is a measure of prediction error for probabilistic predictions. For binary outcomes: . Brier score = mean((predicted probability - observed outcome)^2) . The observed outcome is coded as: . 1 = event occurred 0 = event did not occur . A lower Brier score is better. A Brier score of 0 means perfect probability prediction. Example: . | Patient | Predicted probability | Observed outcome | . | A | 0.80 | 1 | . | B | 0.60 | 1 | . | C | 0.30 | 0 | . | D | 0.10 | 0 | . These predictions are reasonably close to the observed outcomes. The Brier score combines aspects of discrimination and calibration, but it should still be interpreted alongside other metrics.

343.title: 21. Example from breast cancer survival work
343.content: Your breast cancer survival dashboard focused mainly on time-to-event outcomes using methods such as Kaplan-Meier curves, log-rank tests, Cox models, penalised Cox models, random survival forests, and calibration. In survival analysis, the outcome is usually: . time to event . such as time to death or time to recurrence. However, sometimes a survival question is converted into a binary classification question. For example: . Did the patient experience recurrence within 5 years? Yes/No . Then classification metrics may be used. Suppose a model predicts 5-year recurrence in breast cancer patients. Important metrics may include: . | sensitivity: among patients who truly recur within 5 years, how many are identified as high risk? | specificity: among patients who do not recur, how many are correctly classified as low risk? | PPV: among patients predicted high risk, how many actually recur? | NPV: among patients predicted low risk, how many remain recurrence-free? | AUC: how well does the model rank patients by recurrence risk? | calibration: do predicted 5-year recurrence probabilities match observed recurrence risks? | . But there is an important caution. If follow-up times differ and censoring is present, ordinary binary classification metrics may be inappropriate or incomplete. A patient who has not recurred by 3 years may still recur by 5 years, but if follow-up ends at 3 years, their 5-year status is unknown. In that case, survival-specific metrics such as the C-index, time-dependent AUC, and time-dependent Brier score may be more appropriate. This connects with a key biostatistical principle: . The evaluation metric should match the outcome structure.

344.title: 22. Example from methylation and EWAS-style work
344.content: Your current research interests include DNA methylation, epidemiology, high-dimensional biomedical data, and EWAS-style analysis. In a traditional EWAS, the question may be: . Which CpG sites are associated with an exposure or outcome? . That is mainly an association or inference question. A classification version of the problem might be: . Can methylation profiles classify disease status? . or: . Can methylation profiles predict exposure status? . For example: . Outcome: exposed vs unexposed Predictors: selected CpG methylation markers . In this setting, classification metrics are useful, but there are several risks. First, the number of predictors may be very large. n = 800 participants p = 450,000 CpG sites . This creates a serious risk of overfitting. Second, feature selection can cause leakage if it is done before the train/test split or before cross-validation. Third, the outcome may be imbalanced. For example, if only a small proportion of participants are exposed, accuracy may be misleading. Fourth, PPV may be low when the positive class is rare. Therefore, methylation-based classification should report more than accuracy. It should include sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, ROC AUC, calibration where probabilities are used, and ideally external validation in an independent cohort. In high-dimensional biomedical prediction, careful validation is just as important as model choice.

345.title: 23. Example: screening versus treatment decision
345.content: The same model may require different thresholds depending on the intended use. Suppose a model predicts risk of a disease. Screening use . If the model is used for screening, the goal may be to identify as many possible cases as possible. In that case, high sensitivity may be prioritised. A lower threshold may be chosen: . Predicted risk ≥ 0.10 → screen positive . This may increase false positives, but that may be acceptable if follow-up testing is safe and inexpensive. Treatment decision use . If the model is used to decide whether to give a treatment with serious side effects, false positives may be more harmful. A higher threshold may be chosen: . Predicted risk ≥ 0.40 → treat . This may reduce overtreatment but may miss some true cases. The correct threshold depends on clinical consequences, not only statistical performance.

346.title: 24. Balanced accuracy
346.content: Balanced accuracy is useful when the outcome classes are imbalanced. It is the average of sensitivity and specificity: . Balanced accuracy = (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2 . For example, if: . Sensitivity = 0.70 Specificity = 0.90 . then: . Balanced accuracy = (0.70 + 0.90) / 2 = 0.80 . Balanced accuracy gives equal weight to positive and negative classes. It can be more informative than ordinary accuracy when one class is much more common than the other. However, balanced accuracy still does not replace clinical interpretation. A model with balanced accuracy of 0.80 may still be unacceptable if sensitivity is too low for the intended use.

347.title: 25. F1 score
347.content: The F1 score combines precision and recall. Precision = PPV Recall = Sensitivity . The F1 score is: . F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall) . F1 score is commonly used in machine learning, especially when the positive class is important and imbalanced. However, F1 score has limitations in medicine. It ignores true negatives. This means it does not directly account for specificity. In some clinical settings, false positives and true negatives are very important. Therefore, F1 score may be useful in some biomedical classification tasks, but it should not be the only metric reported.

348.title: 26. Choosing metrics according to clinical purpose
348.content: The best metric depends on the clinical question. | Clinical purpose | Metrics that may matter | . | Screening | Sensitivity, NPV, PPV, burden of false positives | . | Diagnosis | Sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV | . | Risk prediction | AUC, calibration, Brier score, decision curve analysis | . | Treatment decision | Calibration, net benefit, threshold-specific performance | . | Rare disease prediction | PPV, sensitivity, precision-recall curve | . | Patient reassurance | NPV, calibration, safety of false negatives | . | Resource allocation | PPV, specificity, clinical utility | . A model should not be judged only by the metric that looks best. Instead, the evaluation should match the intended use.

349.title: 27. Common mistakes when reporting classification metrics
349.content: Common mistakes include: . | Reporting accuracy alone. | Ignoring class imbalance. | Reporting AUC without calibration. | Not defining the positive class. | Choosing a threshold without explanation. | Reporting sensitivity but not specificity. | Reporting PPV and NPV without discussing prevalence. | Using test data repeatedly to choose the best threshold. | Ignoring confidence intervals around performance estimates. | Evaluating row-level data when patient-level splitting is needed. | Reporting only internal validation without external validation. | Applying ordinary binary metrics to censored survival outcomes. | . These mistakes can make a model appear more useful than it really is.

350.title: 28. How to report classification performance
350.content: A clear report should include: . | outcome definition; | positive class definition; | sample size; | number of events; | prevalence; | prediction time point; | validation approach; | classification threshold; | confusion matrix; | sensitivity; | specificity; | PPV; | NPV; | ROC AUC; | calibration assessment; | uncertainty intervals where possible; | clinical interpretation. | . For clinical prediction models, transparent reporting is important because readers need to judge whether the model is valid and applicable to their setting (Collins et al., 2024).

351.title: 29. Practical interpretation framework
351.content: When interpreting a classification model, ask: . | What is the clinical question? | What is the positive class? | What is the prediction time point? | What is the outcome prevalence? | Is the dataset imbalanced? | What threshold was used? | Is sensitivity high enough for the intended use? | Is specificity high enough for the intended use? | Is PPV acceptable? | Is NPV acceptable? | Does the model discriminate well? | Is the model calibrated? | Would the model change clinical decisions? | Was the model validated properly? | Could performance be inflated by leakage? | . This framework is more useful than asking only whether accuracy or AUC is high.

352.title: 30. Summary
352.content: Classification metrics are central to evaluating medical machine learning models, but they must be interpreted carefully. Accuracy is easy to understand but can be misleading when outcomes are imbalanced. Sensitivity and specificity describe how well the model identifies true positives and true negatives. PPV and NPV describe how trustworthy positive and negative predictions are, but they depend strongly on prevalence. ROC AUC measures discrimination, but it does not measure calibration or clinical usefulness. Precision-recall curves may be helpful for rare outcomes. Threshold choice should depend on clinical context and the consequences of false positives and false negatives. For your own areas of interest, these ideas are directly relevant. In breast cancer prediction, classification metrics may be useful for fixed-time recurrence prediction, but survival-specific metrics are needed when censoring is present. In methylation and EWAS-style prediction, class imbalance, high dimensionality, feature-selection leakage, and external validation are major concerns. The main message is: . In medical machine learning, model performance should be judged by a combination of metrics that reflect the clinical purpose, outcome structure, prevalence, validation design, calibration, and consequences of decision-making.

354.title: Key terms
354.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Accuracy | Proportion of all patients correctly classified | . | Sensitivity | Proportion of true positives correctly identified | . | Specificity | Proportion of true negatives correctly identified | . | PPV | Proportion of predicted positives who are truly positive | . | NPV | Proportion of predicted negatives who are truly negative | . | ROC curve | Plot of sensitivity against false positive rate across thresholds | . | AUC | Area under the ROC curve; measures discrimination | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted probabilities and observed risks | . | Brier score | Mean squared error of predicted probabilities | . | Threshold | Cut-off used to convert probabilities into classes | . | Prevalence | Proportion of individuals with the outcome | . | False positive | Predicted positive but actually negative | . | False negative | Predicted negative but actually positive | .
