Springer. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. Springer. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R (2nd ed.). Springer. | Kuhn, M., & Johnson, K. (2013). Applied Predictive Modeling. Springer. | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd ed.). Springer. | Collins, G. S., Moons, K. G. M., Dhiman, P., et al. (2024). TRIPOD+AI statement: updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that use regression or machine learning methods. BMJ, 385, e078378. | Moons, K. G. M., Damen, J. A. A. G., Kaul, T., et al. (2025). PROBAST+AI: an updated quality, risk of bias, and applicability assessment tool for prediction models using regression or artificial intelligence methods. BMJ, 388, e082505. | .

358.title: Exercise 1: Confusion matrix terms
358.content: Complete the definitions. | Term | Meaning | . | True positive |   | . | False positive |   | . | True negative |   | . | False negative |   | . Show answer | Term | Meaning |---|---| True positive | Model predicts positive and the patient truly has the outcome | False positive | Model predicts positive but the patient does not have the outcome | True negative | Model predicts negative and the patient truly does not have the outcome | False negative | Model predicts negative but the patient truly has the outcome | .

359.title: Exercise 2: Calculate classification metrics
359.content: A model gives the following results: . True positives = 40 False positives = 20 True negatives = 120 False negatives = 20 . Calculate: . | Accuracy | Sensitivity | Specificity | Positive predictive value | Negative predictive value | . Show answer Total: ```text 40 + 20 + 120 + 20 = 200 ``` Accuracy: ```text (TP + TN) / Total = (40 + 120) / 200 = 0.80 ``` Sensitivity: ```text TP / (TP + FN) = 40 / (40 + 20) = 0.667 ``` Specificity: ```text TN / (TN + FP) = 120 / (120 + 20) = 0.857 ``` Positive predictive value: ```text TP / (TP + FP) = 40 / (40 + 20) = 0.667 ``` Negative predictive value: ```text TN / (TN + FN) = 120 / (120 + 20) = 0.857 ``` .

360.title: Exercise 3: Accuracy and rare disease
360.content: A disease has prevalence 1%. A model predicts every patient as disease negative. Answer: . | What is the accuracy? | What is the sensitivity? | Why is this model not clinically useful for screening? | . Show answer The accuracy is 99%, because 99% of patients do not have the disease. The sensitivity is 0%, because the model identifies none of the true disease cases. The model is not useful for screening because it misses every patient with the disease.

361.title: Exercise 4: Sensitivity or specificity?
361.content: For each situation, state whether sensitivity or specificity is more important. | Situation | Sensitivity or specificity? | . | Cancer screening test |   | . | Sepsis early warning alert |   | . | Confirmatory test before invasive procedure |   | . | Model recommending toxic chemotherapy |   | . | Infectious disease screening at airport |   | . | Avoiding unnecessary biopsy |   | . Show answer | Situation | Sensitivity or specificity? |---|---| Cancer screening test | Sensitivity | Sepsis early warning alert | Sensitivity | Confirmatory test before invasive procedure | Specificity | Model recommending toxic chemotherapy | Specificity | Infectious disease screening at airport | Sensitivity | Avoiding unnecessary biopsy | Specificity | These are general answers. In practice, the final decision depends on clinical consequences, cost, feasibility, and patient safety.

362.title: Exercise 5: PPV and prevalence
362.content: Two populations are tested with the same model. The model has the same sensitivity and specificity in both populations. Population A prevalence = 20% Population B prevalence = 2% . Question: . In which population will PPV usually be higher, and why? . Show answer PPV will usually be higher in Population A because the outcome is more common. When prevalence is low, many positive predictions may be false positives, even if sensitivity and specificity are reasonable.

363.title: Exercise 6: NPV and rare disease
363.content: A model has very high NPV in a rare disease setting. Question: . Does this automatically mean the model is excellent? Explain. Show answer No. NPV can be high simply because the disease is rare. If most people do not have the disease, many negative predictions will be correct even for a weak model. NPV should be interpreted alongside prevalence, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and clinical purpose.

364.title: Exercise 7: Threshold trade-off
364.content: A model predicts probability of disease. At threshold 0.20: . Sensitivity = 0.92 Specificity = 0.55 . At threshold 0.70: . Sensitivity = 0.50 Specificity = 0.94 . Answer: . | Which threshold may be preferred for screening? | Which threshold may be preferred before giving a risky treatment? | Why? | . Show answer For screening, threshold 0.20 may be preferred because it gives higher sensitivity and misses fewer true cases. Before giving a risky treatment, threshold 0.70 may be preferred because it gives higher specificity and reduces false positives. The preferred threshold depends on the consequences of false positives and false negatives.

365.title: Exercise 8: AUC interpretation
365.content: A model has: . AUC = 0.84 . Answer: . | What does this tell us? | Does it tell us whether predicted probabilities are well calibrated? | Does it tell us which threshold should be used? | . Show answer An AUC of 0.84 suggests good discrimination. The model is reasonably good at ranking patients who experience the outcome above those who do not. AUC does not tell us whether predicted probabilities are calibrated. AUC also does not tell us which threshold should be used. Threshold choice depends on clinical context.

366.title: Exercise 9: Calibration problem
366.content: A model predicts 40% risk for a group of patients. The observed event rate in that group is 15%. Answer: . | Is the model well calibrated? | Is it overestimating or underestimating risk? | Why could this be harmful? | . Show answer The model is not well calibrated in this group. It is overestimating risk because predicted risk is 40%, but observed risk is 15%. This could lead to unnecessary treatment, monitoring, anxiety, or inefficient use of healthcare resources.

367.title: Exercise 10: Breast cancer recurrence example
367.content: A model predicts 5-year breast cancer recurrence. Answer: . | What is the positive class? | What would sensitivity mean? | What would specificity mean? | Why might ordinary binary classification metrics be incomplete if censoring is present? | . Show answer The positive class is recurrence within 5 years. Sensitivity means: among patients who truly recur within 5 years, how many are classified as high risk. Specificity means: among patients who do not recur within 5 years, how many are classified as low risk. If censoring is present, some patients may not have known 5-year recurrence status. In that case, survival-specific metrics such as C-index, time-dependent AUC, and time-dependent Brier score may be more appropriate.

368.title: Exercise 11: Methylation classification example
368.content: Suppose a model uses DNA methylation markers to classify disease status. n = 600 participants p = 450,000 CpG sites . Answer: . | Why might accuracy be misleading? | Why is feature-selection leakage a risk? | Which metrics would you report? | . Show answer Accuracy may be misleading if the disease classes are imbalanced. Feature-selection leakage is a risk because selecting CpG sites using the full dataset before cross-validation allows outcome information from the test folds to influence model development. Useful metrics include sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, ROC AUC, calibration if predicted probabilities are used, and external validation where possible.

369.title: Exercise 12: Choose appropriate metrics
369.content: Choose useful metrics for each clinical purpose. | Clinical purpose | Useful metrics | . | Screening |   | . | Confirmatory diagnosis |   | . | Treatment decision |   | . | Rare disease prediction |   | . | Risk prediction |   | . | Patient reassurance |   | . Show answer | Clinical purpose | Useful metrics |---|---| Screening | Sensitivity, NPV, PPV, false positive burden | Confirmatory diagnosis | Specificity, PPV, sensitivity | Treatment decision | Calibration, threshold-specific sensitivity/specificity, net benefit | Rare disease prediction | PPV, sensitivity, precision-recall curve | Risk prediction | AUC, calibration, Brier score, decision curve analysis | Patient reassurance | NPV, sensitivity, calibration | .

370.title: Exercise 13: Short answer questions
370.content: Write 3–5 sentences for each question. | Why is accuracy not enough in medical prediction? | Why does PPV depend on prevalence? | Why can AUC be misleading if reported alone? | What is the difference between discrimination and calibration? | Why should threshold choice depend on clinical context? | Why is external validation important for classification models? | .

371.title: Exercise 14: Mini case study
371.content: A hospital develops a model to identify patients at risk of sepsis. At threshold 0.30: . Sensitivity = 0.95 Specificity = 0.50 . At threshold 0.70: . Sensitivity = 0.60 Specificity = 0.90 . The clinical team wants to use the model as an early warning alert. Answer: . | Which threshold seems more appropriate? | What is the trade-off? | What additional information would you want before making a final decision? | . Show answer Threshold 0.30 may be more appropriate because the model is being used as an early warning alert, where missing sepsis cases could be dangerous. The trade-off is that specificity is lower, meaning more false positives and more alerts for patients who do not develop sepsis. Additional information needed includes alert burden, clinician workload, harms of false alarms, PPV, calibration, time-to-alert, clinical workflow, and external validation.

374.title: Learning objectives
374.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why cross-validation is used in medical machine learning. | Distinguish a single train/test split from k-fold cross-validation. | Describe how k-fold cross-validation works. | Explain why cross-validation is a form of internal validation. | Recognise why preprocessing and feature selection must be done inside the cross-validation process. | Understand why cross-validation does not replace external validation. | Explain how cross-validation can be used for model comparison and tuning. | Identify common mistakes that can make cross-validation results too optimistic. | .

375.title: 1. Why this note matters
375.content: In previous notes, we discussed why validation is central to medical machine learning. A model should not be judged only by how well it performs on the data used to develop it. The important question is whether it can make useful predictions for new patients. A simple train/test split is one way to estimate performance on unseen data. However, a single split can be unstable, especially when the dataset is small, the outcome is uncommon, or the patient population is heterogeneous. The result may depend too much on which patients happen to be placed in the training set and which patients happen to be placed in the test set. Cross-validation helps address this problem. Instead of relying on one random split, it repeatedly splits the data into training and validation parts. This gives a more stable internal estimate of model performance. The practical message of this note is: . Cross-validation helps estimate how well a model may perform on unseen patients from a similar source population, but it must be done carefully to avoid leakage.

376.title: 2. The problem with one train/test split
376.content: A train/test split divides the dataset into two parts: . Training set: used to fit the model Test set: used to evaluate the model . A common split is: . 80% training 20% testing . This is simple and easy to understand. However, a single split may give a performance estimate that depends heavily on the random split. For example, in a diabetes prediction study, one split may give: . AUC = 0.82 . Another split may give: . AUC = 0.74 . This does not necessarily mean the model has changed. It may mean that the test set changed. One test set may contain easier-to-predict patients, while another may contain more difficult cases. This instability becomes more important when: . | the sample size is small; | the number of outcome events is limited; | the outcome is imbalanced; | predictors are noisy; | the model is flexible; | there is strong patient heterogeneity. | .

377.title: 3. What is cross-validation?
377.content: Cross-validation is an internal validation method. It repeatedly divides the available data into training and validation parts. The model is fitted on the training part and evaluated on the validation part. This process is repeated several times, and the performance estimates are averaged. Instead of asking: . How well does the model perform on one test split? . cross-validation asks: . How well does the model perform across several validation splits? . This usually gives a more stable estimate of internal performance.

378.title: 4. k-fold cross-validation
378.content: The most common form is k-fold cross-validation. In k-fold cross-validation, the dataset is divided into k approximately equal parts called folds. For example, in 5-fold cross-validation: . | The data are split into 5 folds. | The model is trained on 4 folds. | The remaining fold is used for validation. | This is repeated 5 times. | Each fold is used once as the validation fold. | The performance values are averaged. | . Iteration 1: Train on folds 2,3,4,5; validate on fold 1 Iteration 2: Train on folds 1,3,4,5; validate on fold 2 Iteration 3: Train on folds 1,2,4,5; validate on fold 3 Iteration 4: Train on folds 1,2,3,5; validate on fold 4 Iteration 5: Train on folds 1,2,3,4; validate on fold 5 . Each patient is used for validation once and training several times.

379.title: 5. Why cross-validation is useful
379.content: Cross-validation is useful because it reduces dependence on one particular split. It is commonly used for: . | estimating internal model performance; | comparing different models; | tuning model parameters; | assessing model stability; | making better use of limited data. | . For example, suppose we compare: . | logistic regression; | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | random forest. | . Cross-validation can estimate how each model performs across the same resampling structure. This helps avoid choosing a model because it performed well on one lucky split.

380.title: 6. Medical example: diabetes prediction
380.content: Suppose we want to predict diabetes status using: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | insulin; | blood pressure. | . The outcome is: . Diabetes positive versus diabetes negative . If we use a single train/test split, the model may appear better or worse depending on the patients in the test set. With 5-fold cross-validation, the model is evaluated across five validation folds. This gives a more stable view of how the model performs internally. However, this still only tells us about performance within the same dataset. It does not prove that the model will work in another population.

381.title: 7. Cross-validation is internal validation
381.content: Cross-validation is a form of internal validation. Internal validation uses data from the same source as the development data. It estimates how the model might perform in similar patients from the same population. It does not fully answer whether the model will work in: . | another hospital; | another country; | another time period; | another laboratory system; | another ethnic group; | another healthcare setting. | . That requires external validation. A useful way to remember this is: . Cross-validation tests reuse within the same data source. External validation tests transport to a new data source.

382.title: 8. Cross-validation and data leakage
382.content: Cross-validation can still give misleading results if it is done incorrectly. A common mistake is to perform preprocessing before cross-validation. Examples include: . | imputing missing values using the full dataset; | scaling variables using the full dataset; | selecting features using the full dataset; | performing PCA using the full dataset; | filtering biomarkers using the full dataset; | tuning model settings repeatedly using validation results. | . If these steps are done before cross-validation, information from the validation folds leaks into the training process. This can make performance look better than it really is.

383.title: 9. The correct principle
383.content: The correct principle is: . Anything learned from data must be learned only from the training part of each fold. This includes: . | imputation; | scaling; | transformation; | feature selection; | model tuning; | model fitting. | . The validation fold should behave like unseen data. A better workflow is: . For each fold: 1. Split into training fold and validation fold. 2. Learn preprocessing only from the training fold. 3. Apply preprocessing to the validation fold. 4. Fit the model on the training fold. 5. Evaluate on the validation fold. Average performance across folds.

384.title: 10. Feature selection inside cross-validation
384.content: Feature selection is a major source of leakage. Suppose a dataset contains: . n = 250 patients p = 20,000 gene expression features . An incorrect workflow is: . | Use the full dataset to select the 100 features most associated with the outcome. | Run cross-validation using only those 100 features. | Report cross-validated AUC. | . This is biased because the validation folds helped choose the features. A correct workflow is: . | Split into cross-validation folds. | Within each training fold, select features. | Fit the model using features selected from the training fold only. | Evaluate on the validation fold. | Repeat for each fold. | . This is especially important in omics, imaging, and high-dimensional biomedical prediction.

385.title: 11. Cross-validation for model tuning
385.content: Many machine learning models have tuning parameters. Examples include: . | penalty strength in ridge or lasso regression; | number of trees in a random forest; | maximum tree depth in boosting; | number of neighbours in k-nearest neighbours. | . Cross-validation is often used to choose these tuning parameters. However, tuning must be handled carefully. If the same validation results are repeatedly used to choose the best model and report final performance, the reported performance may become optimistic. For strict model comparison, nested cross-validation may be needed.

386.title: 12. Nested cross-validation
386.content: Nested cross-validation separates model tuning from model evaluation. It has two layers: . Inner loop: choose tuning parameters Outer loop: estimate model performance . This avoids using the same validation data both to tune the model and to estimate final performance. Nested cross-validation is more computationally expensive, but it is useful when many models or tuning options are compared.

387.title: 13. Repeated cross-validation
387.content: Cross-validation can be repeated several times. For example: . 10-fold cross-validation repeated 5 times . This means different random fold assignments are created repeatedly. Performance is averaged across all repeats. Repeated cross-validation can reduce instability due to one particular fold assignment. It is useful when the dataset is small or when model performance varies across folds.

388.title: 14. Leave-one-out cross-validation
388.content: Leave-one-out cross-validation uses one observation as the validation set and all remaining observations as the training set. If there are n patients, the model is fitted n times. This uses nearly all data for training each time. However, it can be computationally expensive and may have high variance. It is not always the best choice for medical prediction studies.

389.title: 15. Cross-validation with repeated patient observations
389.content: Medical datasets often contain repeated observations. For example: . | multiple admissions per patient; | multiple scans per patient; | multiple visits per patient; | multiple samples per participant. | . If rows are split randomly, data from the same patient may appear in both training and validation folds. This causes leakage. The correct approach is usually to split at the patient level, not the row level. For example: . All records from Patient A should be in either training or validation, not both.

390.title: 16. Cross-validation and class imbalance
390.content: Medical outcomes are often imbalanced. For example, only 5% of patients may have the disease or event. In such cases, folds should often be stratified so that each fold has a similar outcome distribution. If one validation fold contains very few positive cases, performance estimates such as sensitivity or AUC may be unstable. Stratified cross-validation helps reduce this problem.

391.title: 17. Reporting cross-validation
391.content: When reporting cross-validation, describe: . | number of folds; | whether cross-validation was repeated; | whether folds were stratified; | whether patient-level grouping was used; | what metrics were calculated; | how preprocessing was handled; | whether feature selection occurred inside folds; | how tuning was performed. | . This is important for transparency and reproducibility.

392.title: 18. Common mistakes
392.content: 18.1 Feature selection before cross-validation . This leaks information from validation folds into model development. 18.2 Preprocessing before cross-validation . Imputation, scaling, or PCA using the full dataset can leak information. 18.3 Treating cross-validation as external validation . Cross-validation is internal validation only. 18.4 Reporting only the best fold . Performance should be summarised across all folds, not selected from the best fold. 18.5 Ignoring patient clustering . Repeated observations from the same patient must not be split across training and validation folds.

393.title: 19. Summary
393.content: Cross-validation is a valuable internal validation method in medical machine learning. It is more stable than a single train/test split and can be used for model comparison and tuning. However, cross-validation must be done carefully. Preprocessing, imputation, scaling, feature selection, and tuning should be included inside the resampling process. Otherwise, performance estimates may be too optimistic. Cross-validation helps estimate performance in similar patients from the same source population, but it does not replace external validation.

394.title: Revision questions
394.content: . | What is cross-validation? | Why can a single train/test split be unstable? | How does k-fold cross-validation work? | In 5-fold cross-validation, how many times is each patient used for validation? | Why is cross-validation considered internal validation? | Why does cross-validation not replace external validation? | Why should feature selection happen inside each fold? | Why can preprocessing before cross-validation cause leakage? | What is nested cross-validation? | Why might repeated cross-validation be useful? | Why is patient-level splitting important in repeated-measure datasets? | Why might stratified cross-validation be useful for rare outcomes? | What should be reported when describing cross-validation? | Why should we not report only the best fold? | How can cross-validation be used for model tuning? | .

395.title: Key terms
395.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Cross-validation | Internal validation method using repeated training and validation splits | . | Fold | One part of the data used in k-fold cross-validation | . | k-fold cross-validation | Cross-validation where the data are divided into k folds | . | Validation fold | Fold used to evaluate the model in one cross-validation iteration | . | Internal validation | Validation within the same dataset or source population | . | External validation | Validation in a separate dataset, setting, time period, or population | . | Data leakage | Use of information that should not be available during model training or validation | . | Feature-selection leakage | Leakage caused by selecting predictors before validation | . | Nested cross-validation | Cross-validation with separate inner tuning and outer evaluation loops | . | Repeated cross-validation | Cross-validation repeated with different fold assignments | . | Stratified cross-validation | Cross-validation that preserves outcome proportions across folds | . | Patient-level splitting | Splitting data so records from the same patient do not appear in both training and validation | .

396.title: References
396.content: . | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. Springer. Official website: https://www.statlearning.com/ . | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd ed.). Springer. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating (2nd ed.). Springer. | Harrell, F. E. (2015). Regression Modeling Strategies. Springer. | Kuhn, M., & Johnson, K. (2013). Applied Predictive Modeling. Springer. | .

397.title: Key takeaway
397.content: Cross-validation is useful because it gives a more stable internal estimate of model performance than one random split. However, it is only trustworthy when the full modelling workflow, including preprocessing and feature selection, is kept inside the validation process.

400.title: Opening scene
400.content: Scene: The train/test split courtroom Curious Learner: “I split my data once into training and test sets. Is that enough?” . Prof Stat: “It is a good start, but one split can be unstable.” . Captain Overfit: “Yes, choose the lucky split where I look best!” . Dr Clinic: “I need a performance estimate I can trust, not one that depends on a lucky random split.” .

401.title: Today’s big question
401.content: Why do we use cross-validation in medical machine learning? . Cross-validation is a method for estimating how well a model may perform on unseen data. Instead of using only one training/test split, cross-validation repeatedly splits the data into training and validation parts.

402.title: Intuition first
402.content: Imagine testing a student using only one question. The result may depend too much on that one question. A better assessment uses several questions. Cross-validation works similarly. It tests the model across several different splits of the data. Cross-validation gives a more stable estimate of model performance than relying on one random split.

403.title: What is k-fold cross-validation?
403.content: In k-fold cross-validation, the data are divided into k parts called folds. For example, in 5-fold cross-validation: . | split the data into 5 folds; | train the model on 4 folds; | validate it on the remaining fold; | repeat until each fold has been used for validation; | average the performance. | . Fold 1 → validation Fold 2 → validation Fold 3 → validation Fold 4 → validation Fold 5 → validation . Each patient is used for validation once.

404.title: Mini conversation
404.content: Curious Learner: “So cross-validation gives me many test sets?” . Prof Stat: “Not exactly. It gives repeated internal validation splits within the development data.” . Dr Clinic: “So it is still not external validation?” . Prof Stat: “Correct. Cross-validation is internal validation, not proof that the model works elsewhere.” .

405.title: Why cross-validation is useful
405.content: Cross-validation is useful when: . | the dataset is not very large; | one train/test split may be unstable; | we want to compare models; | we want to tune model settings; | we want a better internal estimate of performance. | . It helps us avoid judging a model based on one lucky or unlucky split.

406.title: Medical example
406.content: Suppose we are developing a diabetes prediction model. A single train/test split may give: . AUC = 0.81 . But another split may give: . AUC = 0.74 . This variation happens because each split contains a different set of patients. Cross-validation gives performance across multiple splits, which is more informative.

407.title: Common mistake
407.content: Common mistake: doing feature selection before cross-validation . If you select predictors using the full dataset before cross-validation, information from the validation folds has already influenced model development. This creates data leakage. Feature selection, scaling, imputation, and preprocessing should happen inside the cross-validation process.

408.title: Clinical reality check
408.content: Clinical reality check . Cross-validation can improve internal validation, but it does not replace external validation. A model may perform well during cross-validation and still fail in a different hospital, country, time period, or patient group.

409.title: Key takeaway
409.content: Cross-validation helps estimate model performance more reliably than a single split. But it must be done carefully. The full modelling workflow, including preprocessing and feature selection, should be repeated inside each fold.

410.title: Reflection question
410.content: Why is it unsafe to select predictors using the full dataset before cross-validation? . Show suggested answer Because the validation folds influence predictor selection before the model is evaluated. This means the validation data are no longer truly unseen, and performance estimates may be too optimistic.

413.title: Learning aim
413.content: This exercise helps you understand why cross-validation is used in medical machine learning and how it differs from a single train/test split. By the end of this exercise, you should be able to explain: . | why a single train/test split can be unstable; | how k-fold cross-validation works; | why preprocessing must happen inside the validation process; | why cross-validation is internal validation, not external validation. | .

414.title: Question 1: Concept check
414.content: Explain in your own words why a single train/test split may give an unstable estimate of model performance. Show suggested answer A single train/test split depends on which patients happen to be placed in the training set and which patients happen to be placed in the test set. If the test set is unusually easy, performance may look too good. If the test set is unusually difficult, performance may look too poor. This is especially important when the dataset is small or the outcome is uncommon. Cross-validation reduces this problem by evaluating the model across multiple splits.

415.title: Question 2: Medical interpretation
415.content: You develop a diabetes prediction model. One train/test split gives: . AUC = 0.82 . Another train/test split gives: . AUC = 0.74 . What does this suggest? . Show suggested answer This suggests that the performance estimate may be sensitive to the particular split of the data. The difference could be due to: - small sample size; - different outcome distributions in the test sets; - different patient characteristics in each split; - model instability. Cross-validation may provide a more stable internal estimate of performance by evaluating the model across several splits.

416.title: Question 3: True or false
416.content: Feature selection can be performed on the full dataset before cross-validation because cross-validation will still provide an unbiased performance estimate. Show suggested answer False. If feature selection is performed on the full dataset before cross-validation, information from the validation folds has already influenced the modelling process. This creates data leakage. Feature selection should happen inside each training fold, so that the validation fold remains genuinely unseen.

417.title: Question 4: Applied reasoning
417.content: Suppose you are using 5-fold cross-validation. How many times is each patient used for validation? . Show suggested answer Each patient is used for validation once. In 5-fold cross-validation: 1. the data are divided into five folds; 2. the model is trained on four folds; 3. the remaining fold is used for validation; 4. the process repeats until each fold has served as the validation fold.

418.title: Question 5: Short answer
418.content: Why does cross-validation not replace external validation? . Show suggested answer Cross-validation is a form of internal validation. It estimates model performance within the same dataset or source population. External validation tests the model in a different dataset, such as another hospital, time period, country, or patient population. A model may perform well during cross-validation but still perform poorly in a new clinical setting.

419.title: Question 6: Workflow problem
419.content: A researcher follows this workflow: . 1. Use the full dataset to select the top 10 predictors. 2. Perform 10-fold cross-validation using only those 10 predictors. 3. Report the average cross-validated AUC. What is wrong with this workflow? . Show suggested answer The problem is that predictor selection was performed before cross-validation using the full dataset. This means the validation folds influenced which predictors were selected. As a result, the validation folds are no longer truly independent, and the cross-validated AUC may be too optimistic. A better approach would be to repeat predictor selection inside each training fold.

420.title: Question 7: Clinical reasoning
420.content: A model developed for hospital readmission has good cross-validated performance. Before using it in a new hospital, what additional evaluation would you want? . Show suggested answer You would want external validation in data from the new hospital or a similar external setting. This is important because hospitals may differ in: - patient population; - disease prevalence; - coding systems; - admission and discharge practices; - available predictors; - follow-up procedures. Good internal validation does not guarantee good external performance.

421.title: Reflection
421.content: In a medical prediction study, what could go wrong if the cross-validation process is not carefully designed? . Show reflection answer Poorly designed cross-validation can lead to overly optimistic results. Problems may include: - preprocessing before splitting; - feature selection before cross-validation; - repeated measurements from the same patient appearing in both training and validation folds; - outcome information leaking into predictor construction; - tuning the model repeatedly until the best result is found. In medical machine learning, this can make a model appear useful when it may fail on real patients.

424.title: Opening scene
424.content: Scene: The optimism machine Curious Learner: “My model performs beautifully on the development data.” . Prof Stat: “That performance is probably optimistic.” . Captain Overfit: “Of course it is. I helped the model look amazing on the same data it learned from.” . Dr Clinic: “So we need to estimate how much performance is exaggerated?” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. That is where bootstrap validation can help.” .

425.title: Today’s big question
425.content: How can bootstrap validation help estimate optimism in model performance? . Bootstrap validation is an internal validation method. It repeatedly samples from the development data, fits the model, and estimates how much apparent performance may be too optimistic.

426.title: Intuition first
426.content: When a model is evaluated on the same data used to build it, it usually looks better than it really is. This is called optimism. Bootstrap validation tries to estimate that optimism. Bootstrap validation helps adjust for overly optimistic performance estimates from the development data.

427.title: What is a bootstrap sample?
427.content: A bootstrap sample is created by sampling patients from the original dataset with replacement. This means: . | some patients appear more than once; | some patients do not appear at all; | the sample has the same size as the original dataset. | . The patients not selected in a bootstrap sample are sometimes called out-of-bag observations.

428.title: Mini conversation
428.content: Curious Learner: “So bootstrap creates new fake datasets?” . Prof Stat: “Not fake, but resampled versions of the original data.” . Dr Clinic: “And we use them to understand how stable the model performance is?” . Prof Stat: “Yes. Especially how much apparent performance may be optimistic.” .

429.title: Apparent performance
429.content: Apparent performance means performance calculated on the same dataset used to fit the model. For example: . Fit model on all development data Evaluate model on the same data . This often gives an overly positive result. It is like giving a student the same exam paper they practised on.

430.title: Optimism
430.content: Optimism is the difference between performance on the data used for model development and performance on new data. Conceptually: . Optimism = apparent performance - expected new-data performance . Bootstrap validation estimates this optimism and can be used to correct the apparent performance.

431.title: Medical example
431.content: Suppose a logistic regression model for cancer recurrence has: . Apparent AUC = 0.84 . After bootstrap validation, estimated optimism is: . Optimism = 0.04 . Then optimism-corrected AUC is approximately: . 0.84 - 0.04 = 0.80 . This gives a more realistic estimate of performance.

432.title: Common mistake
432.content: Common mistake: reporting only apparent performance . If you fit a model and report performance on the same data, the result is likely too optimistic. Medical prediction models should include internal validation, such as bootstrap validation or cross-validation.

433.title: Clinical reality check
433.content: Clinical reality check . An optimism-corrected model can still fail externally. Bootstrap validation improves internal assessment, but it does not prove the model will work in another hospital or population. External validation is still needed before clinical use.

434.title: Key takeaway
434.content: Bootstrap validation is useful for estimating optimism in model performance. It is especially valuable in clinical prediction modelling because it uses the full development dataset while still assessing how much performance may be exaggerated.

435.title: Reflection question
435.content: Why is apparent performance usually too optimistic? . Show suggested answer Because the model is evaluated on the same data it used to learn. It may capture noise or dataset-specific patterns, so performance on the development data is usually better than expected performance on new patients.

437.title: Bootstrap Validation in Clinical Prediction Modelling

438.title: Learning objectives
438.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain what apparent performance means. | Explain why apparent performance is usually optimistic. | Define optimism in the context of prediction model performance. | Describe what bootstrap sampling means. | Explain how bootstrap validation estimates optimism. | Interpret optimism-corrected performance. | Understand why bootstrap validation is useful in clinical prediction modelling. | Distinguish bootstrap internal validation from external validation. | .

439.title: 1. Why this note matters
439.content: In medical prediction modelling, performance estimated in the development dataset is usually too optimistic. A model may appear to discriminate well, classify patients accurately, or produce low prediction error simply because it has already been developed using the same data. This is especially important in medical research because many datasets are small, outcomes may be rare, and the number of candidate predictors may be large. A model can easily learn dataset-specific patterns that do not apply to future patients. Bootstrap validation is a method for estimating this optimism. It is widely used in clinical prediction modelling because it can make efficient use of the development data while still assessing how much performance may be exaggerated. The practical message of this note is: . Bootstrap validation helps estimate how much a model’s apparent performance is likely to be too optimistic.

440.title: 2. Apparent performance
440.content: Apparent performance is the performance of a model when it is evaluated on the same data used to develop it. For example: . Fit the model on the full development dataset. Evaluate the model on the same full development dataset. This may give: . Apparent AUC = 0.86 . The problem is that the model has already seen the data. It may have learned real patterns, but it may also have learned random noise, sample-specific patterns, or modelling choices that were selected because they worked well in this dataset. Therefore, apparent performance is usually optimistic.

441.title: 3. Why apparent performance is optimistic
441.content: Apparent performance is optimistic because model development is not a neutral process. During development, analysts may: . | try several models; | select predictors; | transform variables; | tune hyperparameters; | choose interactions; | remove outliers; | compare thresholds; | select the best-performing model. | . Even if the final model is evaluated only once, the dataset may have influenced many modelling decisions. This means apparent performance reflects both true signal and overfitting.

442.title: 4. Optimism
442.content: Optimism is the amount by which apparent performance overestimates performance on new data. Conceptually: . Optimism = apparent performance - expected performance on new data . For performance measures where higher is better, such as AUC or C-index, optimism is subtracted from apparent performance. For example: . Apparent AUC = 0.86 Estimated optimism = 0.04 Optimism-corrected AUC = 0.82 . The optimism-corrected estimate is usually more realistic than the apparent estimate.

443.title: 5. What is bootstrap sampling?
443.content: Bootstrap sampling means sampling observations from the original dataset with replacement. If the original dataset contains 500 patients, a bootstrap sample also contains 500 rows. However: . | some patients may appear more than once; | some patients may not appear at all; | the sample is a resampled version of the original dataset. | . Sampling with replacement allows us to create many slightly different versions of the development dataset.

444.title: 6. Why bootstrap sampling is useful
444.content: Bootstrap sampling helps us study how the model behaves when the development data change slightly. Each bootstrap sample acts like a new version of the dataset. The model is fitted in the bootstrap sample and evaluated to estimate how much performance is inflated by fitting the model to that sample. Repeating this many times gives an estimate of average optimism.

445.title: 7. Basic bootstrap validation process
445.content: A simplified bootstrap validation process is: . | Fit the original model in the full development data. | Calculate apparent performance. | Draw a bootstrap sample. | Fit the model in the bootstrap sample. | Evaluate performance in the bootstrap sample. | Evaluate the same bootstrap-fitted model in the original data. | Estimate optimism for that bootstrap sample. | Repeat many times. | Average the optimism. | Subtract average optimism from apparent performance. | . This gives an optimism-corrected estimate.

446.title: 8. Medical example: cancer recurrence prediction
446.content: Suppose we develop a model to predict cancer recurrence within 5 years. The apparent performance is: . Apparent AUC = 0.84 . Bootstrap validation estimates: . Optimism = 0.05 . The optimism-corrected estimate is: . Optimism-corrected AUC = 0.84 - 0.05 = 0.79 . This suggests that some of the apparent performance was due to overfitting. The model may still be useful, but the corrected estimate is more cautious.

447.title: 9. Bootstrap validation versus train/test split
447.content: A train/test split holds out part of the dataset for evaluation. Bootstrap validation uses resampling and can allow the full dataset to contribute to model development. This can be attractive in clinical prediction modelling because sample sizes are often limited. For example, if there are only 200 patients and 40 events, holding out 20% of the data may leave too little data for model development and too few events for stable evaluation. Bootstrap validation can be more efficient in such situations.

448.title: 10. Bootstrap validation versus cross-validation
448.content: Both bootstrap validation and cross-validation are internal validation methods. Cross-validation repeatedly divides the dataset into training and validation folds. Bootstrap validation repeatedly samples with replacement and estimates optimism. In clinical prediction modelling, bootstrap validation is often preferred for estimating optimism in a final model, especially when the sample size is limited. Cross-validation is often used for model comparison and tuning. Both methods must include the full modelling process to avoid biased results.

449.title: 11. Bootstrap validation and the full modelling process
449.content: Bootstrap validation should include all major steps in model development. If the original modelling process involved: . | feature selection; | transformation selection; | tuning; | imputation; | variable filtering; | model comparison; | . then these steps should be repeated inside the bootstrap process. If not, the optimism estimate may be too small. The bootstrap should reflect the real modelling process, not only the final fitted model.

450.title: 12. Bootstrap validation and calibration
450.content: Bootstrap validation can assess more than discrimination. It can also assess: . | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | calibration plots; | C-index; | overall performance. | . This is important because a model may rank patients well but produce risk estimates that are too extreme. A calibration slope less than 1 often suggests overfitting.

451.title: 13. Calibration slope and shrinkage
451.content: When a model is overfitted, predictions may be too extreme. High-risk patients may be predicted too high. Low-risk patients may be predicted too low. This often produces a calibration slope less than 1. Bootstrap validation can help estimate the degree of overfitting and may support shrinkage or recalibration.

452.title: 14. How many bootstrap samples?
452.content: There is no single universal number. Common choices include: . 100 bootstrap samples 200 bootstrap samples 500 bootstrap samples 1000 bootstrap samples . More samples usually give more stable estimates but require more computation. For serious modelling work, several hundred bootstrap samples are commonly used.

453.title: 15. Bootstrap validation is internal validation
453.content: Bootstrap validation uses the development dataset. Therefore, it remains internal validation. It gives information about expected performance in similar patients from the same source population. It does not prove that the model will work in another hospital, country, laboratory system, or time period. External validation is still needed before claiming broad generalisability.

454.title: 16. What bootstrap validation does not solve
454.content: Bootstrap validation does not fix: . | poor outcome definition; | poor predictor quality; | data leakage; | missing important predictors; | poor measurement quality; | unrepresentative data; | lack of external validation; | lack of clinical usefulness. | . If the model is developed using future information or leakage, bootstrap validation may still give misleading results.

455.title: 17. Common mistakes
455.content: 17.1 Reporting only apparent performance . This can seriously exaggerate model performance. 17.2 Calling bootstrap validation external validation . Bootstrap validation is internal validation. 17.3 Not repeating feature selection . Feature selection should be included inside the bootstrap process. 17.4 Ignoring calibration . Optimism should be considered for calibration and overall error, not only AUC. 17.5 Using too few bootstrap samples . Very few bootstrap samples may give unstable estimates.

456.title: 18. Reporting bootstrap validation
456.content: A clear report should describe: . | number of bootstrap samples; | performance measures evaluated; | apparent performance; | estimated optimism; | optimism-corrected performance; | whether feature selection was repeated; | whether tuning was repeated; | calibration assessment; | limitations. | . Transparent reporting is important because bootstrap validation can be misunderstood if the workflow is unclear.

457.title: 19. Worked example: hospital mortality model
457.content: Suppose we develop a model to predict 30-day hospital mortality. The apparent AUC is: . 0.88 . Bootstrap validation estimates optimism as: . 0.06 . The optimism-corrected AUC is: . 0.82 . This suggests that the model is likely less impressive than the apparent performance suggested. If the calibration slope is also low, the model may be overfitted and may require shrinkage or recalibration before further testing.

458.title: 20. Summary
458.content: Bootstrap validation is a practical internal validation method for clinical prediction models. It estimates how much apparent performance may be exaggerated due to overfitting. It is particularly useful when sample size is limited and when holding out a test set would be inefficient. However, bootstrap validation is not external validation. It should be used as part of responsible model development, not as final proof of clinical usefulness.

459.title: Revision questions
459.content: . | What is apparent performance? | Why is apparent performance usually optimistic? | What is optimism? | What does sampling with replacement mean? | What is a bootstrap sample? | How does bootstrap validation estimate optimism? | How is optimism-corrected AUC calculated? | Why can bootstrap validation be useful in small clinical datasets? | Why should feature selection be repeated inside bootstrap validation? | Why is bootstrap validation internal validation? | Why does bootstrap validation not replace external validation? | How can bootstrap validation be used to assess calibration? | What does a calibration slope less than 1 suggest? | What should be reported in a bootstrap validation study? | What problems does bootstrap validation not solve? | .

460.title: Key terms
460.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Apparent performance | Performance measured on the development data used to fit the model | . | Optimism | Amount by which apparent performance overestimates expected new-data performance | . | Bootstrap sample | Sample drawn from the original data with replacement | . | Sampling with replacement | Sampling where observations can be selected more than once | . | Optimism-corrected performance | Apparent performance adjusted by estimated optimism | . | Internal validation | Validation using the same data source as model development | . | External validation | Validation using independent data from another setting, time, or population | . | Calibration slope | Measure of whether predictions are too extreme or not extreme enough | . | Overfitting | Learning noise or dataset-specific patterns | . | Shrinkage | Reducing model coefficients or predictions to improve generalisability | .

461.title: References
461.content: . | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating (2nd ed.). Springer. | Harrell, F. E. (2015). Regression Modeling Strategies. Springer. | Riley, R. D., van der Windt, D., Croft, P., & Moons, K. G. M. (2019). Prognosis Research in Healthcare: Concepts, Methods, and Impact. Oxford University Press. | Moons, K. G. M., et al. (2015). Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis: TRIPOD. | Van Calster, B., McLernon, D. J., van Smeden, M., Wynants, L., & Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics. BMC Medicine, 17, 230. | .

462.title: Key takeaway
462.content: Bootstrap validation estimates how much a model’s apparent performance is likely to be too optimistic. It gives a more realistic internal estimate of performance, but it does not replace external validation or clinical usefulness assessment.

465.title: Learning aim
465.content: This exercise helps you understand apparent performance, optimism, and the role of bootstrap validation in clinical prediction modelling. By the end of this exercise, you should be able to explain: . | what apparent performance means; | why apparent performance is usually too optimistic; | what bootstrap sampling means; | how optimism-corrected performance is interpreted; | why bootstrap validation does not replace external validation. | .

466.title: Question 1: Concept check
466.content: What is apparent performance? . Show suggested answer Apparent performance is the performance of a model when it is evaluated on the same data used to develop or fit the model. For example: ```text Fit the model on the full development dataset. Evaluate the model on the same full development dataset. ``` This is usually optimistic because the model has already seen the data.

467.title: Question 2: Optimism
467.content: A model has: . Apparent AUC = 0.86 Estimated optimism = 0.05 . What is the optimism-corrected AUC? . Show suggested answer The optimism-corrected AUC is: ```text 0.86 - 0.05 = 0.81 ``` So the optimism-corrected AUC is approximately: ```text 0.81 ``` .

468.title: Question 3: Interpretation
468.content: Why is apparent performance usually too optimistic? . Show suggested answer Apparent performance is usually too optimistic because the model is evaluated on the same data it used to learn. The model may capture: - real signal; - random noise; - dataset-specific patterns; - accidental associations. These patterns may not generalise to new patients.

469.title: Question 4: Bootstrap sample
469.content: What does it mean to sample with replacement? . Show suggested answer Sampling with replacement means that after a patient is selected, that patient is placed back into the pool before the next selection. As a result: - some patients may appear more than once in a bootstrap sample; - some patients may not appear at all; - the bootstrap sample usually has the same size as the original dataset.

470.title: Question 5: Clinical interpretation
470.content: A cancer recurrence prediction model has: . Apparent C-index = 0.78 Optimism-corrected C-index = 0.70 . What does this suggest? . Show suggested answer This suggests that the apparent performance was overly optimistic. The model performed well on the development data, but bootstrap validation estimated that some of this performance may not generalise to new patients. The optimism-corrected C-index of 0.70 is a more realistic internal estimate than 0.78.

471.title: Question 6: External validation
471.content: A model has good optimism-corrected performance after bootstrap validation. Is it ready for clinical use? . Show suggested answer Not necessarily. Bootstrap validation is internal validation. It estimates performance within the development dataset or source population. Before clinical use, the model would still need: - external validation; - calibration assessment; - clinical usefulness evaluation; - assessment of predictor availability; - subgroup and fairness checks; - transparent reporting.

472.title: Question 7: Method choice
472.content: Why might bootstrap validation be attractive in studies with limited sample size? . Show suggested answer Bootstrap validation can be attractive because it allows the full dataset to be used for model development while still estimating optimism. Unlike a simple train/test split, it does not permanently hold out a large portion of the data. This can be useful when clinical datasets are small.

473.title: Reflection
473.content: Why is it risky to report only apparent performance in a medical prediction study? . Show reflection answer It is risky because apparent performance may make the model look better than it really is. If a model is overfitted, it may appear accurate in the development data but fail on new patients. In clinical settings, this can lead to unsafe decisions, poor risk communication, and misleading claims about model usefulness.

475.title: Calibration and Brier Score in Medical Prediction Models

476.title: Learning objectives
476.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain what calibration means in clinical prediction modelling. | Distinguish calibration from discrimination. | Interpret calibration plots. | Explain overestimation and underestimation of risk. | Describe calibration intercept and calibration slope. | Explain what the Brier score measures. | Understand why calibration is important for clinical decisions. | Recognise why a model with high AUC may still be clinically unreliable. | .

477.title: 1. Why this note matters
477.content: Many medical prediction models produce predicted probabilities. For example: . Predicted diabetes risk = 40% Predicted cancer recurrence risk = 25% Predicted hospital readmission risk = 18% . These probabilities are useful only if they are meaningful. If a model says that a group of patients has a 40% risk, then approximately 40% of those patients should experience the outcome. This is the idea of calibration. The practical message of this note is: . A medical prediction model should not only rank patients correctly. It should also give predicted risks that agree with observed risks.

478.title: 2. Discrimination versus calibration
478.content: Discrimination and calibration are different. Discrimination asks: . Can the model separate patients with the outcome from patients without the outcome? . Calibration asks: . Do the predicted probabilities match observed risks? . AUC measures discrimination. Calibration assesses whether predicted risks are numerically reliable. A model can have good discrimination but poor calibration.

479.title: 3. Example: good AUC but poor calibration
479.content: Suppose a diabetes model correctly gives higher predicted risks to most patients with diabetes than to patients without diabetes. This may produce a good AUC. However, suppose the model predicts: . Average predicted risk = 60% Observed risk = 30% . The model ranks patients well, but it overestimates absolute risk. This is poor calibration. In clinical practice, this matters because a patient may be told they are at much higher risk than they really are.

480.title: 4. What does calibration mean?
480.content: Calibration asks: . When the model predicts x% risk, is the observed risk close to x%? . For example: . | among patients predicted around 10% risk, about 10% should have the outcome; | among patients predicted around 30% risk, about 30% should have the outcome; | among patients predicted around 70% risk, about 70% should have the outcome. | . If this is approximately true across the risk range, the model is well calibrated.

481.title: 5. Why calibration matters in medicine
481.content: Calibration matters because clinical decisions often depend on absolute risk. For example: . Offer treatment if predicted risk is at least 20%. If the model overestimates risk, patients may receive unnecessary treatment. If the model underestimates risk, high-risk patients may not receive needed treatment. Poor calibration can affect: . | treatment decisions; | screening decisions; | referral decisions; | follow-up intensity; | patient counselling; | shared decision-making; | resource allocation. | .

482.title: 6. Calibration plot
482.content: A calibration plot compares predicted risk with observed risk. Usually: . x-axis: predicted risk y-axis: observed risk . A perfectly calibrated model would follow the diagonal line. Interpretation: . | points close to the diagonal suggest good calibration; | points above the diagonal suggest the model is underestimating risk; | points below the diagonal suggest the model is overestimating risk. | .

483.title: 7. Points above the diagonal
483.content: If points are above the diagonal: . Observed risk > predicted risk . This means the model predicted too low. The model is underestimating risk. Example: . Predicted risk = 30% Observed risk = 50% . The model may give false reassurance.

484.title: 8. Points below the diagonal
484.content: If points are below the diagonal: . Observed risk < predicted risk . This means the model predicted too high. The model is overestimating risk. Example: . Predicted risk = 60% Observed risk = 35% . The model may cause unnecessary concern or intervention.

485.title: 9. Grouped calibration
485.content: One simple way to create a calibration plot is to group patients by predicted risk. For example, patients may be divided into ten groups based on predicted risk. For each group, we calculate: . Mean predicted risk Observed outcome proportion . These values are plotted against each other. This approach is easy to understand but can be unstable when sample size is small.

486.title: 10. Smooth calibration curves
486.content: Instead of grouped calibration, a smooth calibration curve can be fitted. This can show patterns across the range of predicted risks. Smooth curves may be more informative than grouped points, but they also require careful interpretation. In small datasets, calibration curves may be noisy.

487.title: 11. Calibration intercept
487.content: The calibration intercept measures whether predictions are systematically too high or too low. An ideal calibration intercept is: . 0 . If the intercept is greater than 0, the model may be underestimating risk overall. If the intercept is less than 0, the model may be overestimating risk overall. The exact interpretation can depend on how the calibration model is specified, but the ideal value is usually 0.

488.title: 12. Calibration slope
488.content: The calibration slope measures whether predictions are too extreme or not extreme enough. An ideal calibration slope is: . 1 . A slope less than 1 often suggests overfitting. This means: . | high risks are predicted too high; | low risks are predicted too low. | . A slope greater than 1 can suggest that predictions are not extreme enough.

489.title: 13. Overfitting and calibration slope
489.content: Overfitted models often produce predictions that are too confident. For example, the model may classify some patients as extremely high risk and others as extremely low risk. In new data, these extremes may not be justified. A calibration slope less than 1 warns that the model may be overfitted. This is common when too many predictors are used for the available sample size.

490.title: 14. Brier score
490.content: The Brier score measures the average squared difference between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes. For a binary outcome: . Brier score = mean((predicted probability - observed outcome)^2) . where: . observed outcome = 1 if event occurred observed outcome = 0 if event did not occur . Lower Brier scores are better.

491.title: 15. Simple Brier score example
491.content: Suppose a patient has: . Predicted risk = 0.80 Observed outcome = 1 . The squared error is: . (0.80 - 1)^2 = 0.04 . This is a good prediction because the event occurred and the predicted risk was high. Now suppose another patient has: . Predicted risk = 0.80 Observed outcome = 0 . The squared error is: . (0.80 - 0)^2 = 0.64 . This is a poor prediction because the model predicted high risk but the event did not occur.

492.title: 16. Interpreting the Brier score
492.content: The Brier score captures overall probability prediction error. It reflects aspects of: . | discrimination; | calibration; | prediction accuracy. | . However, the Brier score is not always intuitive by itself. It should usually be interpreted together with: . | AUC; | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | clinical context. | .

493.title: 17. Calibration in external validation
493.content: Calibration often changes when a model is applied to a new population. For example, a model developed in one hospital may overestimate risk in another hospital if the outcome is less common there. This is why external validation should assess calibration, not only AUC. A model may transport reasonably well in terms of ranking but require recalibration before use.

494.title: 18. Recalibration
494.content: Recalibration means updating the model so that predicted risks better match observed risks in a new population. This may involve adjusting: . | intercept; | slope; | baseline risk; | model coefficients. | . Recalibration should be done carefully and transparently. A model should not be silently recalibrated without reporting what was changed.

495.title: 19. Clinical example: diabetes risk
495.content: Suppose a diabetes model predicts that a group of patients has an average risk of 40%. If the observed diabetes rate is 42%, the model is well calibrated in that group. If the observed rate is 65%, the model is underestimating risk. If the observed rate is 20%, the model is overestimating risk. This matters because different decisions may be made depending on whether the predicted risk is 20%, 40%, or 65%.

496.title: 20. Clinical example: cancer recurrence
496.content: Suppose a cancer recurrence model predicts a 5-year recurrence risk of 30%. A clinician may use this estimate to discuss follow-up intensity or treatment options. If the model is poorly calibrated, the patient may receive misleading information. This could lead to overtreatment, undertreatment, unnecessary anxiety, or false reassurance.

497.title: 21. Common mistakes
497.content: 21.1 Reporting only AUC . AUC does not assess predicted probability accuracy. 21.2 Assuming high AUC means good calibration . A model can rank patients well but still overestimate or underestimate risk. 21.3 Ignoring calibration in external validation . Calibration may change across populations. 21.4 Using arbitrary risk thresholds without checking calibration . Threshold-based decisions require meaningful predicted risks. 21.5 Treating calibration as optional . For clinical prediction, calibration is usually essential.

498.title: 22. Reporting calibration
498.content: A strong report should include: . | calibration plot; | calibration intercept; | calibration slope; | Brier score; | AUC or other discrimination measure; | validation method; | explanation of clinical implications. | . Calibration should be interpreted in the context of the intended use.

499.title: 23. Summary
499.content: Calibration is central to medical prediction modelling because predicted probabilities are often used to guide decisions. A model can have good discrimination but poor calibration. This means it may rank patients correctly but give risk estimates that are too high or too low. The Brier score summarises probability prediction error, while calibration plots and calibration slope/intercept help diagnose how predicted risks behave. In clinical applications, calibration is not a technical extra. It directly affects risk communication and decision-making.

500.content: . | What is calibration? | How is calibration different from discrimination? | Why can a model have high AUC but poor calibration? | How do you interpret points above the diagonal in a calibration plot? | How do you interpret points below the diagonal? | What is the ideal calibration intercept? | What is the ideal calibration slope? | What does a calibration slope less than 1 often suggest? | What is the Brier score? | How is the Brier score calculated for binary outcomes? | Why is calibration important for treatment decisions? | Why should calibration be assessed in external validation? | What is recalibration? | Why should risk thresholds not be used without calibration assessment? | Why is AUC alone insufficient for clinical prediction? | .

501.title: Key terms
501.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted probabilities and observed risks | . | Discrimination | Ability to rank patients with the outcome above patients without the outcome | . | AUC | Measure of discrimination for binary classification | . | Calibration plot | Plot comparing predicted risk with observed risk | . | Underestimation | Predicted risk is lower than observed risk | . | Overestimation | Predicted risk is higher than observed risk | . | Calibration intercept | Measure of systematic overprediction or underprediction | . | Calibration slope | Measure of whether predictions are too extreme or not extreme enough | . | Brier score | Mean squared error of predicted probabilities | . | Recalibration | Updating model predictions to better match a new population | .

J., van Smeden, M., Wynants, L., & Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics. BMC Medicine, 17, 230. | Harrell, F. E. (2015). Regression Modeling Strategies. Springer. | Riley, R. D., van der Windt, D., Croft, P., & Moons, K. G. M. (2019). Prognosis Research in Healthcare: Concepts, Methods, and Impact. Oxford University Press. | Moons, K. G. M., et al. (2015). Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis: TRIPOD. | .

503.title: Key takeaway
503.content: A model with good AUC is not automatically clinically reliable. For medical prediction, predicted probabilities should be calibrated so that risk estimates are meaningful for patients, clinicians, and decision-making.

506.title: Opening scene
506.content: Scene: The risk prediction clinic Curious Learner: “My model has a good AUC. So the predicted risks are reliable, right?” . Ms Calibration: “Not necessarily. AUC tells you about ranking, not whether the probabilities are accurate.” . Dr Clinic: “If I tell a patient they have 40% risk, that number must mean something.” . Prof Stat: “That is why calibration matters.” .

507.title: Today’s big question
507.content: Do predicted probabilities agree with observed risks? . Calibration is about whether predicted probabilities are believable. If a model says a group of patients has 30% risk, then about 30% of those patients should experience the outcome.

508.title: Intuition first
508.content: Imagine a weather forecast says: . Chance of rain = 80% . If it rains only 20% of the time when the forecast says 80%, the forecast is poorly calibrated. Medical risk prediction works similarly. Calibration asks whether predicted risks match observed outcome frequencies.

509.title: Discrimination versus calibration
509.content: Discrimination and calibration are different. | Concept | Question | . | Discrimination | Can the model rank high-risk patients above low-risk patients? | . | Calibration | Are the predicted probabilities close to observed risks? | . A model can have good discrimination but poor calibration.

510.title: Mini conversation
510.content: Curious Learner: “So high AUC does not guarantee good calibration?” . Ms Calibration: “Exactly. A model may rank patients correctly but still overestimate or underestimate risk.” . Dr Clinic: “And if risk estimates are wrong, decisions may be wrong.” .

511.title: Calibration plot
511.content: A calibration plot compares: . Predicted risk . against: . Observed risk . A well-calibrated model should have points close to the diagonal line. If points are above the diagonal, observed risk is higher than predicted risk. The model may be underestimating risk. If points are below the diagonal, observed risk is lower than predicted risk. The model may be overestimating risk.

512.title: Medical example
512.content: Suppose a diabetes model predicts that a group of patients has average risk: . Predicted risk = 40% . If the observed diabetes rate in that group is close to 40%, calibration is good. If the observed rate is 65%, the model is underestimating risk. If the observed rate is 20%, the model is overestimating risk.

513.title: Brier score
513.content: The Brier score measures the average squared difference between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes. For binary outcomes: . Brier score = mean((predicted probability - observed outcome)^2) . where observed outcome is: . 1 = event occurred 0 = event did not occur . Lower Brier scores are better.

514.title: Common mistake
514.content: Common mistake: reporting AUC but ignoring calibration . AUC is useful, but it does not tell us whether predicted probabilities are correct. For clinical decision-making, calibration is essential because decisions are often based on absolute risk.

515.title: Clinical reality check
515.content: Clinical reality check . If a model overestimates risk, patients may receive unnecessary testing or treatment. If a model underestimates risk, high-risk patients may be missed. Poor calibration can affect patient communication, clinical decisions, and resource use.

516.title: Key takeaway
516.content: Calibration is central to clinical prediction modelling. A model should not only rank patients well. It should also give predicted risks that match observed risks.

517.title: Reflection question
517.content: Why might a model with high AUC still be unsafe for clinical risk communication? . Show suggested answer Because AUC measures ranking ability, not whether predicted probabilities are accurate. A high-AUC model may still overestimate or underestimate absolute risk, leading to misleading communication or poor decisions.

520.title: Learning aim
520.content: This exercise helps you understand calibration, predicted probabilities, and the Brier score. By the end of this exercise, you should be able to explain: . | what calibration measures; | why AUC does not assess calibration; | how to interpret a calibration plot; | what the Brier score measures; | why calibration matters for clinical risk communication. | .

521.title: Question 1: Concept check
521.content: What does calibration measure? . Show suggested answer Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities agree with observed risks. For example, if a group of patients has an average predicted risk of 30%, then approximately 30% of those patients should experience the outcome if the model is well calibrated.

522.title: Question 2: Discrimination versus calibration
522.content: A model has a high AUC but poor calibration. What does this mean? . Show suggested answer This means the model may rank patients well but give unreliable predicted probabilities. A high AUC means the model can often assign higher risk to patients with the outcome than to patients without the outcome. Poor calibration means the actual risk estimates may be too high or too low.

523.title: Question 3: Calibration plot
523.content: In a calibration plot, points are mostly above the diagonal line. What does this suggest? . Show suggested answer If points are above the diagonal line, observed risk is higher than predicted risk. This suggests that the model may be underestimating risk. For example, if the model predicts 30% risk but observed risk is 50%, the model is predicting too low.

524.title: Question 4: Calibration plot
524.content: In a calibration plot, points are mostly below the diagonal line. What does this suggest? . Show suggested answer If points are below the diagonal line, observed risk is lower than predicted risk. This suggests that the model may be overestimating risk. For example, if the model predicts 60% risk but observed risk is 35%, the model is predicting too high.

525.title: Question 5: Brier score
525.content: For a binary outcome, what values are used for the observed outcome in the Brier score? . Show suggested answer The observed outcome is usually coded as: ```text 1 = event occurred 0 = event did not occur ``` The Brier score is the mean squared difference between the predicted probability and the observed outcome.

526.title: Question 6: Simple calculation
526.content: A patient has: . Predicted risk = 0.80 Observed outcome = 1 . What is the squared error contribution to the Brier score? . Show suggested answer The squared error is: ```text (0.80 - 1)^2 = (-0.20)^2 = 0.04 ``` So this patient contributes: ```text 0.04 ``` to the Brier score.

527.title: Question 7: Simple calculation
527.content: A patient has: . Predicted risk = 0.80 Observed outcome = 0 . What is the squared error contribution to the Brier score? . Show suggested answer The squared error is: ```text (0.80 - 0)^2 = 0.80^2 = 0.64 ``` So this patient contributes: ```text 0.64 ``` to the Brier score. This is much larger because the model predicted high risk but the event did not occur.

528.title: Question 8: Clinical interpretation
528.content: Why is calibration important when communicating risk to patients? . Show suggested answer Calibration is important because predicted probabilities are often used for communication and decision-making. If a model tells a patient that their risk is 40%, that number should be meaningful. Poor calibration can lead to: - unnecessary anxiety; - false reassurance; - over-treatment; - under-treatment; - poor shared decision-making.

529.title: Reflection
529.content: Why might calibration be especially important for models used to guide treatment decisions? . Show reflection answer Treatment decisions often depend on absolute risk. For example, a treatment may be recommended if predicted risk is above a certain threshold. If the model overestimates risk, patients may receive unnecessary treatment. If the model underestimates risk, high-risk patients may miss beneficial treatment. Therefore, calibration is central when predicted probabilities guide clinical action.

531.title: Decision Curve Analysis and Clinical Usefulness

532.title: Learning objectives
532.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why good model performance does not automatically imply clinical usefulness. | Define threshold probability. | Explain the purpose of decision curve analysis. | Describe treat-all, treat-none, and model-based strategies. | Explain the idea of net benefit. | Understand how false positives and false negatives are weighted in decision-making. | Recognise why decision curve analysis requires a clear clinical decision. | Interpret decision curve analysis in medical prediction studies. | .

533.title: 1. Why this note matters
533.content: A prediction model can have good statistical performance but still be clinically unhelpful. For example, a model may have: . AUC = 0.82 Good calibration Acceptable sensitivity and specificity . This sounds promising. But it does not automatically mean that using the model will improve patient care. A clinical prediction model should support a decision. Decision curve analysis helps evaluate whether using a model improves decisions compared with simpler strategies. The practical message of this note is: . A prediction model is clinically useful only if it improves decisions at relevant risk thresholds.

534.title: 2. From prediction to decision
