534.content: Many prediction models estimate risk. For example: . Predicted diabetes risk = 25% Predicted cancer recurrence risk = 18% Predicted readmission risk = 30% . But risk prediction alone is not the final goal. The question is what should be done with the prediction. Possible actions include: . | further testing; | referral; | treatment; | closer monitoring; | reassurance; | follow-up planning. | . Decision curve analysis links predicted risk to clinical action.

535.title: 3. The clinical decision
535.content: Before using decision curve analysis, define the decision clearly. Examples: . Should this patient receive further diabetes testing? . Should this patient receive additional cancer follow-up? . Should this patient be referred to a specialist? . Without a clear decision, decision curve analysis becomes difficult to interpret. A decision curve is not just a statistical plot. It is a way of asking whether model-based decisions are better than simple alternatives.

536.title: 4. Threshold probability
536.content: A threshold probability is the risk level at which action is taken. For example: . Refer for further testing if predicted risk ≥ 20% . Here, 20% is the threshold probability. The threshold reflects the point at which the expected benefit of action is judged to outweigh the potential harm, cost, or burden.

537.title: 5. Why threshold probability matters
537.content: Different clinical actions have different thresholds. A low threshold may be reasonable when: . | the action is safe; | the action is cheap; | the test is non-invasive; | missing disease is harmful. | . A high threshold may be reasonable when: . | treatment is risky; | treatment is expensive; | false positives cause harm; | the intervention is invasive. | . Therefore, the same model may be useful at some thresholds but not others.

538.title: 6. Treat-all strategy
538.content: The treat-all strategy means every patient receives the action. For example: . Test every patient. This may be reasonable when the test is cheap and safe, or when the outcome is common. However, it may lead to unnecessary testing, anxiety, cost, or overtreatment.

539.title: 7. Treat-none strategy
539.content: The treat-none strategy means no patient receives the action. For example: . Test no patients. This may be reasonable when the outcome is rare, the action is harmful, or the cost is high. However, it may miss patients who could benefit from intervention.

540.title: 8. Model-based strategy
540.content: The model-based strategy uses predicted risk. For example: . Test patients if predicted risk ≥ 20%. This strategy is useful only if it improves the balance between detecting true cases and avoiding unnecessary action. Decision curve analysis compares model-based decisions with treat-all and treat-none strategies.

541.title: 9. Net benefit
541.content: Decision curve analysis uses a measure called net benefit. Net benefit combines: . | true positives; | false positives; | threshold probability. | . It rewards correct identification of patients who should receive action and penalises unnecessary action in patients who do not need it. The exact formula is less important at this stage than the interpretation: . Higher net benefit = better decision strategy .

542.title: 10. Intuition behind net benefit
542.content: A true positive is beneficial because the model correctly identifies a patient who may need action. A false positive is harmful because the model recommends action for a patient who does not need it. The threshold probability determines how much false positives are penalised. At a low threshold, false positives are penalised less. At a high threshold, false positives are penalised more. This reflects clinical judgement about the relative harm of false positives and false negatives.

543.title: 11. Medical example: diabetes testing
543.content: Suppose a diabetes model predicts risk. The clinic considers further testing if risk is at least 20%. Possible strategies are: . Test everyone Test nobody Test only if predicted risk ≥ 20% . Decision curve analysis asks whether the model-based strategy gives higher net benefit than testing everyone or testing nobody. If it does, the model may be clinically useful at that threshold.

544.title: 12. Medical example: cancer follow-up
544.content: Suppose a model predicts cancer recurrence risk. A clinician may consider intensive follow-up if predicted recurrence risk is high. However, intensive follow-up may involve cost, anxiety, imaging, and clinic visits. Decision curve analysis can help assess whether the model improves decisions across plausible thresholds for additional follow-up.

545.title: 13. Why AUC is not enough
545.content: AUC measures discrimination. It asks: . Does the model rank patients with the outcome above patients without the outcome? . But AUC does not tell us whether using the model improves decisions. A model can have good AUC and still be clinically unhelpful if it does not improve net benefit at relevant thresholds.

546.title: 14. Why calibration is still important
546.content: Decision curve analysis uses predicted probabilities. If the model is poorly calibrated, threshold-based decisions may be misleading. For example, if a model overestimates risk, too many patients may be placed above the treatment threshold. If it underestimates risk, high-risk patients may be missed. Therefore, calibration should be assessed before interpreting decision curves.

547.title: 15. Relevant threshold range
547.content: Decision curve analysis should focus on clinically relevant thresholds. For example, if clinicians would only act between 10% and 30% predicted risk, then model usefulness outside this range may be less relevant. A decision curve should not be interpreted without asking: . Are these thresholds realistic for the clinical decision? .

548.title: 16. Interpreting a decision curve
548.content: A model is potentially useful when its net benefit is higher than both: . | treat-all; | treat-none. | . This should be true across thresholds that make clinical sense. If a model has higher net benefit only at unrealistic thresholds, it may not be useful in practice.

549.title: 17. Clinical usefulness depends on context
549.content: The same model may be useful in one setting but not another. For example, a diabetes risk model may be useful for deciding who should receive a low-cost blood test. The same model may not be sufficient for deciding who should start a medication with side effects. Clinical usefulness depends on: . | the decision; | the intervention; | harms and benefits; | disease prevalence; | patient preferences; | healthcare resources. | .

550.title: 18. Common mistakes
550.content: 18.1 Assuming high AUC means clinical usefulness . High AUC does not guarantee improved decisions. 18.2 Using decision curve analysis without a clear decision . The method requires a defined action. 18.3 Ignoring threshold relevance . The threshold range must be clinically realistic. 18.4 Ignoring calibration . Poor calibration can make threshold-based decisions unreliable. 18.5 Overclaiming clinical value . Decision curve analysis can support usefulness, but it does not prove real-world implementation benefit by itself.

551.title: 19. Reporting decision curve analysis
551.content: A clear report should describe: . | target population; | prediction model; | clinical decision; | action triggered by the model; | threshold probability range; | reason the threshold range is clinically relevant; | comparison strategies; | validation dataset; | interpretation of net benefit. | . This makes the decision curve clinically interpretable.

552.title: 20. Summary
552.content: Decision curve analysis evaluates clinical usefulness. It asks whether using a model improves decisions compared with treating everyone or treating no one. It is useful because good statistical performance does not automatically mean clinical value. A decision curve should always be interpreted in context: what decision is being made, what threshold range is realistic, what harms are involved, and whether the model has been validated.

553.title: Revision questions
553.content: . | What is decision curve analysis? | Why is AUC not enough to show clinical usefulness? | What is threshold probability? | What does the treat-all strategy mean? | What does the treat-none strategy mean? | What is a model-based decision strategy? | What is net benefit? | Why are false positives penalised in net benefit? | Why does the threshold probability matter? | Why should decision curves focus on clinically relevant thresholds? | Why is calibration important before decision curve analysis? | Why might a model with good AUC have limited clinical usefulness? | What should be reported when presenting decision curve analysis? | Why does decision curve analysis require a clear clinical decision? | How can patient preferences influence threshold probability? | .

554.title: Key terms
554.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Decision curve analysis | Method for evaluating clinical usefulness of prediction models | . | Threshold probability | Risk level at which clinical action is taken | . | Treat all | Strategy where every patient receives the action | . | Treat none | Strategy where no patient receives the action | . | Model-based strategy | Strategy where action depends on predicted risk | . | Net benefit | Measure balancing true positives and false positives according to threshold | . | Clinical usefulness | Whether a model improves decisions in practice | . | False positive | Patient incorrectly classified as needing action | . | False negative | Patient incorrectly classified as not needing action | . | Relevant threshold range | Range of risk thresholds that are realistic for the clinical decision | .

555.title: References
555.content: . | Vickers, A. J., & Elkin, E. B. (2006). Decision curve analysis: a novel method for evaluating prediction models. Medical Decision Making. | Van Calster, B., Wynants, L., Verbeek, J. F. M., et al. (2018). Reporting and interpreting decision curve analysis: a guide for investigators. European Urology. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating (2nd ed.). Springer. | Riley, R. D., van der Windt, D., Croft, P., & Moons, K. G. M. (2019). Prognosis Research in Healthcare: Concepts, Methods, and Impact. Oxford University Press. | Moons, K. G. M., et al. (2015). Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis: TRIPOD. | .

556.title: Key takeaway
556.content: Decision curve analysis connects prediction modelling to clinical action. A model is not useful simply because it has good AUC or calibration; it is useful when it improves decisions at clinically meaningful risk thresholds.

559.title: Opening scene
559.content: Scene: The model meets the decision Curious Learner: “My model has good AUC and decent calibration. Is that enough?” . Dr Clinic: “Maybe not. What decision will the model actually support?” . Prof Stat: “That is the key question. A model should improve decisions, not just metrics.” . Confusion Bug: “I thought good AUC automatically means clinical usefulness.” . Prof Stat: “Not always. That is why decision curve analysis can be useful.” .

560.title: Today’s big question
560.content: Does the model help make better clinical decisions? . Decision curve analysis is used to evaluate the clinical usefulness of a prediction model. It asks whether using the model gives more benefit than simple alternatives such as: . Treat everyone Treat no one .

561.title: Intuition first
561.content: A model is clinically useful only if it changes decisions in a helpful way. For example: . | who should receive further testing? | who should be referred? | who should receive treatment? | who should be monitored more closely? | . Good statistical performance does not automatically mean good clinical usefulness.

562.title: Threshold probability
562.content: Decision curve analysis uses the idea of a threshold probability. A threshold probability is the risk level at which action would be taken. For example: . If predicted risk ≥ 20%, refer for further testing . Here, 20% is the threshold probability. Different clinical settings may use different thresholds.

563.title: Mini conversation
563.content: Curious Learner: “Why does the threshold matter?” . Dr Clinic: “Because action depends on risk. I may act at 5% risk for a harmless test, but not for a risky treatment.” . Prof Stat: “Exactly. The threshold reflects the trade-off between false positives and false negatives.” .

564.title: Net benefit
564.content: Decision curve analysis uses a measure called net benefit. Net benefit combines: . | true positives; | false positives; | threshold probability. | . The idea is to reward correct identification of patients who need action, while penalising unnecessary action in patients who do not need it.

565.title: Medical example
565.content: Suppose a model predicts diabetes risk. A clinic may decide: . Refer for further testing if predicted risk ≥ 20% . Decision curve analysis asks: . | Is using the model better than testing everyone? | Is using the model better than testing no one? | Across which risk thresholds is the model useful? | .

566.title: Treat all versus treat none
566.content: Decision curves often compare three strategies: . | Strategy | Meaning | . | Treat all | Everyone receives the action | . | Treat none | Nobody receives the action | . | Model-based | Action depends on predicted risk | . A model is useful if it gives higher net benefit than both treat-all and treat-none across relevant thresholds.

567.title: Common mistake
567.content: Common mistake: assuming strong AUC means clinical usefulness . A model can discriminate well but still not improve clinical decisions. Clinical usefulness depends on the decision, threshold, harms, benefits, and target population.

568.title: Clinical reality check
568.content: Clinical reality check . A prediction model should support a real decision. Before using decision curve analysis, ask: . | What action will be taken? | What threshold risk would trigger action? | What are the harms of false positives? | What are the harms of false negatives? | Is the model useful across realistic thresholds? | .

569.title: Key takeaway
569.content: Decision curve analysis connects prediction modelling with clinical decision-making. It asks whether using the model improves decisions compared with simple alternatives.

570.title: Reflection question
570.content: Why might a model with good AUC and calibration still have limited clinical usefulness? . Show suggested answer Because it may not improve decisions at clinically relevant thresholds. A model must be linked to a real action, and the benefits of correct decisions must outweigh the harms of false positives and false negatives.

573.title: Learning aim
573.content: This exercise helps you understand why good statistical performance does not automatically mean clinical usefulness. By the end of this exercise, you should be able to explain: . | what decision curve analysis is used for; | what threshold probability means; | why “treat all” and “treat none” are useful comparisons; | why AUC and calibration are not enough by themselves; | how decision curve analysis connects prediction models to clinical decisions. | .

574.title: Question 1: Concept check
574.content: What is the main purpose of decision curve analysis? . Show suggested answer Decision curve analysis evaluates whether using a prediction model improves clinical decision-making compared with simple strategies such as treating everyone or treating no one. It focuses on clinical usefulness, not only statistical performance.

575.title: Question 2: Threshold probability
575.content: What is a threshold probability? . Show suggested answer A threshold probability is the risk level at which a clinical action would be taken. For example: ```text Refer for further testing if predicted risk ≥ 20% ``` Here, 20% is the threshold probability.

576.title: Question 3: Clinical example
576.content: A diabetes risk model is used to decide whether patients should receive further testing. The clinic chooses: . Refer if predicted risk ≥ 20% . What does the 20% represent? . Show suggested answer The 20% represents the threshold probability. It is the risk level at which the clinic believes the expected benefit of further testing outweighs the possible harms, cost, inconvenience, or anxiety.

577.title: Question 4: Treat all versus treat none
577.content: Why are “treat all” and “treat none” included in decision curve analysis? . Show suggested answer They are simple comparison strategies. A prediction model is clinically useful only if it gives greater net benefit than simple alternatives. The model should improve decision-making compared with: - giving the action to everyone; - giving the action to nobody.

578.title: Question 5: Interpretation
578.content: Why might a model with good AUC and calibration still have limited clinical usefulness? . Show suggested answer A model with good AUC and calibration may still not improve decisions at clinically relevant thresholds. Clinical usefulness depends on: - the action being considered; - the threshold probability; - harms of false positives; - harms of false negatives; - availability of simpler alternatives; - whether the model changes management in a helpful way.

579.title: Question 6: Screening context
579.content: For a screening test with low cost and low harm, would the threshold probability usually be low or high? . Show suggested answer The threshold probability may be relatively low. If the screening action is safe, cheap, and easy, clinicians may be willing to act even at lower predicted risk. This is especially true when missing true cases is harmful.

580.title: Question 7: Treatment context
580.content: For a treatment with serious side effects, would the threshold probability usually be low or high? . Show suggested answer The threshold probability may be higher. If treatment has serious side effects, clinicians may require stronger evidence of risk before recommending treatment. This avoids unnecessary harm in false-positive patients.

581.title: Question 8: Medical interpretation
581.content: A model has high net benefit only between threshold probabilities of 10% and 30%. What should you consider before recommending the model? . Show suggested answer You should consider whether 10% to 30% is a clinically realistic threshold range for the decision. If clinicians would only act at much higher or much lower thresholds, the model may not be useful in practice. Decision curve analysis must be interpreted in the context of the actual clinical decision.

582.title: Reflection
582.content: Think of a screening model. What are the possible harms of false positives and false negatives? . Show reflection answer False positives may lead to unnecessary testing, anxiety, cost, and possible over-treatment. False negatives may lead to missed disease, delayed diagnosis, delayed treatment, and unsafe reassurance. The relative importance of these harms depends on the disease, the test, and the clinical decision.

585.title: Opening scene
585.content: Scene: Too many predictors enter the model Curious Learner: “I have 500 predictors and only 150 patients. Can I put everything into the model?” . Captain Overfit: “Yes! More predictors, more memorisation, more chaos.” . Prof Stat: “That is exactly when we need regularisation.” . Dr Clinic: “I also need predictors that make clinical sense, not just random noise.” .

586.title: Today’s big question
586.content: How can we build prediction models when there are many predictors? . In biomedical data, we often have many possible predictors. Examples include: . | clinical variables; | lab measurements; | imaging features; | genetic markers; | methylation sites; | gene expression features. | . When the number of predictors is large, overfitting becomes a major risk.

587.title: Intuition first
587.content: Imagine trying to explain a small group of patients using hundreds of variables. The model may find patterns that are not real. It may learn noise instead of signal. Regularisation helps control model complexity. Regularisation reduces overfitting by discouraging overly complex models.

588.title: What is regularisation?
588.content: Regularisation adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. Instead of only trying to fit the data as closely as possible, the model is also encouraged to keep coefficients smaller or simpler. This can improve performance on new data.

589.title: Ridge regression
589.content: Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. It usually keeps all predictors in the model, but reduces their influence. This can be useful when many predictors are correlated. Example: . Many biomarkers are weakly related to the outcome . Ridge can stabilise the model.

590.title: Lasso regression
590.content: Lasso regression can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means it can perform variable selection. Example: . Only a subset of biomarkers may be selected . This can make the model simpler, but selection can be unstable if predictors are highly correlated.

591.title: Elastic net
591.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It can shrink coefficients and select variables. It is often useful when there are many correlated predictors, such as omics features.

592.title: Mini conversation
592.content: Curious Learner: “So lasso chooses the important predictors?” . Prof Stat: “It chooses predictors that help prediction in that dataset, but selection may not always be stable.” . Dr Clinic: “So I should not automatically treat selected predictors as causal or clinically proven?” . Prof Stat: “Exactly.” .

593.title: Feature selection versus clinical reasoning
593.content: Feature selection can be statistical, clinical, or both. Purely data-driven selection may choose variables that do not generalise. Purely clinical selection may miss useful patterns. A good modelling strategy should consider: . | clinical relevance; | predictor availability; | measurement quality; | missingness; | correlation between predictors; | sample size; | validation performance. | .

594.title: Common mistake
594.content: Common mistake: selecting features before validation . If feature selection is performed before cross-validation or train/test splitting, the validation process becomes biased. Feature selection must be included inside the resampling or validation workflow. Otherwise, performance estimates may be too optimistic.

595.title: Medical example
595.content: Suppose we use methylation data to predict an exposure or disease outcome. There may be hundreds of thousands of methylation sites. A standard logistic regression model cannot handle all of them directly with a small sample size. Regularisation methods such as lasso, ridge, or elastic net can help build more stable prediction models. But careful validation is essential.

596.title: Clinical reality check
596.content: Clinical reality check . A selected predictor is not automatically clinically important. It may be selected because it improves prediction in one dataset. Before interpreting selected features strongly, consider: . | biological plausibility; | stability across resamples; | external validation; | measurement reliability; | whether the predictor is available in practice. | .

597.title: Key takeaway
597.content: Regularisation helps reduce overfitting when there are many predictors. Ridge shrinks coefficients. Lasso can select predictors. Elastic net combines both ideas. But feature selection must be validated carefully and interpreted cautiously.

598.title: Reflection question
598.content: Why should selected features from a lasso model not automatically be interpreted as causal factors? . Show suggested answer Because lasso selects predictors that help prediction in a specific dataset. Selection may be affected by correlation, noise, sample size, and model settings. A selected feature may improve prediction without being causal.

600.title: Regularisation and Feature Selection in Biomedical Prediction

601.title: Learning objectives
601.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why many predictors increase the risk of overfitting. | Describe why regularisation is useful in medical and biomedical prediction. | Explain the basic idea of ridge regression. | Explain the basic idea of lasso regression. | Explain why elastic net can be useful for correlated predictors. | Distinguish prediction-based feature selection from causal interpretation. | Recognise how feature selection can cause data leakage. | Understand why selected features should be validated and interpreted cautiously. | .

602.title: 1. Why this note matters
602.content: Medical and biomedical datasets often contain many possible predictors. Examples include: . | age, sex, and clinical history; | blood test results; | imaging features; | genetic variants; | gene expression measurements; | DNA methylation sites; | proteomics and metabolomics markers; | electronic health record variables. | . When the number of predictors is large compared with the number of patients or outcome events, ordinary modelling can become unstable. The model may fit random noise instead of real signal. Regularisation helps control this problem. The practical message of this note is: . Regularisation can reduce overfitting in high-dimensional prediction, but feature selection must be validated and interpreted cautiously.

603.title: 2. The problem of too many predictors
603.content: Suppose we have: . 150 patients 500 candidate predictors . A model may find apparent patterns that look strong in the development data. However, many of these patterns may be accidental. This is especially important when the outcome is rare. For example: . 150 patients 30 outcome events 500 predictors . There are many more possible predictor patterns than outcome events. Without careful modelling, overfitting is likely.

604.title: 3. High-dimensional biomedical data
604.content: Biomedical datasets can be extremely high-dimensional. Examples include: . Gene expression: thousands of genes DNA methylation: hundreds of thousands of CpG sites Genetics: thousands or millions of variants Imaging: many extracted features Electronic health records: many coded variables . In these settings, standard regression models may not work well without constraints. Regularisation provides one way to handle many predictors.

605.title: 4. What is regularisation?
605.content: Regularisation adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. Instead of only fitting the data as closely as possible, the model is encouraged to be simpler or more stable. The penalty discourages overly large coefficients. This can improve performance on new data. Regularisation is useful when: . | there are many predictors; | predictors are correlated; | sample size is limited; | overfitting risk is high; | prediction stability is important. | .

606.title: 5. Ridge regression
606.content: Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. It usually keeps all predictors in the model, but reduces their influence. Ridge is useful when many predictors may each contain a small amount of information. It is also useful when predictors are correlated. For example, many biomarkers may be related to the same biological pathway. Ridge can stabilise coefficient estimates in this situation.

607.title: 6. Intuition for ridge regression
607.content: Imagine a model with many predictors. Without regularisation, some predictors may receive large coefficients because of random noise. Ridge regression reduces this by shrinking coefficients. This makes the model less sensitive to small changes in the training data. In medical prediction, this stability can be valuable.

608.title: 7. Lasso regression
608.content: Lasso regression also shrinks coefficients. However, lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can perform feature selection. Predictors with zero coefficients are effectively removed from the model. This can make the model simpler and easier to describe.

609.title: 8. Intuition for lasso regression
609.content: Lasso tries to find a smaller subset of predictors that helps prediction. For example, from 100 candidate predictors, lasso may select 12. This can be attractive when we want a more compact model. However, selected predictors should not automatically be treated as clinically important or causal. Selection can be unstable, especially when predictors are correlated.

610.title: 9. Elastic net
610.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso penalties. It can shrink coefficients and perform variable selection. Elastic net is often useful when predictors are correlated. This is common in: . | gene expression data; | methylation data; | imaging features; | related clinical measurements; | electronic health record variables. | . Elastic net can be more stable than lasso alone when groups of predictors are strongly correlated.

611.title: 10. Comparing ridge, lasso, and elastic net
611.content: | Method | Main behaviour | Feature selection? | . | Ridge | Shrinks coefficients towards zero | Usually no | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients to zero | Yes | . | Elastic net | Combines ridge and lasso behaviour | Yes, with more stability for correlated predictors | . There is no universally best method. The choice depends on the data, the prediction task, the sample size, and validation performance.

612.title: 11. Feature selection
612.content: Feature selection means choosing a subset of predictors. Feature selection may be based on: . | clinical reasoning; | previous literature; | statistical association; | prediction performance; | regularisation; | measurement quality; | availability in practice. | . In medical prediction, feature selection should not be purely mechanical. A predictor should also make sense in the intended clinical workflow.

613.title: 12. Clinical versus data-driven feature selection
613.content: A purely data-driven approach may select predictors that do not generalise. A purely clinical approach may miss useful predictive information. A sensible approach often combines: . | clinical knowledge; | biological plausibility; | data quality; | statistical modelling; | internal validation; | external validation. | . This balance is especially important in biomedical prediction, where many features may be noisy or correlated.

614.title: 13. Feature selection and data leakage
614.content: Feature selection can easily create data leakage. An incorrect workflow is: . 1. Use the full dataset to select predictors. 2. Split data into training and test sets. 3. Fit the model using selected predictors. 4. Evaluate performance on the test set. This is wrong because the test set influenced predictor selection. The test set is no longer independent. Performance may be too optimistic.

615.title: 14. Correct feature selection workflow
615.content: A safer workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test sets. 2. Select features using only the training set. 3. Fit the model using selected features. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. For cross-validation: . Feature selection must happen inside each training fold. The validation fold should not influence which features are selected.

616.title: 15. Prediction versus causation
616.content: A selected feature is not automatically causal. A lasso model may select a predictor because it helps prediction. This does not mean the predictor causes the outcome. A selected feature may be: . | biologically relevant; | correlated with a causal factor; | a proxy for another variable; | selected due to noise; | affected by measurement artefacts; | specific to one dataset. | . Prediction and causal explanation are different goals.

617.title: 16. Medical example: methylation prediction
617.content: Suppose we use DNA methylation data to predict exposure status. The dataset contains: . 300 participants 450,000 CpG sites . This is a high-dimensional problem. A standard logistic regression model cannot handle all predictors directly. Regularisation methods such as ridge, lasso, or elastic net may help build a prediction model. However, selected CpG sites should be interpreted cautiously. They may be predictive markers, not causal effects.

618.title: 17. Medical example: cancer recurrence
618.content: Suppose we predict breast cancer recurrence using: . | age; | tumour stage; | tumour grade; | receptor status; | treatment information; | biomarkers; | gene expression features. | . If the number of predictors is large and the number of recurrence events is small, overfitting is likely. Regularisation may improve stability. However, the final model still needs: . | internal validation; | calibration assessment; | external validation; | clinical usefulness evaluation. | .

619.title: 18. Choosing the penalty strength
619.content: Regularised models require a tuning parameter that controls the amount of shrinkage. If the penalty is too weak, the model may overfit. If the penalty is too strong, the model may underfit. Cross-validation is commonly used to choose the penalty strength. However, this tuning process must be included properly within the validation workflow.

620.title: 19. Stability of selected features
620.content: Selected features may change across samples. For example, one bootstrap sample may select one biomarker, while another sample selects a different but correlated biomarker. This does not necessarily mean the method is wrong. It means that feature selection can be unstable when predictors are correlated or sample size is limited. Feature stability should be assessed before making strong biological claims.

621.title: 20. Interpreting selected features
621.content: Before claiming that a selected feature is important, ask: . | Is it biologically plausible? | Is it selected consistently across resamples? | Does it improve validated prediction performance? | Is it measured reliably? | Is it available in clinical practice? | Does it validate externally? | Could it be a proxy for another variable? | . This is especially important in omics and biomarker research.

622.title: 21. Model simplicity versus performance
622.content: A simpler model is often easier to implement and explain. However, a simpler model is not automatically better. A complex model is not automatically worse. The goal is to balance: . | predictive performance; | calibration; | interpretability; | stability; | feasibility; | reproducibility; | clinical usefulness. | .

623.title: 22. Regularisation and calibration
623.content: Regularisation can reduce overfitting, which may improve calibration. However, regularised models can still be miscalibrated. Therefore, after fitting a regularised model, we should still assess: . | AUC; | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness. | . Regularisation is not a substitute for validation.

624.title: 23. Common mistakes
624.content: 23.1 Too many predictors for too few events . This increases overfitting risk. 23.2 Feature selection before validation . This causes leakage. 23.3 Treating selected predictors as causal . Prediction does not prove causation. 23.4 Ignoring correlated predictors . Correlation can make lasso selection unstable. 23.5 Reporting selected features without validation . Selected features may not generalise. 23.6 Assuming regularisation solves everything . Regularisation helps, but validation and calibration are still essential.

625.title: 24. Reporting regularised models
625.content: A clear report should describe: . | candidate predictors; | sample size; | number of outcome events; | missing data handling; | regularisation method; | tuning process; | validation method; | selected predictors, if applicable; | discrimination; | calibration; | limitations; | clinical interpretation. | . Transparency is important because regularised models can be misunderstood, especially when feature selection is involved.

626.title: 25. Summary
626.content: Regularisation is useful when there are many predictors and limited data. It reduces overfitting by shrinking coefficients and controlling model complexity. Ridge regression shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors. Lasso can shrink some coefficients to zero and perform feature selection. Elastic net combines ridge and lasso and is often useful when predictors are correlated. Feature selection should be interpreted carefully. A selected feature may improve prediction without being causal or clinically important.

627.title: Revision questions
627.content: . | Why does overfitting become more likely with many predictors? | What is regularisation? | What does ridge regression do? | Does ridge regression usually perform feature selection? | What does lasso regression do? | How does lasso perform feature selection? | What is elastic net? | Why can elastic net be useful for correlated predictors? | Why is feature selection before train/test splitting problematic? | Why must feature selection happen inside cross-validation? | Why should selected features not automatically be interpreted as causal? | Why can selected features be unstable? | Why is regularisation useful in omics prediction? | What should be checked before claiming a selected biomarker is important? | Why does regularisation not replace calibration assessment? | .

628.title: Key terms
628.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Regularisation | Method that penalises model complexity to reduce overfitting | . | Ridge regression | Regularisation method that shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors | . | Lasso regression | Regularisation method that can shrink coefficients to zero | . | Elastic net | Regularisation method combining ridge and lasso penalties | . | Feature selection | Choosing a subset of predictors for a model | . | High-dimensional data | Data with many predictors, often more predictors than observations | . | Penalty strength | Tuning parameter controlling amount of shrinkage | . | Overfitting | Learning noise or dataset-specific patterns | . | Feature-selection leakage | Bias caused by selecting features before validation | . | Stability | Whether selected features remain similar across resamples | . | Prediction | Estimating outcomes for new observations | . | Causation | Explanation of whether one factor causes another | .

629.title: References
629.content: . | Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B. | Zou, H., & Hastie, T. (2005). Regularization and variable selection via the elastic net. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B. | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction (2nd ed.). Springer. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. Springer. | Harrell, F. E. (2015). Regression Modeling Strategies. Springer. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating (2nd ed.). Springer. | .

630.title: Key takeaway
630.content: Regularisation can make prediction models more stable when there are many predictors, but it does not remove the need for careful validation. Selected features should be interpreted as prediction features first, not automatically as causal or clinically proven factors.

633.title: Learning aim
633.content: This exercise helps you understand why regularisation is useful when there are many predictors and why feature selection must be interpreted carefully. By the end of this exercise, you should be able to explain: . | why many predictors increase overfitting risk; | how ridge regression differs from lasso regression; | why elastic net can be useful for correlated predictors; | why feature selection must be included inside validation; | why selected features should not automatically be interpreted as causal. | .

634.title: Question 1: Concept check
634.content: Why does overfitting become more likely when there are many predictors and few patients? . Show suggested answer When there are many predictors compared with the number of patients, the model has many opportunities to find patterns that are due to noise rather than real signal. This can lead to excellent performance in the development data but poor performance in new patients.

635.title: Question 2: Ridge regression
635.content: What does ridge regression do to model coefficients? . Show suggested answer Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. It usually keeps all predictors in the model, but reduces their size. This can improve stability, especially when predictors are correlated.

636.title: Question 3: Lasso regression
636.content: How is lasso regression different from ridge regression? . Show suggested answer Lasso regression can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can perform variable selection. Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero but usually keeps all predictors in the model.

637.title: Question 4: Elastic net
637.content: Why might elastic net be useful for omics data? . Show suggested answer Omics data often contain many correlated predictors. Elastic net combines ridge and lasso penalties. This means it can: - shrink coefficients; - perform some feature selection; - handle correlated predictors more stably than lasso alone.

638.title: Question 5: Interpretation
638.content: Why should selected features from a lasso model not automatically be interpreted as causal factors? . Show suggested answer Lasso selects features that help prediction in a specific dataset. Selection may be affected by: - correlation between predictors; - sample size; - random noise; - tuning choices; - measurement artefacts. A selected feature may improve prediction without being causal.

639.title: Question 6: Validation
639.content: Why is it problematic to perform feature selection before train/test splitting? . Show suggested answer If feature selection is performed before train/test splitting, the test set has already influenced which features were selected. This creates data leakage. The test set is no longer truly independent, and performance estimates may be too optimistic.

640.title: Question 7: Clinical reasoning
640.content: A lasso model selects a biomarker for cancer recurrence prediction. What should you check before claiming that this biomarker is clinically important? . Show suggested answer You should check: - whether the biomarker is biologically plausible; - whether it is selected consistently across resamples; - whether it improves validated prediction performance; - whether it is available and measurable in practice; - whether the finding is externally validated; - whether it may simply be correlated with another important predictor.

641.title: Question 8: Many predictors
641.content: You have: . 100 patients 20,000 gene expression features . Why would ordinary logistic regression be problematic? . Show suggested answer Ordinary logistic regression would be problematic because the number of predictors is far larger than the number of patients. This can lead to unstable estimates, overfitting, and failure to generalise. Regularisation or dimension reduction would usually be needed.

642.title: Question 9: Prediction versus explanation
642.content: A regularised model selects a group of methylation sites that predict exposure status. Does this prove that those methylation sites are caused by the exposure? . Show suggested answer No. The model identifies features that help prediction. It does not automatically establish causality. The selected methylation sites may be associated with exposure, correlated with other features, affected by confounding, or selected due to dataset-specific patterns.

643.title: Reflection
643.content: In a biomedical prediction model, how would you balance statistical feature selection with clinical or biological reasoning? . Show reflection answer A strong modelling strategy should combine statistical performance with subject-matter reasoning. Statistical feature selection can help identify predictors that improve prediction, but clinical and biological reasoning is needed to assess whether selected features are meaningful, measurable, stable, and practical. Validation is essential before making strong claims about selected features.

646.title: Learning objectives
646.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why ordinary regression can overfit when there are many predictors or limited outcome events. | Describe the idea of coefficient shrinkage. | Explain how ridge regression differs from ordinary logistic regression. | Understand why ridge regression usually keeps all predictors in the model. | Explain why ridge regression is useful when predictors are correlated. | Describe the role of the tuning parameter lambda. | Understand why ridge regression still requires validation and calibration assessment. | Recognise when ridge regression may be preferable to lasso regression. | .

647.title: 1. Why this note matters
647.content: In medical prediction modelling, ordinary regression can become unstable when there are too many predictors for the available sample size or number of outcome events. This is common in clinical and biomedical datasets. For example, we may have: . 200 patients 35 outcome events 25 candidate predictors . Ordinary logistic regression may fit the development data well, but some coefficients may be too large, unstable, or strongly affected by random noise. Ridge regression helps by shrinking coefficients towards zero. The practical message of this note is: . Ridge regression improves stability by reducing overly large coefficients, especially when predictors are correlated or the model is at risk of overfitting.

648.title: 2. Ordinary regression and instability
648.content: Ordinary logistic regression estimates coefficients that maximise the fit to the data. This works well when: . | the sample size is large; | the number of predictors is modest; | predictors are not highly correlated; | there are enough outcome events; | the relationships are not too noisy. | . However, medical datasets often violate these conditions. Clinical prediction studies may have: . | small sample sizes; | rare outcomes; | many candidate predictors; | correlated clinical measurements; | noisy biomarker data; | missing values; | site-specific patterns. | . In such settings, ordinary regression may overfit.

649.title: 3. What overfitting looks like
649.content: Overfitting means the model learns patterns that are too specific to the development dataset. An overfitted model may show: . High training performance Lower validation performance Poor external performance . For example: . Training AUC = 0.90 Test AUC = 0.72 External validation AUC = 0.64 . This suggests that the model learned some unstable patterns that did not generalise. Ridge regression tries to reduce this instability.

650.title: 4. What ridge regression does
650.content: Ridge regression adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. Ordinary regression says: . Fit the data as well as possible. Ridge regression says: . Fit the data well, but avoid unnecessarily large coefficients. The penalty discourages large coefficients. As a result, ridge regression produces a model that is usually less extreme and more stable.

651.title: 5. Coefficient shrinkage
651.content: Shrinkage means pulling coefficients towards zero. For example, ordinary logistic regression may estimate: . glucose coefficient = 1.20 BMI coefficient = 0.80 age coefficient = 0.45 . Ridge regression may shrink these to: . glucose coefficient = 0.95 BMI coefficient = 0.62 age coefficient = 0.33 . The predictors are still in the model, but their effects are less extreme. This can improve performance in new patients.

652.title: 6. Why shrinkage helps
652.content: Large coefficients can appear because the model is fitting random variation in the training data. This is especially likely when: . | the sample size is small; | predictors are correlated; | there are many predictors; | outcome events are limited. | . By shrinking coefficients, ridge regression reduces the model’s sensitivity to random noise. It may fit the training data slightly less perfectly, but it can perform better on new data. This is an example of the bias-variance trade-off.

653.title: 7. Bias-variance trade-off
653.content: Ridge regression introduces some bias by shrinking coefficients. However, it reduces variance. This means the model becomes less sensitive to small changes in the training data. The trade-off is: . slightly more bias less variance better generalisation . In prediction, this can be a good trade-off. A model that fits the training data perfectly is not useful if it fails on new patients.

654.title: 8. Ridge usually keeps all predictors
654.content: Ridge regression usually does not set coefficients exactly to zero. This means all predictors usually remain in the model. For example, if the original model includes 20 predictors, ridge regression will usually keep all 20, but shrink their coefficients. This is different from lasso regression, which can remove predictors by setting their coefficients to zero. Therefore, ridge is usually not considered a feature selection method.

655.title: 9. Why ridge is useful with correlated predictors
655.content: Medical predictors are often correlated. Examples include: . | BMI and waist circumference; | systolic and diastolic blood pressure; | tumour size and tumour stage; | related blood biomarkers; | gene expression features from the same biological pathway. | . When predictors are correlated, ordinary regression coefficients can become unstable. One predictor may receive a large positive coefficient, while a correlated predictor receives a small or even opposite coefficient. Ridge regression stabilises this by shrinking coefficients. It allows correlated predictors to share information more smoothly.

656.title: 10. Medical example: diabetes prediction
656.content: Suppose we build a diabetes prediction model using: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | insulin; | blood pressure; | diabetes pedigree function. | . Some of these predictors may be correlated. For example, BMI and insulin resistance-related measures may contain overlapping information. Ordinary logistic regression may estimate unstable coefficients if the sample size is limited. Ridge regression can produce a more stable prediction model. However, the ridge model still needs validation.

657.title: 11. Medical example: cancer prognosis
657.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using: . | age; | tumour size; | tumour stage; | tumour grade; | receptor status; | treatment; | biomarkers. | . Many predictors may be related. For example, tumour size and stage are connected. Ridge regression can help stabilise the model when several predictors contribute overlapping information. This may improve predictions for new patients.

658.title: 12. The tuning parameter lambda
658.content: Ridge regression uses a tuning parameter often called lambda. Lambda controls how strongly coefficients are shrunk. Small lambda → weak penalty Large lambda → strong penalty . If lambda is too small, ridge behaves more like ordinary regression and may still overfit. If lambda is too large, coefficients may be shrunk too much and the model may underfit.

659.title: 13. Choosing lambda
659.content: Lambda is usually chosen using cross-validation. A common workflow is: . 1. Define candidate lambda values. 2. Fit ridge models across those values. 3. Use cross-validation to estimate performance. 4. Choose a lambda that gives good validated performance. The test set should not be used repeatedly to choose lambda. If the test set is used for tuning, it becomes part of model development and performance may become optimistic.

660.title: 14. Ridge regression and calibration
660.content: Ridge regression can reduce overfitting, which may improve calibration. However, ridge does not guarantee good calibration. After fitting a ridge model, we should still assess: . | AUC; | calibration plot; | calibration intercept; | calibration slope; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness. | . A model can be regularised and still poorly calibrated.

661.title: 15. Ridge versus lasso
661.content: Ridge and lasso are both regularisation methods. | Method | Main behaviour | Feature selection? | . | Ridge | Shrinks coefficients | Usually no | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients to zero | Yes | . Ridge may be preferred when: . | predictors are correlated; | many predictors may contain small amounts of information; | stability is more important than sparsity; | we do not need a small predictor list. | . Lasso may be preferred when: . | we want a smaller model; | some predictors are likely irrelevant; | feature selection is a major goal. | .

662.title: 16. What ridge regression does not solve
662.content: Ridge regression does not solve all modelling problems. It does not fix: . | data leakage; | poor outcome definition; | predictors measured after the prediction time; | poor data quality; | unrepresentative samples; | lack of external validation; | lack of clinical usefulness. | . Ridge is a modelling tool, not a complete study design.

663.title: 17. Reporting ridge regression
663.content: A clear ridge regression report should describe: . | outcome definition; | prediction time point; | candidate predictors; | sample size; | number of outcome events; | missing data handling; | whether predictors were standardised; | lambda selection method; | validation method; | discrimination; | calibration; | clinical interpretation. | . Without these details, readers cannot judge whether the model is reliable.

664.title: 18. Summary
664.content: Ridge regression is a regularisation method that shrinks coefficients towards zero. It is useful when ordinary regression may overfit. It is especially helpful when predictors are correlated or when many predictors may each contribute small amounts of information. Ridge usually keeps all predictors in the model, so it is not mainly a feature selection method. Like all medical prediction models, ridge regression still requires careful validation, calibration assessment, and clinical interpretation.

665.title: Revision questions
665.content: . | What problem does ridge regression try to solve? | What is coefficient shrinkage? | Why can ordinary regression become unstable in small medical datasets? | Does ridge regression usually remove predictors? | Why is ridge useful when predictors are correlated? | What does lambda control? | What happens if lambda is too large? | How is ridge different from lasso? | Why does ridge regression still need calibration assessment? | Why does ridge regression not solve data leakage? | When might ridge be preferable to lasso? | Why should the test set not be used repeatedly to choose lambda? | .

666.title: Key terms
666.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Ridge regression | Regularised regression method that shrinks coefficients towards zero | . | Shrinkage | Pulling coefficients towards smaller values | . | Lambda | Tuning parameter controlling penalty strength | . | Overfitting | Learning noise or dataset-specific patterns rather than generalisable signal | . | Correlated predictors | Predictors that carry overlapping information | . | Bias-variance trade-off | Trade-off between model simplicity and sensitivity to the training data | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted risks and observed risks | . | Internal validation | Validation using data from the same source as the development data | . | External validation | Validation using independent data from another setting, time, or population | .

667.title: References
667.content: . | Hoerl, A. E., & Kennard, R. W. (1970). Ridge regression: biased estimation for nonorthogonal problems. Technometrics. | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. | Friedman, J., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. glmnet: Lasso and elastic-net regularized generalized linear models. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating. | .

668.title: Key takeaway
668.content: Ridge regression reduces overfitting by shrinking coefficients towards zero. It is especially useful when predictors are correlated or when many predictors may each contribute modest predictive information.

671.title: Big idea
671.content: Ridge regression is a regularisation method. It helps when an ordinary regression model becomes too unstable or too confident. Instead of allowing coefficients to become very large, ridge regression shrinks them towards zero. In medical prediction, this can help reduce overfitting and improve performance on new patients.

672.title: Scene: the model is too confident
672.content: Learner: I fitted a logistic regression model, but the coefficients look unstable. Some are very large. Prof. Stat: That can happen when the dataset is small, predictors are correlated, or there are too many predictors for the number of events. Dr. Clinic: Clinically, that worries me. A model that changes too much may not be reliable. Prof. Stat: Exactly. Ridge regression can help by shrinking the coefficients and making the model more stable.

673.title: 1. Why ordinary regression may overfit
673.content: Ordinary logistic regression tries to fit the training data as well as possible. This is useful when the data are strong enough to support the model. But in medical prediction, we often have: . | limited sample size; | rare outcomes; | many candidate predictors; | correlated clinical variables; | noisy biomarkers; | missing data; | local hospital-specific patterns. | . In this situation, ordinary regression may learn random patterns in the development data. This is overfitting.

674.title: 2. What overfitting looks like
674.content: An overfitted model often looks excellent in the training data. For example: . Training AUC = 0.91 Test AUC = 0.73 External validation AUC = 0.65 . The training result looks impressive, but the model does not generalise well. This is not just a technical problem. In medical prediction, overfitting can make a model appear clinically useful when it may fail on future patients.

675.title: 3. What ridge regression does
675.content: Ridge regression adds a penalty to the model. The model is still trying to predict the outcome, but it is also discouraged from using very large coefficients. Ordinary regression says: . Fit the training data as well as possible. Ridge regression says: . Fit the training data well, but avoid overly large coefficients. This usually makes the model more stable.

676.title: 4. Shrinkage
676.content: Shrinkage means pulling coefficients towards zero. Suppose ordinary logistic regression gives: . glucose coefficient = 1.20 BMI coefficient = 0.75 age coefficient = 0.45 . Ridge regression may shrink them to: . glucose coefficient = 0.95 BMI coefficient = 0.58 age coefficient = 0.32 . The predictors remain in the model, but their influence is reduced. This can prevent the model from becoming too extreme.

677.title: Scene: shrinking without deleting
677.content: Learner: So ridge removes weak predictors? . Prof. Stat: Usually no. Ridge shrinks coefficients, but it normally keeps all predictors in the model. Dr. Clinic: So if I include glucose, BMI, blood pressure, and age, ridge keeps them but reduces their coefficient sizes? . Prof. Stat: Correct. It is more about stabilisation than feature selection.

678.title: 5. Why ridge can help prediction
678.content: Ridge regression may fit the training data slightly less perfectly. But this can be a good thing. A model that fits training data perfectly may have learned noise. Ridge sacrifices some training fit to gain better generalisation. This is the bias-variance trade-off: . Slightly more bias Less variance Better generalisation . In prediction modelling, better generalisation is often more important than perfect training fit.

679.title: 6. Ridge and correlated predictors
679.content: Medical predictors are often correlated. Examples: . | BMI and waist circumference; | systolic and diastolic blood pressure; | tumour size and tumour stage; | multiple inflammatory biomarkers; | neighbouring methylation sites; | genes in the same biological pathway. | . When predictors are correlated, ordinary regression coefficients can become unstable. Ridge regression handles correlated predictors more smoothly by shrinking coefficients. This can make the model less sensitive to small changes in the dataset.

680.title: 7. Clinical example: diabetes prediction
680.content: Suppose we predict diabetes using: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | insulin; | blood pressure; | pedigree. | . Some predictors may overlap in the information they provide. Ordinary logistic regression may give unstable coefficients if the sample size is limited. Ridge regression can reduce coefficient instability. It may produce more reliable predicted risks for new patients.

681.title: 8. Biomedical example: biomarkers
681.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using 40 biomarkers. Many of these biomarkers may be correlated because they belong to similar pathways. Ordinary regression may struggle. Ridge regression can allow all biomarkers to contribute while controlling coefficient size. This is useful when many predictors may each carry small predictive information.

682.title: 9. Lambda
682.content: Ridge regression uses a tuning parameter called lambda. Lambda controls the strength of the penalty. Small lambda → weak shrinkage Large lambda → strong shrinkage . If lambda is too small, the model may still overfit. If lambda is too large, the model may underfit. Cross-validation is commonly used to choose lambda.

683.title: Scene: choosing lambda
683.content: Learner: Can I just try many lambda values and choose the one with the best test AUC? . Prof. Stat: Be careful. The test set should not be used repeatedly for tuning. Dr. Clinic: So tuning should happen inside training data? . Prof. Stat: Yes. Use cross-validation in the training data, then evaluate once on the test data.

684.title: 10. Ridge is not enough by itself
684.content: Ridge regression can reduce overfitting, but it does not solve everything. It does not fix: . | poor outcome definition; | predictors measured after the prediction time; | data leakage; | missing important predictors; | poor data quality; | lack of external validation; | poor calibration. | . A ridge model still needs proper validation.

685.title: 11. Ridge and calibration
685.content: Because ridge reduces overly extreme coefficients, it may improve calibration. But this is not guaranteed. After fitting a ridge model, we should still check: . | AUC; | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness. | . A model can be regularised and still poorly calibrated.

686.title: 12. When ridge may be useful
686.content: Ridge may be useful when: . | many predictors are clinically plausible; | predictors are correlated; | we do not want to remove predictors; | many predictors may each carry small signals; | stable prediction is more important than a short feature list. | . Ridge may be less useful when the goal is to produce a very small model. For feature selection, lasso may be more appropriate.

687.title: Common mistake
687.content: Do not call ridge regression a feature selection method. Ridge usually keeps predictors in the model. It shrinks coefficients rather than removing variables.

688.title: Key takeaway
688.content: Ridge regression improves stability by shrinking coefficients towards zero. It is especially useful when predictors are correlated or when many predictors may each contribute modest predictive information.

691.title: Aim
691.content: This exercise helps you understand why ridge regression is useful in medical prediction modelling, especially when ordinary regression may overfit or become unstable. You will practise thinking about: . | coefficient shrinkage; | overfitting; | correlated predictors; | the role of lambda; | why ridge is not mainly a feature selection method; | how ridge should be evaluated in clinical prediction. | .

692.title: Part A: Conceptual understanding
692.content: Question 1 . In your own words, explain what ridge regression does. Try to use the following terms in your answer: . | shrinkage; | coefficients; | overfitting; | prediction. | . Question 2 . Which of the following statements is most correct? . A. Ridge regression removes weak predictors from the model. B. Ridge regression usually keeps all predictors but shrinks their coefficients. C. Ridge regression always improves AUC. D. Ridge regression removes the need for validation. Question 3 . Why might ordinary logistic regression become unstable in this situation? . Number of patients = 180 Number of outcome events = 28 Number of candidate predictors = 24 . Write 4–6 lines. Question 4 . A student says: . Ridge regression is useful because it selects the most important clinical predictors. Explain why this statement is not fully correct.

693.title: Part B: Shrinkage intuition
693.content: Question 5 . An ordinary logistic regression model gives the following coefficients: . | Predictor | Ordinary logistic regression coefficient | . | Glucose | 1.30 | . | BMI | 0.85 | . | Age | 0.50 | . | Blood pressure | 0.30 | . After ridge regression, the coefficients become: . | Predictor | Ridge coefficient | . | Glucose | 0.95 | . | BMI | 0.62 | . | Age | 0.36 | . | Blood pressure | 0.18 | . Answer the following: . | What happened to the coefficients? | Were any predictors removed? | Why might this help prediction in new patients? | . Question 6 . Explain the difference between: . a model that fits the training data very well . and . a model that generalises well to new patients . Why is the second more important in medical prediction? .

694.title: Part C: Correlated predictors
694.content: Question 7 . A clinical dataset contains the following predictors: . BMI Waist circumference Body fat percentage Weight . These variables are strongly correlated. Why might ordinary logistic regression coefficients become unstable here? . Question 8 . Why might ridge regression be useful when predictors are correlated? . Write your answer in simple clinical language. Question 9 . A model includes 10 biomarkers from the same inflammatory pathway. Ridge regression keeps all 10 biomarkers in the model but shrinks their coefficients. Is this necessarily a problem? . Explain your answer.

695.title: Part D: Lambda and tuning
695.content: Question 10 . What does lambda control in ridge regression? . Choose the best answer: . A. The number of patients in the dataset B. The amount of coefficient shrinkage C. The outcome prevalence D. The number of missing values . Question 11 . Complete the following: . Small lambda → __________ shrinkage Large lambda → __________ shrinkage . Question 12 . What might happen if lambda is too small? . What might happen if lambda is too large? . Question 13 . Why should the final test set not be used repeatedly to choose lambda? .

696.title: Part E: Clinical prediction scenario
696.content: A team is developing a 1-year hospital readmission prediction model. Candidate predictors include: . Age Sex Number of previous admissions Length of hospital stay Number of medications Comorbidity score Blood pressure Heart rate Creatinine Haemoglobin . The team finds that several predictors are correlated. They are considering ridge regression. Question 14 . Give two reasons why ridge regression might be appropriate here. Question 15 . Give two things ridge regression will not automatically solve. Question 16 . After fitting the ridge model, the team reports only the AUC. Why is this incomplete? . What else should they assess? .

697.title: Part F: True or false
697.content: Decide whether each statement is true or false. | Statement | True / False | . | Ridge regression usually removes predictors from the model. |   | . | Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. |   | . | Ridge regression can help reduce overfitting. |   | . | Ridge regression removes the need for calibration assessment. |   | . | Ridge regression may be useful with correlated predictors. |   | . | A ridge model should still be externally validated if possible. |   | .

698.title: Part G: Short written answer
698.content: Question 17 . Write a short paragraph explaining ridge regression to a clinician. Your explanation should avoid heavy mathematics and focus on why ridge may make a prediction model more stable.

699.title: Suggested answers
699.content: Answer 1 . Ridge regression is a regularisation method that shrinks regression coefficients towards zero. This helps reduce overfitting by preventing the model from relying too strongly on patterns that may be specific to the training data. It can make predictions more stable in new patients. Answer 2 . B. Ridge regression usually keeps all predictors but shrinks their coefficients. Answer 3 . The model may be unstable because there are relatively few outcome events compared with the number of candidate predictors. With only 28 events and 24 predictors, the model may fit random patterns in the data. This can produce large or unstable coefficients and poor performance in new patients. Answer 4 . Ridge regression is not mainly a feature selection method. It usually keeps predictors in the model and shrinks their coefficients. Lasso is more commonly used when the aim is to set some coefficients to zero and select a smaller predictor set. Answer 5 . | The coefficients were shrunk towards zero. | No predictors were removed. | Smaller coefficients may reduce overfitting and make predictions less extreme, which may improve performance in new patients. | . Answer 6 . A model that fits the training data very well may have learned random noise. A model that generalises well performs well on new patients who were not used to build the model. In medical prediction, generalisation is more important because the model will be used on future patients, not only the development dataset. Answer 7 . When predictors are strongly correlated, ordinary regression may struggle to separate their individual effects. This can produce unstable coefficients, where one variable receives a large coefficient and another correlated variable receives a small or unexpected coefficient. Answer 8 . Ridge regression can shrink correlated predictors and make the model less sensitive to small changes in the dataset. This can produce more stable predictions. Answer 9 . No, not necessarily. If all 10 biomarkers contain small amounts of predictive information, ridge may allow them to contribute while controlling their coefficient sizes. However, the model may be less simple to interpret than a smaller selected model. Answer 10 . B. The amount of coefficient shrinkage. Answer 11 . Small lambda → less shrinkage Large lambda → more shrinkage . Answer 12 . If lambda is too small, the model may still overfit. If lambda is too large, the coefficients may be shrunk too strongly and the model may underfit. Answer 13 . If the final test set is used repeatedly to choose lambda, it becomes part of model development. The reported test performance may then be too optimistic. Answer 14 . Ridge may be appropriate because several predictors are correlated and because the model includes several candidate predictors. Ridge can shrink coefficients and make predictions more stable. Answer 15 . Ridge will not automatically solve data leakage, poor outcome definition, missing important predictors, poor calibration, poor data quality, or lack of external validation. Answer 16 . AUC only measures discrimination. The team should also assess calibration, Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, clinical usefulness, and external validation if possible. Answer 17 . Ridge regression is a way of making a prediction model less overconfident. It keeps the clinical predictors in the model but reduces very large coefficient values. This can help when predictors overlap or when the dataset is small, because the model becomes less sensitive to random patterns in the training data.

702.title: Learning objectives
702.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain what lasso regression does. | Describe how lasso performs feature selection. | Explain how lasso differs from ridge regression. | Understand the role of the tuning parameter lambda. | Explain why lasso-selected predictors should not automatically be interpreted as causal. | Recognise why lasso can be unstable when predictors are correlated. | Explain why lasso feature selection must happen inside validation. | Describe how lasso can be used in biomedical prediction. | .

703.title: 1. Why this note matters
703.content: Medical and biomedical datasets often contain many candidate predictors. Some predictors may be useful. Others may be weak, redundant, noisy, unavailable in practice, or not clinically meaningful. Lasso regression is attractive because it can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can produce a smaller prediction model. The practical message of this note is: . Lasso regression can perform feature selection, but selected features must be validated and interpreted cautiously.

704.title: 2. What lasso regression does
704.content: Lasso regression is a regularisation method. It adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. This penalty encourages some coefficients to become exactly zero. If a predictor has a coefficient of zero, it is effectively removed from the model. This is why lasso is often used for feature selection.

705.title: 3. Lasso and coefficient shrinkage
705.content: Like ridge regression, lasso shrinks coefficients. However, lasso can shrink some coefficients all the way to zero. For example, suppose ordinary logistic regression gives: . glucose coefficient = 1.10 BMI coefficient = 0.70 pressure coefficient = 0.15 triceps coefficient = 0.05 . Lasso may produce: . glucose coefficient = 0.90 BMI coefficient = 0.50 pressure coefficient = 0 triceps coefficient = 0 . In this example, pressure and triceps are removed from the model.

706.title: 4. Why lasso is useful
706.content: Lasso can be useful when: . | there are many candidate predictors; | only some predictors are expected to be useful; | a simpler model is desired; | the clinical measurement burden matters; | the model needs to be easier to explain; | the dataset is too small for ordinary regression with all predictors. | . In medical prediction, a smaller model may be easier to implement. For example, a clinical tool requiring five predictors may be more practical than a tool requiring fifty.

707.title: 5. Lasso as feature selection
707.content: Feature selection means choosing a subset of predictors. Lasso performs feature selection automatically by shrinking some coefficients to zero. This can help create a sparse model. A sparse model uses only a subset of available predictors. However, sparse does not automatically mean better. A smaller model still needs validation.

708.title: 6. The tuning parameter lambda
708.content: Lasso uses a tuning parameter called lambda. Lambda controls how much shrinkage is applied. Small lambda → less shrinkage, more predictors retained Large lambda → more shrinkage, fewer predictors retained . Different lambda values can lead to different selected predictors. Cross-validation is commonly used to choose lambda.

709.title: 7. Choosing lambda carefully
709.content: Lambda should be chosen using training data or internal validation. The final test set should not be used repeatedly to choose lambda. An honest workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test sets. 2. Use cross-validation within the training set to choose lambda. 3. Fit the lasso model using the chosen lambda. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. This keeps the test set honest.

710.title: 8. Lasso versus ridge
710.content: Ridge and lasso both shrink coefficients, but they behave differently. | Method | Main behaviour | Feature selection? | . | Ridge | Shrinks coefficients towards zero | Usually no | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients exactly to zero | Yes | . Ridge is often useful when many predictors each contribute small information. Lasso is often useful when we want a smaller predictor set.

711.title: 9. Lasso and correlated predictors
711.content: Lasso can be unstable when predictors are strongly correlated. For example, suppose two biomarkers measure a similar biological process. Lasso may select biomarker A and remove biomarker B. In another sample, it may select biomarker B and remove biomarker A. This does not necessarily mean one is truly more important. It may simply reflect instability due to correlation.

712.title: 10. Stability of selected predictors
712.content: Because lasso selection can be unstable, it is useful to check whether selected predictors are stable across: . | cross-validation folds; | bootstrap samples; | repeated analyses; | external datasets. | . If selected predictors change dramatically across resamples, biological interpretation should be cautious. The model may still predict reasonably well, but the selected feature list may not be reliable.

713.title: 11. Prediction versus causation
713.content: Lasso is a prediction tool. A selected predictor may be: . | causally related to the outcome; | correlated with a causal variable; | a proxy for disease severity; | a technical artefact; | selected by chance; | specific to one dataset. | . Therefore, lasso selection does not prove causality. For causal interpretation, we need additional design, assumptions, and analysis.

714.title: 12. Feature-selection leakage
714.content: Lasso can easily cause data leakage if used incorrectly. An incorrect workflow is: . 1. Run lasso on the full dataset. 2. Keep the selected predictors. 3. Perform cross-validation using only those predictors. 4. Report cross-validated performance. This is biased because the validation folds helped select predictors. The performance estimate may be too optimistic.

715.title: 13. Correct lasso validation workflow
715.content: A correct cross-validation workflow is: . For each fold: 1. Use only the training fold. 2. Select lambda using only the training fold. 3. Fit the lasso model using only the training fold. 4. Predict the validation fold. 5. Store validation performance. Feature selection must happen inside the validation process. This is especially important in biomedical data with many predictors.

716.title: 14. Lasso in omics
716.content: Lasso is often used in omics research. Examples include: . | gene expression prediction; | methylation-based prediction; | proteomic biomarker panels; | metabolomic risk prediction. | . For example: . 300 participants 20,000 gene expression features . Lasso may select a smaller set of genes. But selected genes must be validated. They should not automatically be treated as biological discoveries.

717.title: 15. Medical example: diabetes prediction
