717.content: Suppose we predict diabetes using clinical variables. Lasso may remove weak predictors and keep variables such as: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | pedigree. | . This can produce a simpler model. However, we still need to assess: . | AUC; | calibration; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness; | external validation. | . A lasso-selected model is not automatically clinically useful.

718.title: 16. Medical example: cancer biomarkers
718.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using 200 candidate biomarkers. Lasso may select 12 biomarkers. Before claiming these biomarkers are clinically important, ask: . | Are they selected consistently? | Are they biologically plausible? | Are they measurable in routine practice? | Do they validate externally? | Could they reflect batch effects? | Do they improve calibration and clinical usefulness? | .

719.title: 17. Limitations of lasso
719.content: Lasso has several limitations: . | selected features may be unstable; | correlated predictors can cause arbitrary selection; | selected features are not automatically causal; | performance may be optimistic if validation is incorrect; | a sparse model may underfit if many predictors each have small effects. | . Elastic net may be useful when correlated predictors are common.

720.title: 18. Reporting lasso regression
720.content: A clear report should describe: . | outcome definition; | candidate predictors; | preprocessing steps; | missing data handling; | lambda selection method; | whether feature selection was inside validation; | selected predictors; | discrimination; | calibration; | external validation if available; | limitations of selected feature interpretation. | .

721.title: 19. Summary
721.content: Lasso regression is a regularisation method that can perform feature selection. It creates sparse models by shrinking some coefficients to zero. This can be useful in medical prediction when we want a smaller model. However, selected predictors must be validated and interpreted carefully. Lasso is a prediction method, not automatic causal discovery.

722.title: Revision questions
722.content: . | What does lasso regression do? | How does lasso perform feature selection? | What does lambda control? | How is lasso different from ridge regression? | What is a sparse model? | Why can lasso be unstable with correlated predictors? | Why should lasso-selected features not automatically be interpreted as causal? | What is feature-selection leakage? | Why must lasso selection happen inside cross-validation? | Why is lasso useful in omics prediction? | What should be checked before claiming selected biomarkers are important? | Why does lasso still need calibration assessment? | .

723.title: Key terms
723.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Lasso regression | Regularisation method that can shrink coefficients to zero | . | Sparse model | Model using a relatively small number of predictors | . | Feature selection | Choosing predictors for a model | . | Lambda | Tuning parameter controlling shrinkage | . | Correlated predictors | Predictors that contain overlapping information | . | Feature-selection leakage | Leakage caused by selecting predictors before validation | . | Stability | Consistency of selected predictors across resamples | . | Causal interpretation | Claim that a predictor causes an outcome | . | Omics | High-dimensional molecular data such as genomics, methylation, proteomics, or metabolomics | .

724.title: References
724.content: . | Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B. | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. | Friedman, J., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. glmnet: Lasso and elastic-net regularized generalized linear models. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating. | .

725.title: Key takeaway
725.content: Lasso regression can create a smaller prediction model by shrinking some coefficients to zero. However, lasso-selected features should be interpreted as prediction features first, not automatically as causal or clinically proven predictors.

728.title: Big idea
728.content: Lasso regression is a regularisation method that can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can perform feature selection. In medical prediction, lasso can be useful when we have many candidate predictors and want a smaller model. But selected predictors should be interpreted carefully.

729.title: Scene: too many predictors
729.content: Learner: I have 60 possible predictors for a clinical prediction model. Ordinary logistic regression feels too crowded. Dr. Clinic: Also, collecting 60 predictors for every patient may not be realistic. Prof. Stat: This is where lasso can help. It can shrink some coefficients to zero and produce a smaller model. Learner: So lasso chooses the important predictors? . Prof. Stat: It chooses predictors useful for prediction in the data. That is not the same as proving clinical or causal importance.

730.title: 1. What lasso regression does
730.content: Lasso regression adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. Like ridge, it shrinks coefficients. But lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. If a coefficient becomes zero, that predictor is removed from the model. This makes lasso useful for feature selection.

731.title: 2. A simple example
731.content: Suppose ordinary logistic regression gives: . glucose coefficient = 1.10 BMI coefficient = 0.70 age coefficient = 0.40 pressure coefficient = 0.12 triceps coefficient = 0.05 . Lasso may give: . glucose coefficient = 0.90 BMI coefficient = 0.55 age coefficient = 0.30 pressure coefficient = 0 triceps coefficient = 0 . In this example, pressure and triceps are removed. The final model is smaller.

732.title: 3. Why lasso is attractive in medical prediction
732.content: Lasso can be useful when: . | there are many candidate predictors; | some predictors may be weak or redundant; | a simpler model is preferred; | clinical measurement burden matters; | the dataset is too small for ordinary regression with all predictors; | the goal is prediction, not causal explanation. | . A smaller model may be easier to implement in practice.

733.title: 4. Lasso and feature selection
733.content: Feature selection means choosing a subset of predictors. Lasso performs feature selection by setting some coefficients to zero. This can create a sparse model. A sparse model uses only a limited number of predictors. However, sparse does not automatically mean good. A smaller model can still be poorly validated, miscalibrated, or clinically unhelpful.

734.title: Scene: selected does not mean causal
734.content: Dr. Clinic: Lasso selected biomarker A. Can we say biomarker A causes the disease? . Prof. Stat: No. Lasso selection means the biomarker helped prediction in this dataset. Learner: It could be correlated with something else? . Prof. Stat: Exactly. It may be a proxy, a correlated marker, or even a dataset-specific signal.

735.title: 5. Lambda in lasso
735.content: Lasso uses a tuning parameter called lambda. Lambda controls the amount of shrinkage. Small lambda → less shrinkage, more predictors kept Large lambda → more shrinkage, fewer predictors kept . Different lambda values can produce different selected features. This is why lambda should be chosen carefully using training data or internal validation.

736.title: 6. Lasso versus ridge
736.content: Ridge and lasso are related, but they behave differently. | Method | What it does | . | Ridge | Shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients to zero and selects predictors | . Ridge is useful when many predictors may each contain useful information. Lasso is useful when we want a smaller selected predictor set.

737.title: 7. Correlated predictors
737.content: Lasso can be unstable when predictors are strongly correlated. Suppose two biomarkers carry very similar information. Lasso may select biomarker A and remove biomarker B. In another sample, it may select biomarker B and remove biomarker A. This does not necessarily mean one biomarker is truly better. It may simply reflect instability caused by correlation.

738.title: 8. Biomedical example
738.content: Suppose we have: . 300 patients 500 candidate biomarkers . Lasso may select 15 biomarkers. This looks useful. But before trusting the selected biomarkers, ask: . | Were they selected inside validation? | Are they stable across resamples? | Are they biologically plausible? | Are they measured reliably? | Do they validate externally? | Could batch effects explain the selection? | .

739.title: 9. Data leakage with lasso
739.content: Lasso can easily create leakage. An incorrect workflow is: . 1. Run lasso on the full dataset. 2. Keep selected predictors. 3. Cross-validate a model using those predictors. 4. Report cross-validated AUC. This is wrong because the validation folds influenced feature selection. The reported AUC may be too optimistic.

740.title: 10. Correct workflow
740.content: A better workflow is: . For each cross-validation fold: 1. Use only the training fold. 2. Run lasso inside the training fold. 3. Select predictors inside the training fold. 4. Fit the model inside the training fold. 5. Evaluate on the validation fold. The validation fold must remain unseen during feature selection. This is especially important in biomedical data.

741.title: Scene: the leakage monster
741.content: Leakage Monster: Select features using the full dataset first. The AUC will look amazing. Learner: That sounds tempting. Prof. Stat: And biased. The validation data would already have influenced the model. Dr. Clinic: So the model may fail on real patients? . Prof. Stat: Exactly.

742.title: 11. Lasso in clinical prediction
742.content: A lasso model should be judged by: . | discrimination; | calibration; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness; | feature stability; | external validation; | feasibility of predictor measurement. | . A lasso model with a neat feature list but poor calibration may still be clinically unsafe.

743.title: 12. When lasso may be useful
743.content: Lasso may be useful when: . | there are many predictors; | a smaller model is desired; | some predictors are likely irrelevant; | measurement burden matters; | the goal is prediction. | . Lasso may be less useful when predictors are highly correlated and feature stability is important. In those settings, elastic net may be more stable.

744.title: Common mistake
744.content: Do not treat lasso-selected predictors as automatically causal, clinically proven, or biologically validated.

745.title: Key takeaway
745.content: Lasso regression can shrink coefficients and select predictors, but feature selection must happen inside validation and selected features should be interpreted cautiously.

748.title: Aim
748.content: This exercise helps you understand lasso regression as a regularisation and feature selection method. You will practise thinking about: . | coefficient shrinkage; | feature selection; | sparse models; | the role of lambda; | instability with correlated predictors; | feature-selection leakage; | cautious interpretation of selected predictors. | .

749.title: Part A: Conceptual understanding
749.content: Question 1 . In your own words, explain what lasso regression does. Use the following words in your answer: . | shrinkage; | zero; | feature selection; | prediction. | . Question 2 . Which statement best describes lasso regression? . A. It always keeps all predictors in the model. B. It can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. C. It removes the need for cross-validation. D. It proves which predictors cause the outcome. Question 3 . What is a sparse model? . Why might a sparse model be attractive in clinical prediction? . Question 4 . A student says: . Lasso selected glucose and BMI, so these are the only clinically important predictors. Explain why this conclusion may be too strong.

750.title: Part B: Coefficient selection
750.content: Question 5 . An ordinary logistic regression model gives: . | Predictor | Ordinary coefficient | . | Glucose | 1.10 | . | BMI | 0.70 | . | Age | 0.40 | . | Blood pressure | 0.15 | . | Triceps thickness | 0.08 | . A lasso model gives: . | Predictor | Lasso coefficient | . | Glucose | 0.90 | . | BMI | 0.55 | . | Age | 0.28 | . | Blood pressure | 0 | . | Triceps thickness | 0 | . Answer: . | Which predictors were retained? | Which predictors were removed? | Why might this be useful? | Why should we still be cautious? | . Question 6 . Explain the difference between ridge and lasso in terms of feature selection.

751.title: Part C: Lambda and sparsity
751.content: Question 7 . Complete the following: . Small lambda → less shrinkage → __________ predictors retained Large lambda → more shrinkage → __________ predictors retained . Question 8 . What might happen if lambda is too large in lasso regression? . Question 9 . What might happen if lambda is too small? .

752.title: Part D: Correlated predictors
752.content: Question 10 . A dataset contains three highly correlated biomarkers: . Biomarker A Biomarker B Biomarker C . Lasso selects Biomarker A and removes B and C. Can we conclude that Biomarker A is biologically more important than B and C? . Explain your answer. Question 11 . Why can lasso be unstable when predictors are strongly correlated? . Question 12 . What method might be considered if lasso is unstable because predictors are highly correlated? .

753.title: Part E: Feature-selection leakage
753.content: Question 13 . A researcher uses this workflow: . 1. Run lasso on the full dataset. 2. Keep the selected predictors. 3. Perform 10-fold cross-validation using only those selected predictors. 4. Report the cross-validated AUC. What is wrong with this workflow? . Question 14 . Rewrite the workflow correctly. Question 15 . Why is feature-selection leakage especially dangerous in biomedical datasets with many predictors? .

754.title: Part F: Biomedical prediction scenario
754.content: A team is building a cancer recurrence prediction model using: . 300 patients 80 recurrence events 150 candidate biomarkers . They use lasso and select 12 biomarkers. Question 16 . List three questions the team should ask before trusting the selected biomarkers. Question 17 . What model performance measures should they report besides AUC? . Question 18 . Why should selected biomarkers be externally validated? .

755.title: Part G: True or false
755.content: | Statement | True / False | . | Lasso can set some coefficients exactly to zero. |   | . | Lasso-selected predictors are automatically causal. |   | . | Lasso can be used for feature selection. |   | . | Lasso can be unstable with correlated predictors. |   | . | Feature selection can be done before cross-validation without bias. |   | . | A lasso model still requires calibration assessment. |   | .

756.title: Part H: Short written answer
756.content: Question 19 . Write a short explanation of lasso regression for a clinician who wants a smaller prediction model.

757.title: Suggested answers
757.content: Answer 1 . Lasso regression is a regularisation method that shrinks coefficients. Some coefficients can be shrunk exactly to zero, which removes those predictors from the model. This means lasso can perform feature selection while building a prediction model. Answer 2 . B. It can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. Answer 3 . A sparse model uses a smaller number of predictors. It may be attractive in clinical prediction because it can be easier to measure, explain, implement, and use in practice. Answer 4 . Lasso selected those predictors because they helped prediction in that dataset and modelling workflow. This does not prove that they are the only clinically important predictors or that they cause the outcome. Answer 5 . | Retained predictors: glucose, BMI, age. | Removed predictors: blood pressure, triceps thickness. | This may create a smaller and more practical model. | We should be cautious because selected predictors may be unstable, dataset-specific, or not externally validated. | . Answer 6 . Ridge shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors. Lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero, so it can remove predictors and perform feature selection. Answer 7 . Small lambda → less shrinkage → more predictors retained Large lambda → more shrinkage → fewer predictors retained . Answer 8 . If lambda is too large, lasso may remove too many predictors and underfit the data. Answer 9 . If lambda is too small, lasso may keep too many predictors and may not sufficiently reduce overfitting. Answer 10 . No. If the biomarkers are highly correlated, lasso may choose one of them somewhat arbitrarily. Biomarker A may be useful for prediction, but this does not prove it is biologically more important. Answer 11 . When predictors are strongly correlated, several predictors may contain similar information. Lasso may choose one and remove others, but small changes in the data may change which predictor is selected. Answer 12 . Elastic net may be considered because it combines lasso and ridge behaviour and can handle correlated predictors more smoothly. Answer 13 . The full dataset was used to select predictors before cross-validation. This means the validation folds influenced feature selection. The reported cross-validated AUC may be optimistic. Answer 14 . Correct workflow: . For each cross-validation fold: 1. Use only the training fold. 2. Run lasso inside the training fold. 3. Select predictors inside the training fold. 4. Fit the model inside the training fold. 5. Evaluate on the validation fold. Answer 15 . With many predictors, some will appear related to the outcome by chance. If the full dataset is used to select features, random signals from validation data can enter model development and inflate performance. Answer 16 . They should ask: . | Were biomarkers selected inside validation? | Are selected biomarkers stable across resamples? | Are they biologically plausible? | Do they validate externally? | Could batch effects explain the selection? | Are they clinically measurable? | . Answer 17 . They should report calibration, Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, decision curve analysis if relevant, and external validation if available. Answer 18 . External validation tests whether the selected biomarkers and model performance generalise to new patients, settings, platforms, or populations. Answer 19 . Lasso regression builds a prediction model while also reducing the number of predictors. It shrinks weak coefficients to zero, so some variables are removed. This can make the model smaller and more practical, but the selected predictors still need validation before clinical use.

760.title: Big idea
760.content: Elastic net combines ridge regression and lasso regression. Ridge shrinks coefficients but usually keeps all predictors. Lasso shrinks some coefficients to zero and performs feature selection. Elastic net sits between them. It can shrink coefficients, select predictors, and behave more stably when predictors are correlated.

761.title: Scene: between ridge and lasso
761.content: Learner: Ridge keeps everything. Lasso selects predictors. What if I want something in between? . Prof. Stat: That is the motivation for elastic net. Dr. Clinic: So it can select predictors but not behave as sharply as lasso? . Prof. Stat: Exactly. It combines ridge-like shrinkage with lasso-like selection.

762.title: 1. Ridge and lasso recap
762.content: Ridge regression: . Shrinks coefficients Usually keeps all predictors Useful with correlated predictors . Lasso regression: . Shrinks coefficients Can set some coefficients to zero Useful for feature selection . Elastic net combines these ideas. It can be useful when we want both stability and some feature selection.

763.title: 2. Why elastic net exists
763.content: Lasso can be unstable when predictors are highly correlated. For example, if three biomarkers measure a similar biological process, lasso may select one and discard the others. In another dataset, it may select a different one. Elastic net can handle correlated groups more smoothly. It may keep related predictors together while still applying shrinkage.

764.title: 3. Alpha
764.content: Elastic net uses a mixing parameter often called alpha. A common interpretation is: . alpha = 0 ridge regression alpha = 1 lasso regression 0 < alpha < 1 elastic net . So alpha controls whether the model behaves more like ridge or more like lasso.

765.title: 4. Lambda
765.content: Elastic net also uses lambda. Lambda controls the overall strength of shrinkage. Small lambda → less shrinkage Large lambda → more shrinkage . So elastic net has two tuning ideas: . alpha → balance between ridge and lasso lambda → strength of regularisation . Both matter.

766.title: Scene: tuning two knobs
766.content: Learner: Ridge has lambda. Lasso has lambda. Elastic net has alpha and lambda? . Prof. Stat: Correct. Alpha controls the ridge-lasso mixture. Lambda controls how strong the penalty is. Dr. Clinic: More tuning means more responsibility. Prof. Stat: Exactly. Tuning must be done inside the validation process.

767.title: 5. Why correlated predictors matter
767.content: Biomedical predictors are often correlated. Examples: . | genes in the same pathway; | neighbouring methylation sites; | related proteins; | repeated clinical measurements; | imaging features from the same region. | . If predictors are correlated, lasso may choose one and ignore others. Elastic net can allow groups of related predictors to contribute. This may produce a more stable model.

768.title: 6. Elastic net in omics
768.content: Omics data are often high-dimensional and correlated. For example: . 300 participants 20,000 gene expression features . Many genes may move together because they belong to the same pathway. Elastic net can be useful because it can select features while also keeping grouped information more stable than lasso alone.

769.title: 7. Elastic net in clinical prediction
769.content: Elastic net may also be useful in clinical prediction when we have: . | many candidate predictors; | correlated clinical measures; | biomarker panels; | repeated measurements; | electronic health record features. | . It is especially useful when we want a balance between model simplicity and stability.

770.title: 8. Elastic net versus ridge
770.content: Elastic net may be preferred over ridge when: . | some feature selection is desired; | many predictors are likely irrelevant; | a smaller predictor set is preferred; | clinical measurement burden matters. | . Ridge may be preferred when: . | many predictors each carry small useful signals; | removing predictors is not important; | stability is the main goal. | .

771.title: 9. Elastic net versus lasso
771.content: Elastic net may be preferred over lasso when: . | predictors are correlated; | lasso feature selection is unstable; | grouped biological signals are expected; | high-dimensional omics data are used. | . Lasso may be preferred when: . | a very sparse model is desired; | predictors are not strongly correlated; | simplicity is the main goal. | .

772.title: 10. Tuning and validation
772.content: Elastic net tuning must be handled carefully. An honest workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test data. 2. Use cross-validation inside training data to choose alpha and lambda. 3. Fit the final elastic net model on training data. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test data. The test set should not be used repeatedly for tuning. If it is, test performance becomes optimistic.

773.title: Scene: do not touch the test set
773.content: Learner: I tried many alpha values and chose the one with the best test AUC. Prof. Stat: Then the test set became part of model development. Leakage Monster: My favourite workflow. Dr. Clinic: Which means we should avoid it.

774.title: 11. Interpretation
774.content: Elastic net-selected features are prediction features. They are not automatically causal. Before making biological or clinical claims, ask: . | are the features stable? | are they biologically plausible? | are they measured reliably? | are they available at prediction time? | do they validate externally? | could they reflect batch effects? | .

775.title: 12. Clinical example
775.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using: . | age; | tumour stage; | tumour grade; | treatment; | 5,000 gene expression features. | . Ridge may keep all gene features. Lasso may select a small set but be unstable. Elastic net may select related groups of genes while shrinking coefficients. This can be useful, but external validation is still needed.

776.title: Common mistake
776.content: Do not tune alpha and lambda using the final test set. Tuning should happen inside the training data or inside an inner validation loop.

777.title: Key takeaway
777.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It is especially useful when predictors are correlated and we want both shrinkage and some feature selection.

780.title: Learning objectives
780.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why elastic net combines ridge and lasso penalties. | Describe the role of the alpha parameter. | Describe the role of the lambda parameter. | Explain why elastic net is useful when predictors are correlated. | Understand how elastic net can perform shrinkage and feature selection. | Distinguish elastic net from ridge and lasso. | Explain why elastic net tuning must be done inside validation. | Describe biomedical situations where elastic net may be useful. | .

781.title: 1. Why this note matters
781.content: Ridge and lasso are both useful regularisation methods, but each has limitations. Ridge regression is stable with correlated predictors, but it usually keeps all predictors. Lasso regression can perform feature selection, but it may be unstable when predictors are strongly correlated. Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. The practical message of this note is: . Elastic net is useful when we want both coefficient shrinkage and some feature selection, especially when predictors are correlated.

782.title: 2. Ridge and lasso recap
782.content: Ridge regression shrinks coefficients towards zero. It usually keeps all predictors in the model. Lasso regression also shrinks coefficients, but it can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero. This means lasso can remove predictors from the model. Elastic net combines these two approaches.

783.title: 3. What elastic net does
783.content: Elastic net uses a mixture of ridge and lasso penalties. It can: . | shrink coefficients; | set some coefficients to zero; | handle correlated predictors better than lasso alone; | produce a model that is more stable than pure lasso in some settings. | . This makes it useful in biomedical prediction, especially with high-dimensional and correlated data.

784.title: 4. The alpha parameter
784.content: Elastic net uses a mixing parameter often called alpha. A common interpretation is: . alpha = 0 ridge regression alpha = 1 lasso regression 0 < alpha < 1 elastic net . Alpha controls the balance between ridge-like and lasso-like behaviour. If alpha is closer to 0, the model behaves more like ridge. If alpha is closer to 1, the model behaves more like lasso.

785.title: 5. The lambda parameter
785.content: Elastic net also uses lambda. Lambda controls the overall strength of regularisation. Small lambda → weak penalty Large lambda → strong penalty . So elastic net has two important tuning ideas: . alpha → type of penalty mixture lambda → strength of penalty . Both affect the final model.

786.title: 6. Why elastic net helps with correlated predictors
786.content: Biomedical predictors are often correlated. Examples include: . | genes in the same biological pathway; | neighbouring DNA methylation sites; | related proteins; | repeated clinical measurements; | imaging features from the same region. | . Lasso may select one predictor from a correlated group and ignore the others. Elastic net can allow groups of related predictors to contribute more smoothly. This may improve stability.

787.title: 7. Elastic net and grouped information
787.content: Suppose five genes are highly correlated because they belong to the same pathway. Lasso may select only one gene. Elastic net may keep several genes from the pathway, while still shrinking their coefficients. This can be useful when the biological signal is distributed across related features. However, this does not prove that all retained genes are causal.

788.title: 8. Elastic net in omics
788.content: Elastic net is commonly useful in omics prediction because omics data often have: . | many predictors; | high correlation; | limited sample size; | noisy measurements; | many weak signals; | high risk of overfitting. | . Examples include: . gene expression prediction DNA methylation prediction proteomic risk models metabolomic classification . Elastic net provides a compromise between ridge and lasso.

789.title: 9. Elastic net versus ridge
789.content: Elastic net may be preferable to ridge when: . | we want some feature selection; | some predictors are likely irrelevant; | the final model should be smaller; | measurement burden matters. | . Ridge may be preferable when: . | many predictors each carry small amounts of information; | removing predictors is not important; | stability is more important than sparsity. | .

790.title: 10. Elastic net versus lasso
790.content: Elastic net may be preferable to lasso when: . | predictors are strongly correlated; | feature selection by lasso is unstable; | groups of related predictors may be useful; | high-dimensional biomedical features are present. | . Lasso may be preferable when: . | a very sparse model is desired; | predictors are not highly correlated; | simplicity is a major goal. | .

791.title: 11. Tuning elastic net
791.content: Elastic net tuning should be done carefully. A simple workflow is: . 1. Define candidate alpha values. 2. Define candidate lambda values. 3. Use cross-validation in the training data. 4. Select tuning values. 5. Fit the final model in the training data. 6. Evaluate once on the untouched test data. The test set should not be used repeatedly to choose alpha or lambda.

792.title: 12. Nested cross-validation
792.content: When many tuning values are explored, nested cross-validation can be helpful. The inner loop tunes alpha and lambda. The outer loop estimates model performance. This avoids using the same validation data for both model selection and performance estimation. Nested cross-validation is especially useful when many models are compared.

793.title: 13. Medical example: cancer recurrence
793.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using: . | age; | stage; | grade; | treatment; | 5,000 gene expression features. | . Ridge may keep all 5,000 features. Lasso may select a small number of features but be unstable. Elastic net may select a group of related genes while shrinking coefficients. This may provide a more stable prediction model. But external validation is still essential.

794.title: 14. Medical example: diabetes prediction
794.content: In a smaller clinical dataset, elastic net may not be necessary if only a few predictors are used. For example, if we use only glucose, BMI, age, and blood pressure, ordinary logistic regression or ridge may be enough. Elastic net becomes more useful when the number of candidate predictors increases or predictors are strongly correlated.

795.title: 15. Interpretation of selected features
795.content: Elastic net-selected features should be interpreted cautiously. They may be: . | true biological signals; | proxies for other variables; | correlated with selected pathways; | artefacts of preprocessing; | dataset-specific patterns; | selected due to random variation. | . Prediction feature selection is not the same as biological discovery.

796.title: 16. Elastic net and calibration
796.content: Elastic net may reduce overfitting, but it does not guarantee good calibration. After fitting an elastic net model, we should assess: . | discrimination; | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | decision curve analysis if linked to action. | . Regularisation is not a substitute for validation.

797.title: 17. Common mistakes
797.content: Common mistakes include: . | tuning alpha and lambda on the final test set; | selecting features before validation; | interpreting selected features as causal; | reporting only AUC; | ignoring calibration; | ignoring feature stability; | failing to externally validate high-dimensional models. | . These mistakes can make results look stronger than they really are.

798.title: 18. Reporting elastic net models
798.content: A clear report should describe: . | outcome definition; | candidate predictors; | preprocessing; | standardisation; | alpha selection; | lambda selection; | validation design; | selected predictors; | discrimination; | calibration; | feature stability if relevant; | external validation if available. | .

799.title: 19. Summary
799.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It can shrink coefficients and perform feature selection. It is especially useful when predictors are correlated, which is common in biomedical data. Like ridge and lasso, elastic net requires careful validation and cautious interpretation.

800.title: Revision questions
800.content: . | What is elastic net? | How does elastic net combine ridge and lasso? | What does alpha control? | What does lambda control? | Why is elastic net useful with correlated predictors? | How is elastic net different from ridge? | How is elastic net different from lasso? | Why might elastic net be useful in omics? | Why should tuning happen inside validation? | What is nested cross-validation? | Why are selected features not automatically causal? | Why does elastic net still require calibration assessment? | .

801.title: Key terms
801.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Elastic net | Regularisation method combining ridge and lasso penalties | . | Alpha | Parameter controlling the ridge-lasso mixing | . | Lambda | Parameter controlling overall penalty strength | . | Ridge regression | Method that shrinks coefficients but usually keeps predictors | . | Lasso regression | Method that can shrink coefficients to zero | . | Correlated predictors | Predictors containing overlapping information | . | Feature selection | Choosing predictors for a model | . | Nested cross-validation | Validation approach separating tuning from evaluation | . | Omics | High-dimensional molecular biomedical data | . | Calibration | Agreement between predicted and observed risks | .

802.title: References
802.content: . | Zou, H., & Hastie, T. (2005). Regularization and variable selection via the elastic net. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B. | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. | Friedman, J., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. glmnet: Lasso and elastic-net regularized generalized linear models. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating. | .

803.title: Key takeaway
803.content: Elastic net is a flexible compromise between ridge and lasso. It is especially useful when biomedical predictors are correlated and we want both shrinkage and some feature selection.

806.title: Aim
806.content: This exercise helps you understand elastic net as a compromise between ridge and lasso. You will practise thinking about: . | ridge-lasso mixing; | alpha; | lambda; | correlated predictors; | biomedical feature selection; | tuning without leakage; | interpretation of selected features. | .

807.title: Part A: Conceptual understanding
807.content: Question 1 . In your own words, explain what elastic net does. Use the following words: . | ridge; | lasso; | shrinkage; | feature selection; | correlated predictors. | . Question 2 . Complete the following: . alpha = 0 → __________ alpha = 1 → __________ 0 < alpha < 1 → __________ . Question 3 . What does lambda control in elastic net? . Question 4 . Which statement is most correct? . A. Elastic net is only useful for small datasets with one predictor. B. Elastic net combines ridge-like and lasso-like regularisation. C. Elastic net proves biological causality. D. Elastic net removes the need for external validation.

808.title: Part B: Ridge, lasso, or elastic net?
808.content: Question 5 . For each situation, choose whether ridge, lasso, or elastic net may be most appropriate. More than one answer may sometimes be reasonable. | Situation | Possible method | . | Many correlated predictors may each contain small predictive information |   | . | A very small selected predictor set is desired |   | . | Predictors are highly correlated and some feature selection is desired |   | . | Feature stability is more important than sparsity |   | . | A compromise between shrinkage and selection is desired |   | . Question 6 . Explain why elastic net may be more stable than lasso when predictors are correlated.

809.title: Part C: Biomedical scenarios
809.content: Question 7 . A gene expression dataset contains: . 250 patients 15,000 gene expression features . Many genes belong to related biological pathways. Why might elastic net be useful here? . Question 8 . A clinical prediction model contains only four predictors: . Age BMI Glucose Blood pressure . Would elastic net always be necessary here? . Explain your answer. Question 9 . A methylation prediction study includes neighbouring CpG sites that are highly correlated. Why might pure lasso selection be unstable? .

810.title: Part D: Tuning and validation
810.content: Question 10 . A student says: . I tried many alpha and lambda values and selected the combination with the best final test set AUC. What is wrong with this approach? . Question 11 . Write a better workflow for tuning elastic net. Question 12 . What is nested cross-validation? . Why may it be useful for elastic net? .

811.title: Part E: Interpretation
811.content: Question 13 . An elastic net model selected 25 genes for predicting cancer recurrence. Can we say these 25 genes cause recurrence? . Explain your answer. Question 14 . List four questions you should ask before making biological claims about elastic net-selected features. Question 15 . Why should calibration still be assessed after fitting an elastic net model? .

812.title: Part F: True or false
812.content: | Statement | True / False | . | Elastic net combines ridge and lasso penalties. |   | . | Elastic net always gives a causal explanation. |   | . | Elastic net can be useful when predictors are correlated. |   | . | alpha controls the ridge-lasso mixture. |   | . | lambda controls the strength of the penalty. |   | . | The test set should be repeatedly used for tuning. |   | .

813.title: Part G: Short written answer
813.content: Question 16 . Write a short explanation of elastic net for a biomedical researcher working with omics data.

814.title: Suggested answers
814.content: Answer 1 . Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It shrinks coefficients, can perform some feature selection, and can be more stable than lasso when predictors are correlated. Answer 2 . alpha = 0 → ridge regression alpha = 1 → lasso regression 0 < alpha < 1 → elastic net . Answer 3 . lambda controls the overall strength of the regularisation penalty. Answer 4 . B. Elastic net combines ridge-like and lasso-like regularisation. Answer 5 . | Situation | Possible method | . | Many correlated predictors may each contain small predictive information | Ridge or elastic net | . | A very small selected predictor set is desired | Lasso | . | Predictors are highly correlated and some feature selection is desired | Elastic net | . | Feature stability is more important than sparsity | Ridge or elastic net | . | A compromise between shrinkage and selection is desired | Elastic net | . Answer 6 . Elastic net includes a ridge-like component, which helps handle correlated predictors more smoothly. Lasso may choose one predictor from a correlated group and discard others, while elastic net may allow grouped predictors to contribute together. Answer 7 . Elastic net may be useful because the dataset has many predictors and many genes may be correlated through biological pathways. Elastic net can shrink coefficients, select features, and handle correlated predictors better than pure lasso. Answer 8 . No. With only four clinically meaningful predictors, ordinary logistic regression or ridge may be sufficient. Elastic net becomes more useful when there are many candidate predictors or strong correlation with a need for feature selection. Answer 9 . Neighbouring CpG sites may contain similar information. Lasso may select one CpG site and remove others, but a small change in the sample could lead to a different CpG being selected. Answer 10 . The final test set was used for model tuning. This means the test set became part of model development, so the final test AUC may be optimistic. Answer 11 . A better workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test sets. 2. Use cross-validation inside the training data to choose alpha and lambda. 3. Fit the final model on the training data. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. Answer 12 . Nested cross-validation separates tuning from performance estimation. The inner loop tunes alpha and lambda. The outer loop estimates model performance. This is useful when many tuning combinations are tested. Answer 13 . No. Elastic net-selected genes are predictors in a model. They may be causal, correlated with causal factors, proxies, artefacts, or selected by chance. Causality requires additional evidence. Answer 14 . Ask: . | Are the selected features stable? | Are they biologically plausible? | Do they validate externally? | Could batch effects explain them? | Are they measured reliably? | Are they available at prediction time? | . Answer 15 . Elastic net may improve stability, but it does not guarantee well-calibrated probabilities. Calibration must be checked because clinical decisions may depend on accurate risk estimates. Answer 16 . Elastic net is useful for omics data because it combines shrinkage and feature selection. It can handle correlated features better than lasso alone, which is helpful when genes, methylation sites, or proteins move together. However, selected features still need careful validation and cautious biological interpretation.

816.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
816.title: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

817.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
817.title: Big idea
817.content: Feature selection means choosing a subset of predictors for a model. In biomedical machine learning, feature selection is common because datasets may contain many clinical variables, biomarkers, imaging features, genes, methylation sites, proteins, or metabolites. Feature selection can make a model simpler. But if done incorrectly, it can create serious data leakage.

818.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
818.title: Scene: the top biomarkers
818.content: Learner: I selected the top 20 biomarkers using the full dataset, then cross-validated my model. The AUC is excellent. Leakage Monster: Beautiful! Very impressive! . Prof. Stat: Wait. If you used the full dataset before cross-validation, the validation folds influenced feature selection. Dr. Clinic: So the AUC may be inflated? . Prof. Stat: Exactly. That is feature-selection leakage.

819.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
819.title: 1. Why feature selection is used
819.content: Feature selection may be used to: . | reduce model complexity; | remove noisy predictors; | reduce measurement burden; | make the model easier to explain; | identify candidate biomarkers; | improve practicality; | create a smaller prediction model. | . In medicine, fewer predictors can be helpful. A model using routinely available predictors is often easier to implement than a model requiring expensive specialised tests.

820.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
820.title: 2. Clinical feature selection
820.content: Clinical feature selection uses medical knowledge. For example, a diabetes prediction model may include: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | family history; | blood pressure. | . These predictors are clinically sensible and usually available. Clinical selection helps ensure that predictors are meaningful and available at the intended prediction time.

821.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
821.title: 3. Statistical feature selection
821.content: Statistical feature selection uses the data. Examples include: . | selecting predictors with low p-values; | selecting predictors most correlated with the outcome; | stepwise regression; | lasso selection; | random forest importance; | recursive feature elimination. | . These methods may be useful, but they must be validated carefully.

822.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
822.title: 4. Model-based feature selection
822.content: Model-based feature selection uses a fitted model to identify useful predictors. Examples: . Lasso selected predictors Random forest variable importance Gradient boosting feature importance Recursive feature elimination . These approaches identify features that help prediction in a specific dataset. They do not automatically identify causal variables.

823.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
823.title: 5. Feature-selection leakage
823.content: Feature-selection leakage happens when validation or test data influence predictor selection. Incorrect workflow: . 1. Use full dataset to select features. 2. Split into train and test sets. 3. Fit model on training data. 4. Evaluate on test data. The problem is that the test set helped choose the features. So the test set is no longer independent. Performance may look better than it really is.

824.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
824.title: 6. Correct workflow
824.content: A safer workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test sets. 2. Select features using training data only. 3. Fit the model using training data only. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. For cross-validation: . Feature selection must happen inside each training fold. This keeps validation honest.

825.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
825.title: Scene: validation must come first
825.content: Learner: But selecting features before validation feels easier. Prof. Stat: Easier, yes. Honest, no. Dr. Clinic: If the model is going to be used on patients, honesty matters more than convenience. Prof. Stat: Exactly.

826.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
826.title: 7. Why biomedical data are risky
826.content: Biomedical datasets can contain thousands of predictors. For example: . 300 patients 450,000 methylation sites . With so many features, some will appear associated with the outcome by chance. If we select features using the full dataset before validation, the model may look excellent but fail externally. This is one of the most common problems in biomedical prediction.

827.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
827.title: 8. Batch effects
827.content: Batch effects are technical differences unrelated to biology. Examples: . | lab batch; | sequencing run; | methylation array plate; | sample processing date; | scanner type; | technician; | hospital site. | . If batch is related to outcome, feature selection may pick technical artefacts rather than biological signal. This can produce poor generalisation.

828.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
828.title: 9. Feature stability
828.content: A useful selected feature should ideally be stable. Ask: . Is this feature selected across different resamples? . If selected features change dramatically across folds or bootstrap samples, interpretation should be cautious. The model may still predict, but the specific feature list may not be reliable.

829.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
829.title: 10. Correlated predictors
829.content: Biomedical predictors are often correlated. Examples: . | genes in the same pathway; | nearby methylation sites; | related blood biomarkers; | imaging features from the same region. | . When predictors are correlated, feature selection may choose one feature from a group and ignore the others. Another dataset may select a different feature from the same group. This makes biological interpretation difficult.

830.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
830.title: 11. Prediction versus discovery
830.content: Feature selection for prediction is not the same as biological discovery. A selected feature may be: . | causally important; | correlated with a causal factor; | a proxy for disease severity; | a technical artefact; | selected by chance; | specific to one dataset. | . Always separate the prediction question from the discovery question.

831.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
831.title: 12. Medical example: methylation prediction
831.content: Suppose we predict exposure status using DNA methylation. The dataset contains: . 300 participants 450,000 CpG sites . A poor workflow is: . Select top 100 CpG sites using full data Cross-validate classifier Report AUC . A better workflow is: . Create folds first Select CpG sites inside each training fold Fit model inside each training fold Evaluate on held-out fold . This avoids feature-selection leakage.

832.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
832.title: 13. Practical checklist
832.content: Before trusting selected features, ask: . | Were features selected inside validation? | Was the final test set untouched? | Are selected features stable? | Are they clinically or biologically plausible? | Are they measured reliably? | Are they available at prediction time? | Could batch effects explain them? | Do they validate externally? | Do they improve calibration or only AUC? | Are the claims appropriately cautious? | .

833.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
833.title: Common mistake
833.content: Do not select biomarkers using the full dataset before cross-validation. This leaks outcome information into validation and can seriously inflate performance.

834.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
834.title: Key takeaway
834.content: Feature selection can make biomedical models simpler and more practical, but it must be performed inside validation. Selected features should be interpreted as prediction features first, not automatic biological discoveries.

835.doc: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
835.title: Lecture 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

836.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
836.title: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

837.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
837.title: Learning objectives
837.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain why feature selection is common in biomedical machine learning. | Distinguish clinical, statistical, and model-based feature selection. | Understand why feature selection can cause data leakage. | Explain why feature selection must happen inside validation. | Describe why selected features may be unstable. | Explain why prediction feature selection does not prove causality. | Recognise special risks in omics and biomarker studies. | Apply a practical checklist before trusting selected features. | .

838.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
838.title: 1. Why this note matters
838.content: Biomedical datasets often contain many possible predictors. These may include: . | clinical variables; | blood test measurements; | imaging features; | biomarkers; | gene expression values; | DNA methylation sites; | proteins; | metabolites; | electronic health record codes. | . Feature selection is the process of choosing a subset of predictors. It can make models simpler and more practical. However, feature selection is also one of the easiest ways to accidentally create data leakage. The practical message of this note is: . Feature selection can simplify a biomedical prediction model, but if done incorrectly, it can seriously bias model performance.

839.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
839.title: 2. Why feature selection is used
839.content: Feature selection may be used to: . | reduce overfitting; | simplify the model; | reduce measurement burden; | improve interpretability; | remove noisy predictors; | identify candidate biomarkers; | create a clinically feasible predictor set. | . In medicine, a model using fewer predictors may be easier to implement. For example, a model requiring five routinely measured blood tests may be more practical than a model requiring 100 specialised biomarkers.

840.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
840.title: 3. Feature selection is not automatically good
840.content: Feature selection can help, but it can also harm. It may remove useful predictors. It may select unstable predictors. It may select variables that work only in one dataset. It may create data leakage if performed before validation. Therefore, feature selection should be treated as part of the modelling process, not as a harmless preprocessing step.

841.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
841.title: 4. Clinical feature selection
841.content: Clinical feature selection uses subject-matter knowledge. For example, a diabetes risk model may include: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | family history; | blood pressure. | . These predictors are clinically plausible and usually available. Clinical feature selection helps ensure that the model uses predictors that are meaningful, measurable, and available at the intended prediction time.

842.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
842.title: 5. Statistical feature selection
842.content: Statistical feature selection uses the data to select predictors. Examples include: . | univariable p-value screening; | correlation filtering; | stepwise regression; | lasso selection; | recursive feature elimination; | model-based variable importance. | . These methods may be useful, but they must be used carefully. If they use the full dataset before validation, leakage occurs.

843.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
843.title: 6. Model-based feature selection
843.content: Model-based feature selection uses a fitted model to identify useful predictors. Examples include: . | lasso non-zero coefficients; | random forest variable importance; | gradient boosting importance; | recursive feature elimination. | . These methods identify predictors that help prediction in a specific dataset and modelling workflow. They do not automatically identify causal factors.

844.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
844.title: 7. Feature-selection leakage
844.content: Feature-selection leakage occurs when validation or test data influence predictor selection. An incorrect workflow is: . 1. Use the full dataset to select predictors. 2. Split into training and test sets. 3. Fit the model using selected predictors. 4. Evaluate on the test set. This is biased because the test set helped choose the predictors. The test performance may look better than it really is.

845.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
845.title: 8. Correct feature selection workflow
845.content: A safer workflow is: . 1. Split data into training and test sets. 2. Select features using training data only. 3. Fit the model using training data only. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. For cross-validation: . Feature selection must happen inside each training fold. This means that each validation fold remains genuinely unseen.

846.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
846.title: 9. Why this matters so much in biomedical data
846.content: Biomedical datasets often contain many predictors. For example: . n = 300 participants p = 450,000 methylation sites . With so many predictors, some will appear associated with the outcome by chance. If the full dataset is used to select features before validation, the validation performance can become seriously inflated. This is one of the most common problems in omics prediction studies.

847.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
847.title: 10. Omics feature selection
847.content: Omics feature selection may involve: . | selecting top genes; | selecting CpG sites; | selecting proteins; | selecting metabolites; | selecting genomic variants; | selecting pathway scores. | . These features may be biologically interesting. However, if selected incorrectly, they may simply reflect noise, batch effects, or cohort-specific patterns.

848.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
848.title: 11. Batch effects and feature selection
848.content: Batch effects are technical differences unrelated to biology. Examples include: . | laboratory batch; | sequencing run; | methylation array plate; | sample processing date; | scanner; | technician; | hospital site. | . If batch is associated with outcome, feature selection may select variables that reflect batch rather than disease biology. This can produce excellent internal performance and poor external performance.

849.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
849.title: 12. Feature stability
849.content: A selected feature should ideally be stable. Stability means that the same or similar features are selected across: . | bootstrap samples; | cross-validation folds; | repeated analyses; | external datasets. | . If selected features change dramatically across resamples, interpretation should be cautious. The model may still have some predictive ability, but the specific selected feature list may not be reliable.

850.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
850.title: 13. Correlated predictors and instability
850.content: Biomedical predictors are often correlated. For example: . | nearby methylation sites may move together; | genes in the same pathway may be correlated; | proteins in the same system may be related; | clinical measurements may reflect the same underlying condition. | . When predictors are correlated, feature selection may choose one predictor from a group and ignore others. In another dataset, a different predictor from the same group may be selected. This can make interpretation unstable.

851.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
851.title: 14. Prediction versus discovery
851.content: Feature selection for prediction is not the same as biological discovery. A predictor may help prediction because it is correlated with the outcome. That does not prove it causes the outcome. Similarly, a biologically important predictor may not be selected if it does not improve prediction in that dataset. Always distinguish: . Prediction question: Does this feature help predict? Discovery question: Is this feature biologically or causally important? .

852.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
852.title: 15. Medical example: diabetes prediction
852.content: In a diabetes prediction model, feature selection may identify: . | glucose; | BMI; | age; | pedigree. | . These predictors are clinically plausible. However, even here, selection should be validated. A feature selected in one dataset may not perform similarly in another population.

853.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
853.title: 16. Biomedical example: methylation prediction
853.content: Suppose we want to predict exposure status from DNA methylation data. The dataset contains: . 300 participants 450,000 CpG sites . If we select the top 100 CpG sites using the full dataset and then cross-validate a classifier, performance will be biased. The correct workflow is to select CpG sites inside each training fold. External validation is also important before making strong claims.

854.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
854.title: 17. Feature selection and clinical practicality
854.content: A selected predictor should be practical. Ask: . | Is it available at prediction time? | Is it routinely measured? | Is it affordable? | Is it reliable? | Does it require specialised technology? | Can it be used in the target healthcare setting? | . A statistically selected feature may not be clinically useful if it cannot be measured in practice.

855.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
855.title: 18. Practical checklist
855.content: Before trusting selected features, ask: . | Were features selected inside validation? | Was the final test set untouched? | Are selected features stable? | Are they clinically or biologically plausible? | Are they measured reliably? | Are they available at the intended prediction time? | Could batch effects explain them? | Do they validate externally? | Do they improve calibration or only AUC? | Are claims limited to what the study design supports? | .

856.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
856.title: 19. Common mistakes
856.content: Common mistakes include: . | selecting features before train/test split; | selecting features before cross-validation; | using the test set to refine feature selection; | interpreting selected features as causal; | reporting only selected features without stability checks; | ignoring batch effects; | ignoring clinical feasibility. | . These mistakes can make a model look more convincing than it really is.

857.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
857.title: 20. Reporting feature selection
857.content: A clear report should describe: . | all candidate predictors; | feature selection method; | when feature selection was performed; | whether it was inside validation; | number of selected features; | stability of selected features if assessed; | clinical or biological interpretation; | validation performance; | limitations. | . Transparency is essential for judging reliability.

858.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
858.title: 21. Summary
858.content: Feature selection is common in biomedical machine learning. It can make models simpler and more practical. However, feature selection must be included inside the validation process. Selected features should be interpreted as prediction features first, not automatically as causal or biologically validated discoveries.

859.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
859.title: Revision questions
859.content: . | What is feature selection? | Why is feature selection used in biomedical prediction? | What is clinical feature selection? | What is statistical feature selection? | What is model-based feature selection? | What is feature-selection leakage? | Why must feature selection happen inside cross-validation? | Why is omics feature selection especially risky? | What are batch effects? | What does feature stability mean? | Why can correlated predictors make feature selection unstable? | Why does prediction feature selection not prove causality? | What should be checked before trusting selected biomarkers? | Why does clinical practicality matter? | What should be reported about feature selection? | .

860.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
860.title: Key terms
860.content: | Term | Meaning | . | Feature selection | Choosing a subset of predictors | . | Clinical feature selection | Predictor selection based on domain knowledge | . | Statistical feature selection | Predictor selection based on data-driven criteria | . | Model-based selection | Feature selection based on fitted model behaviour | . | Leakage | Information from validation or test data influencing model development | . | Feature-selection leakage | Leakage caused by selecting predictors before validation | . | Stability | Consistency of selected predictors across resamples | . | Batch effect | Technical variation unrelated to biological signal | . | Omics | High-dimensional molecular biomedical data | . | Prediction | Estimating outcomes in new observations | . | Discovery | Identifying biological mechanisms or associations | .

861.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
861.title: References
861.content: . | Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction. | James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R. | Steyerberg, E. W. (2019). Clinical Prediction Models: A Practical Approach to Development, Validation, and Updating. | Riley, R. D., van der Windt, D., Croft, P., & Moons, K. G. M. (2019). Prognosis Research in Healthcare: Concepts, Methods, and Impact. | Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. | .

862.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
862.title: Key takeaway
862.content: Feature selection can make biomedical models simpler and more practical, but it must be performed inside validation and interpreted as prediction selection rather than automatic biological proof.

863.doc: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
863.title: Note 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

864.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
864.title: Exercise 14: Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

865.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
865.title: Aim
865.content: This exercise helps you understand feature selection in biomedical machine learning. You will practise thinking about: . | clinical feature selection; | statistical feature selection; | model-based feature selection; | feature-selection leakage; | batch effects; | feature stability; | prediction versus discovery. | .

866.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
866.title: Part A: Conceptual understanding
866.content: Question 1 . What is feature selection? . Explain it in one short paragraph. Question 2 . Give three reasons why feature selection may be useful in biomedical prediction modelling. Question 3 . Give three risks of feature selection. Question 4 . What is the difference between: . clinical feature selection . and . statistical feature selection .

867.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
867.title: Part B: Types of feature selection
867.content: Question 5 . Classify each method as clinical, statistical, or model-based feature selection. | Method | Type | . | Selecting predictors based on prior clinical knowledge |   | . | Selecting predictors with lowest p-values |   | . | Selecting predictors retained by lasso |   | . | Selecting variables based on random forest importance |   | . | Selecting predictors because they are routinely available in hospital records |   | . | Selecting top genes by association with outcome |   | . Question 6 . Why is model-based feature selection not the same as causal discovery? .

868.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
868.title: Part C: Feature-selection leakage
868.content: Question 7 . A researcher uses this workflow: . 1. Use the full dataset to select the top 50 biomarkers. 2. Split the dataset into training and test sets. 3. Fit a model using the selected biomarkers. 4. Evaluate the model on the test set. What is wrong with this workflow? . Question 8 . Rewrite the workflow correctly. Question 9 . Why can feature-selection leakage make model performance look better than it really is? .

869.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
869.title: Part D: Cross-validation workflow
869.content: Question 10 . A student wants to use 5-fold cross-validation with feature selection. Which workflow is correct? . A. Select features once using the full dataset. Then run 5-fold cross-validation. B. For each fold, select features using only the training fold. Then evaluate on the validation fold. Explain your answer. Question 11 . Why is feature selection before cross-validation especially problematic in omics data? .

870.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
870.title: Part E: Batch effects
870.content: Question 12 . What is a batch effect? . Give two biomedical examples. Question 13 . A methylation study includes samples processed on two different plates. Most cases were processed on Plate 1. Most controls were processed on Plate 2. Why is this a problem for feature selection? . Question 14 . What might happen if a model learns batch effects instead of disease biology? .

871.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
871.title: Part F: Feature stability
871.content: Question 15 . What does feature stability mean? . Question 16 . A lasso model selects different biomarkers in each bootstrap sample. What does this suggest? . Question 17 . Why can correlated predictors make selected feature lists unstable? .

872.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
872.title: Part G: Prediction versus discovery
872.content: Question 18 . A feature is selected by a prediction model. Does this prove it causes the outcome? . Explain your answer. Question 19 . A biologically important predictor is not selected by the model. Does this mean it is not biologically important? . Explain your answer.

873.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
873.title: Part H: Biomedical scenario
873.content: A team is building a prediction model for disease recurrence using: . 400 patients 120 recurrence events 10 clinical predictors 10,000 gene expression features . They select the top 100 genes using the full dataset before cross-validation. Question 20 . Identify the main problem. Question 21 . Describe a corrected analysis plan. Question 22 . List five questions the team should answer before claiming that the selected genes are important.

874.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
874.title: Part I: True or false
874.content: | Statement | True / False | . | Feature selection can reduce measurement burden. |   | . | Feature selection always improves external validation performance. |   | . | Feature selection should be done inside validation. |   | . | Selected features are automatically causal. |   | . | Batch effects can influence selected features. |   | . | Feature stability is important for interpretation. |   | .

875.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
875.title: Suggested answers
875.content: Answer 1 . Feature selection is the process of choosing a subset of predictors from a larger set of candidate predictors for use in a model. Answer 2 . Feature selection may reduce model complexity, reduce measurement burden, improve practicality, remove noisy predictors, and make the model easier to communicate. Answer 3 . Feature selection can cause leakage, select unstable predictors, remove useful predictors, identify dataset-specific patterns, or encourage overinterpretation of selected features. Answer 4 . Clinical feature selection uses subject knowledge and clinical practicality. Statistical feature selection uses data-driven criteria such as p-values, correlations, or model-based importance. Answer 5 . | Method | Type | . | Selecting predictors based on prior clinical knowledge | Clinical | . | Selecting predictors with lowest p-values | Statistical | . | Selecting predictors retained by lasso | Model-based/statistical | . | Selecting variables based on random forest importance | Model-based | . | Selecting predictors because they are routinely available in hospital records | Clinical/practical | . | Selecting top genes by association with outcome | Statistical | . Answer 6 . Model-based feature selection identifies variables useful for prediction in a specific dataset and model. It does not prove that these variables cause the outcome. Answer 7 . The test set influenced feature selection because the full dataset was used before splitting. This creates leakage and may inflate test performance. Answer 8 . Correct workflow: . 1. Split into training and test sets. 2. Select features using training data only. 3. Fit the model using training data only. 4. Evaluate once on the untouched test set. Answer 9 . Validation or test data have already influenced model development, so the evaluation is no longer independent. Random patterns from the validation/test data can be selected and make performance look better. Answer 10 . B is correct. Feature selection must happen inside each training fold so that the validation fold remains unseen. Answer 11 . Omics data contain many thousands of features. Some will appear related to the outcome by chance. If selected before validation, those chance signals can inflate performance. Answer 12 . A batch effect is technical variation unrelated to biology. Examples include sequencing run, methylation array plate, lab technician, scanner, processing date, or hospital site. Answer 13 . Feature selection may identify methylation sites that distinguish plates rather than disease status. Because plate is confounded with outcome, selected features may reflect technical artefacts. Answer 14 . The model may perform well internally but fail in external data where the batch structure is different. Answer 15 . Feature stability means selected features are consistent across resamples, folds, bootstrap samples, or external datasets. Answer 16 . It suggests the selected biomarker list is unstable. The model may not provide a reliable set of biomarkers for interpretation. Answer 17 . When predictors are correlated, several variables contain similar information. A selection method may choose one in one sample and another in a different sample. Answer 18 . No. A selected feature may be causal, correlated with a causal factor, a proxy, a batch artefact, or selected by chance. Answer 19 . No. A biologically important predictor may not improve prediction enough to be selected, or it may be redundant with other predictors. Answer 20 . The main problem is feature-selection leakage. The full dataset was used to select genes before cross-validation. Answer 21 . A corrected plan: . 1. Define cross-validation folds. 2. Within each training fold, perform gene selection. 3. Fit the prediction model using selected genes in that training fold. 4. Predict the held-out validation fold. 5. Repeat across folds. 6. Estimate performance from held-out predictions. 7. Use external validation if possible. Answer 22 . They should ask: . | Were genes selected inside validation? | Are selected genes stable? | Are they biologically plausible? | Could batch effects explain them? | Do they validate externally? | Are they measured reliably? | Do they improve calibration or only AUC? | Are claims limited to prediction rather than causation? | .

876.doc: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data
876.title: Exercise 14 - Feature Selection in Biomedical Data

877.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
877.title: Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data

878.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
878.title: Big idea
878.content: High-dimensional data often contain far more predictors than patients. This is common in biomedical research. Penalised regression methods, such as ridge, lasso, and elastic net, help build prediction models when ordinary regression would be unstable or impossible. But high-dimensional prediction is risky. Without careful validation, models can look excellent and still fail on new data.

879.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
879.title: Scene: too many columns, too few patients
879.content: Learner: I have 200 patients and 20,000 gene expression features. Can I fit ordinary logistic regression? . Prof. Stat: No, not safely. You have far more predictors than patients. Dr. Clinic: But those genes may contain useful biological information. Prof. Stat: They might. Penalised regression helps use that information while controlling overfitting.

880.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
880.title: 1. What high-dimensional means
880.content: High-dimensional data means there are many predictors. Often: . p >> n . where: . p = number of predictors n = number of observations . Examples: . 300 patients and 20,000 genes 500 patients and 450,000 methylation sites 150 patients and 5,000 proteins . This is common in omics, imaging, and electronic health record research.

881.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
881.title: 2. Why ordinary regression fails
881.content: Ordinary regression becomes unstable when there are too many predictors. Problems include: . | overfitting; | unstable coefficients; | perfect or near-perfect separation; | inability to estimate all coefficients; | excellent training performance but poor validation performance. | . With thousands of features, many will appear predictive by chance.

882.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
882.title: 3. Penalised regression
882.content: Penalised regression adds a penalty to the model. The model is asked to: . predict the outcome well and avoid overly complex coefficients . This helps control overfitting. Common penalised regression methods include: . | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net. | .

883.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
883.title: 4. Ridge for high-dimensional data
883.content: Ridge regression shrinks all coefficients. It can handle many correlated predictors. It is useful when many features may each contain small predictive information. However, ridge usually keeps all predictors, so it may not produce a short biomarker list.

884.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
884.title: 5. Lasso for high-dimensional data
884.content: Lasso can set many coefficients to zero. This makes it attractive for feature selection. For example, from: . 20,000 genes . lasso may select: . 30 genes . But selected features can be unstable, especially when predictors are correlated.

885.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
885.title: 6. Elastic net for high-dimensional data
885.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It can select features while also handling correlated predictors more smoothly than lasso alone. This makes it useful in omics data, where groups of features often move together.

886.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
886.title: Scene: choosing the method
886.content: Learner: Ridge, lasso, elastic net — which one should I choose? . Prof. Stat: It depends. Ridge is stable, lasso is sparse, elastic net is a compromise. Dr. Clinic: So we compare them using proper validation? . Prof. Stat: Exactly. Validation should guide the choice.

887.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
887.title: 7. Tuning matters
887.content: Penalised regression depends on tuning parameters. The most important is often lambda. Low penalty → more complex model High penalty → simpler model . Elastic net also uses alpha, which controls the balance between ridge and lasso. Tuning must be performed using training data or inner validation. The final test set should remain untouched.
