888.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
888.title: 8. Nested validation
888.content: High-dimensional workflows often need nested validation. The inner loop selects features and tunes parameters. The outer loop estimates performance. This avoids using the same data to both choose the model and evaluate the model. Nested validation is especially important when many tuning choices are explored.

889.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
889.title: 9. Preprocessing leakage
889.content: High-dimensional data often require preprocessing. Examples: . | normalisation; | filtering; | batch correction; | PCA; | feature scaling; | missing data handling. | . If preprocessing is learned from the full dataset before validation, leakage can occur. For example: . 1. Perform PCA using all samples. 2. Cross-validate a model using PCA components. This is biased because the validation folds influenced the PCA components.

890.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
890.title: 10. Batch effects
890.content: Batch effects are technical differences unrelated to biology. Examples: . | sequencing batch; | methylation array plate; | laboratory technician; | processing date; | scanner; | hospital site. | . If batch is associated with outcome, the model may learn technical artefacts instead of biological signal. This can produce excellent internal performance and poor external performance.

891.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
891.title: 11. External validation
891.content: High-dimensional models can be fragile. A model trained on one platform, cohort, or laboratory may fail elsewhere. External validation is essential before making strong claims. For biomedical prediction, internal performance alone is not enough.

892.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
892.title: 12. Calibration still matters
892.content: High-dimensional models should not be evaluated only by AUC. A model may rank patients well but produce risk estimates that are too high or too low. For clinical prediction, calibration matters because predicted probabilities may guide action. Assessment should include: . | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness where relevant. | .

893.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
893.title: 13. Interpretation of selected features
893.content: Selected features from penalised regression should be interpreted cautiously. They may represent: . | true biological signal; | correlated proxies; | batch effects; | cohort-specific patterns; | noise. | . A prediction model can suggest candidates for further study, but it does not prove biological mechanism.

894.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
894.title: Scene: discovery or prediction?
894.content: Learner: My penalised model selected 25 genes. Can I say these genes explain the disease? . Prof. Stat: Not from prediction modelling alone. Dr. Clinic: So they are candidate predictors, not confirmed mechanisms? . Prof. Stat: Exactly.

895.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
895.title: 14. Medical example: methylation prediction
895.content: Suppose we want to predict exposure status using DNA methylation. The dataset contains: . 300 participants 450,000 CpG sites . A penalised model may select a small set of CpG sites. Before trusting the result, ask: . | Was feature selection inside validation? | Was preprocessing inside validation? | Were batch effects assessed? | Was calibration assessed? | Was external validation performed? | Are selected features stable? | Are biological claims cautious? | .

896.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
896.title: 15. Practical checklist
896.content: Before trusting a high-dimensional penalised model, ask: . | What is the exact prediction question? | What is the prediction time point? | How many patients are available? | How many outcome events are available? | How many candidate predictors are available? | Was preprocessing done inside validation? | Was feature selection done inside validation? | Was tuning performed without touching the test set? | Were batch effects assessed? | Was calibration assessed? | Was external validation performed? | Are selected features stable? | .

897.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
897.title: Common mistake
897.content: Do not trust a high-dimensional model simply because it has a high internal AUC. High-dimensional models are very vulnerable to leakage, overfitting, and batch effects.

898.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
898.title: Key takeaway
898.content: Penalised regression makes high-dimensional prediction possible, but credible biomedical use requires careful tuning, leakage prevention, validation, calibration, external testing, and cautious interpretation of selected features.

899.doc: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
899.title: Lecture 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data

900.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
900.title: Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data

901.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
901.title: Learning objectives
901.content: By the end of this note, you should be able to: . | Explain what high-dimensional data means. | Describe why ordinary regression can fail when the number of predictors is much larger than the number of observations. | Explain how penalised regression helps control overfitting. | Compare ridge, lasso, and elastic net in high-dimensional settings. | Understand the role of tuning parameters. | Explain why nested validation may be needed. | Recognise the risks of batch effects and preprocessing leakage. | Interpret high-dimensional prediction models cautiously. | .

902.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
902.title: 1. Why this note matters
902.content: High-dimensional biomedical data are now common in medical research. Examples include: . | gene expression data; | DNA methylation data; | proteomics; | metabolomics; | microbiome data; | imaging radiomics; | large electronic health record feature sets. | . These datasets may contain thousands or even hundreds of thousands of predictors. The number of patients may be much smaller. This creates a major risk of overfitting. The practical message of this note is: . Penalised regression makes high-dimensional prediction possible, but trustworthy results require careful validation, leakage prevention, and cautious interpretation.

903.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
903.title: 2. What high-dimensional means
903.content: High-dimensional data means the number of predictors is large. Often, the number of predictors is much larger than the number of observations. This is commonly written as: . p >> n . where: . p = number of predictors n = number of observations . For example: . n = 300 participants p = 450,000 CpG methylation sites . This is a high-dimensional problem.

904.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
904.title: 3. Why high-dimensional data are difficult
904.content: When there are many predictors, some will appear related to the outcome by chance. The model may find patterns that are not real. This is especially risky when: . | sample size is small; | outcome events are limited; | predictors are noisy; | predictors are correlated; | batch effects are present; | feature selection is done before validation; | many models are tried and only the best result is reported. | . High-dimensional prediction requires careful study design.

905.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
905.title: 4. Why ordinary regression can fail
905.content: Ordinary regression may fail when there are too many predictors. Problems include: . | unstable coefficients; | overfitting; | perfect or near-perfect separation; | inability to estimate all parameters; | excellent training performance but poor validation performance. | . For example, ordinary logistic regression cannot safely estimate 20,000 gene coefficients from 300 patients. Penalised regression helps by constraining the model.

906.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
906.title: 5. What penalised regression does
906.content: Penalised regression adds a penalty to the model-fitting process. The model is asked to: . predict the outcome well and avoid excessive model complexity . This helps control overfitting. Common penalised methods include: . | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net. | . Each method handles high-dimensional data differently.

907.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
907.title: 6. Ridge regression in high-dimensional data
907.content: Ridge regression shrinks all coefficients towards zero. It is useful when: . | many predictors may each carry small amounts of information; | predictors are correlated; | stability is important; | removing predictors is not the main goal. | . However, ridge usually keeps all predictors. This means it may not produce a simple list of selected biomarkers.

908.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
908.title: 7. Lasso regression in high-dimensional data
908.content: Lasso regression can shrink many coefficients to zero. This makes it useful for feature selection. For example, from: . 20,000 gene expression features . lasso may select: . 30 genes . This can be useful for building a smaller model. However, selected features may be unstable, especially when predictors are correlated.

909.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
909.title: 8. Elastic net in high-dimensional data
909.content: Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. It can shrink coefficients and select predictors. It is often useful when predictors are correlated. This is common in omics because genes, CpG sites, proteins, and metabolites often move together in biological groups. Elastic net may be more stable than lasso alone in these settings.

910.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
910.title: 9. Comparing ridge, lasso, and elastic net
910.content: | Method | Main behaviour | Useful when | . | Ridge | Shrinks all coefficients | Many correlated predictors may contribute | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients to zero | A sparse predictor set is desired | . | Elastic net | Combines ridge and lasso | Predictors are numerous and correlated | . There is no universally best method. The best method depends on the prediction task, data structure, and validation results.

911.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
911.title: 10. Tuning parameters
911.content: Penalised regression methods require tuning. Important parameters include: . lambda: strength of penalty alpha: ridge-lasso mixing in elastic net . Tuning should be done using training data or inner cross-validation. The final test set should not be repeatedly used to choose tuning parameters.

912.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
912.title: 11. Why nested validation may be needed
912.content: High-dimensional workflows often involve many choices: . | feature filtering; | preprocessing; | penalty type; | lambda value; | alpha value; | model comparison; | threshold choice. | . If the same validation data are used to make these choices and report final performance, performance may be optimistic. Nested validation separates model selection from performance estimation. Inner loop: tune model and select features Outer loop: estimate model performance . This is especially useful when many tuning choices are explored.

913.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
913.title: 12. Preprocessing leakage
913.content: High-dimensional data usually require preprocessing. Examples include: . | normalisation; | transformation; | filtering; | batch correction; | PCA; | feature scaling; | missing data handling. | . If preprocessing is learned from the full dataset before validation, leakage can occur. For example: . 1. Perform PCA using all samples. 2. Cross-validate a model using the PCA components. This is biased because the validation folds influenced the PCA components. Preprocessing must happen inside the validation process.

914.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
914.title: 13. Batch effects
914.content: Batch effects are technical differences unrelated to biology. Examples include: . | sequencing batch; | methylation array plate; | laboratory technician; | processing date; | scanner type; | hospital site. | . If batch is associated with outcome, the model may learn batch differences instead of disease biology. This can produce excellent internal performance but poor external performance.

915.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
915.title: 14. High-dimensional prediction and external validation
915.content: External validation is especially important in high-dimensional prediction. A model may perform well in one dataset because of: . | cohort-specific patterns; | platform-specific measurements; | batch effects; | local preprocessing choices; | population differences. | . External validation tests whether the model works in a different setting. Without external validation, strong claims should be avoided.

916.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
916.title: 15. Calibration in high-dimensional models
916.content: High-dimensional models should not be evaluated only using AUC. They also require calibration assessment. A model may rank patients well but produce risk estimates that are too high or too low. For clinical prediction, calibration matters because predicted probabilities may guide decisions. Assessment should include: . | calibration plot; | calibration slope; | calibration intercept; | Brier score; | clinical usefulness where relevant. | .

917.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
917.title: 16. Interpretation of selected features
917.content: Selected features from high-dimensional penalised regression should be interpreted cautiously. They may represent: . | true biological signal; | correlated proxies; | technical artefacts; | batch effects; | cohort-specific patterns; | random noise. | . Prediction performance and biological mechanism are different questions. A selected feature should be considered a candidate for further study, not definitive proof.

918.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
918.title: 17. Medical example: methylation prediction
918.content: Suppose we want to predict exposure status using DNA methylation data. The dataset contains: . 300 participants 450,000 CpG sites . A penalised regression model may select a small number of CpG sites. Before trusting the result, we should ask: . | Was feature selection inside validation? | Were batch effects handled properly? | Was preprocessing done inside resampling? | Was calibration assessed? | Was external validation performed? | Are selected CpG sites biologically plausible? | Are the findings stable across resamples? | .

919.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
919.title: 18. Medical example: cancer gene expression prediction
919.content: Suppose we predict cancer recurrence using gene expression data. The dataset contains: . 500 patients 20,000 genes 80 recurrence events . Ordinary regression is not appropriate. Penalised regression may help. However, the model must still be validated carefully. A high internal AUC is not enough. We need calibration, external validation, and cautious interpretation of selected genes.

920.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
920.title: 19. Practical checklist
920.content: Before trusting a high-dimensional penalised model, ask: . | What is the exact prediction question? | What is the prediction time point? | How many patients are available? | How many outcome events are available? | How many candidate predictors are available? | Was preprocessing done inside validation? | Was feature selection done inside validation? | Was tuning performed without touching the test set? | Were batch effects assessed? | Was calibration assessed? | Was external validation performed? | Are selected features stable? | Are biological claims appropriately cautious? | .

921.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
921.title: 20. Reporting high-dimensional penalised models
921.content: A clear report should describe: . | sample size; | number of outcome events; | number of candidate predictors; | preprocessing workflow; | feature filtering; | batch handling; | penalised method used; | tuning method; | validation design; | discrimination; | calibration; | external validation if available; | selected features if relevant; | limitations of biological interpretation. | . Transparent reporting is essential because high-dimensional models are easy to overstate.

922.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
922.title: 21. Common mistakes
922.content: Common mistakes include: . | using ordinary regression with too many predictors; | selecting features before validation; | preprocessing using the full dataset; | tuning on the test set; | ignoring batch effects; | reporting only AUC; | interpreting selected features as causal; | claiming clinical usefulness without external validation. | . These mistakes can make a weak model look strong.

923.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
923.title: 22. Summary
923.content: High-dimensional biomedical prediction is challenging because the number of predictors may be far larger than the number of patients. Penalised regression methods help by shrinking coefficients and controlling model complexity. Ridge shrinks all coefficients. Lasso can select predictors. Elastic net combines ridge and lasso. However, these methods do not remove the need for careful validation, leakage prevention, calibration assessment, and cautious interpretation.

925.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
925.title: Key terms
925.content: | Term | Meaning | . | High-dimensional data | Data with many predictors, often more predictors than observations | . | p » n | Number of predictors much larger than number of observations | . | Penalised regression | Regression with a penalty to control model complexity | . | Ridge regression | Penalised method that shrinks coefficients | . | Lasso regression | Penalised method that can select predictors | . | Elastic net | Penalised method combining ridge and lasso | . | Lambda | Tuning parameter controlling penalty strength | . | Alpha | Tuning parameter controlling ridge-lasso balance | . | Nested validation | Validation separating tuning from performance estimation | . | Batch effect | Technical variation unrelated to biological signal | . | Preprocessing leakage | Leakage caused by learning preprocessing from validation or test data | . | External validation | Testing a model in independent data | .

927.doc: Note 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
927.title: Key takeaway
927.content: Penalised regression is essential for many high-dimensional biomedical prediction problems, but high performance is only credible when tuning, preprocessing, feature selection, validation, and calibration are handled without leakage.

930.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
930.title: Aim
930.content: This exercise helps you understand why penalised regression is important in high-dimensional biomedical prediction. You will practise thinking about: . | p » n problems; | ridge, lasso, and elastic net; | tuning and nested validation; | preprocessing leakage; | batch effects; | calibration; | cautious interpretation of selected features. | .

931.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
931.title: Part A: Conceptual understanding
931.content: Question 1 . What does high-dimensional data mean? . Use the notation: . p >> n . in your explanation. Question 2 . Give four examples of high-dimensional biomedical data. Question 3 . Why can ordinary regression fail when there are far more predictors than patients? . Question 4 . What is penalised regression? .

932.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
932.title: Part B: Method comparison
932.content: Question 5 . Complete the table. | Method | Main behaviour | Useful when | . | Ridge |   |   | . | Lasso |   |   | . | Elastic net |   |   | . Question 6 . Which method would you consider in each situation? . | Situation | Possible method | . | Many correlated features may each contain weak signal |   | . | A sparse biomarker panel is desired |   | . | Correlated omics features and some feature selection are desired |   | . | Feature stability is more important than sparsity |   | . | A compromise between ridge and lasso is needed |   | .

933.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
933.title: Part C: High-dimensional scenario
933.content: A dataset contains: . n = 300 participants p = 450,000 CpG methylation sites . Question 7 . Why is this a high-dimensional problem? . Question 8 . Why would ordinary logistic regression not be appropriate here? . Question 9 . Why might lasso be attractive in this setting? . Question 10 . Why might elastic net be preferable to pure lasso? .

934.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
934.title: Part D: Tuning and validation
934.content: Question 11 . What does lambda control in penalised regression? . Question 12 . What does alpha control in elastic net? . Question 13 . A researcher tunes lambda repeatedly using the final test set. Why is this a problem? . Question 14 . What is nested cross-validation? . Question 15 . Why is nested cross-validation useful in high-dimensional prediction? .

935.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
935.title: Part E: Preprocessing leakage
935.content: Question 16 . A researcher performs PCA using the full dataset, then runs cross-validation using the PCA components. What is wrong with this workflow? . Question 17 . How should PCA or other preprocessing be handled in cross-validation? . Question 18 . Give three examples of preprocessing steps that can cause leakage if performed before validation.

936.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
936.title: Part F: Batch effects
936.content: Question 19 . What is a batch effect? . Question 20 . Give three examples of batch effects in biomedical data. Question 21 . Why can batch effects be especially dangerous in high-dimensional prediction? .

937.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
937.title: Part G: Calibration and interpretation
937.content: Question 22 . Why is AUC alone not enough to evaluate a high-dimensional medical prediction model? . Question 23 . List four additional assessments that should be considered. Question 24 . A penalised model selects 20 genes. Can we conclude that these 20 genes are causal mechanisms? . Explain your answer.

938.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
938.title: Part H: Full case analysis
938.content: A team builds a recurrence prediction model using: . 500 cancer patients 80 recurrence events 20,000 gene expression features . They use elastic net and report: . Internal cross-validated AUC = 0.91 . They do not report calibration or external validation. Question 25 . Why should we be cautious about this result? . Question 26 . What additional information should be reported? . Question 27 . Write a better analysis plan for this study.

939.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
939.title: Part I: True or false
939.content: | Statement | True / False | . | High-dimensional data often means p is much larger than n. |   | . | Penalised regression removes the need for validation. |   | . | Lasso can select features. |   | . | Ridge usually keeps all predictors. |   | . | Elastic net can be useful with correlated features. |   | . | Preprocessing can cause leakage if done before validation. |   | . | High internal AUC proves clinical usefulness. |   | . | External validation is important for high-dimensional models. |   | .

940.doc: Exercise 15 - Penalised Regression for High-Dimensional Data
940.title: Suggested answers
940.content: Answer 1 . High-dimensional data contain many predictors, often far more predictors than observations. This is written as p >> n, where p is the number of predictors and n is the number of observations. Answer 2 . Examples include gene expression, DNA methylation, proteomics, metabolomics, microbiome data, radiomics, and large electronic health record feature sets. Answer 3 . Ordinary regression can fail because there are too many coefficients to estimate, leading to unstable estimates, overfitting, perfect separation, and poor generalisation. Answer 4 . Penalised regression adds a penalty to the model-fitting process to control model complexity and reduce overfitting. Answer 5 . | Method | Main behaviour | Useful when | . | Ridge | Shrinks all coefficients | Many correlated predictors may each contribute | . | Lasso | Shrinks some coefficients to zero | A sparse selected feature set is desired | . | Elastic net | Combines ridge and lasso | Predictors are numerous and correlated | . Answer 6 . | Situation | Possible method | . | Many correlated features may each contain weak signal | Ridge or elastic net | . | A sparse biomarker panel is desired | Lasso | . | Correlated omics features and some feature selection are desired | Elastic net | . | Feature stability is more important than sparsity | Ridge or elastic net | . | A compromise between ridge and lasso is needed | Elastic net | . Answer 7 . There are far more predictors than participants: 450,000 CpG sites and only 300 participants. This is a p >> n problem. Answer 8 . Ordinary logistic regression cannot safely estimate so many coefficients from so few observations. It would overfit or fail to estimate stable coefficients. Answer 9 . Lasso may be attractive because it can shrink many coefficients to zero and select a smaller set of CpG sites. Answer 10 . Elastic net may be preferable because neighbouring CpG sites are often correlated. Elastic net can handle correlated predictors more smoothly than pure lasso. Answer 11 . lambda controls the strength of the penalty. Answer 12 . alpha controls the balance between ridge-like and lasso-like behaviour. Answer 13 . The test set becomes part of model development, so the final test performance may be optimistic. Answer 14 . Nested cross-validation separates model tuning from performance estimation. The inner loop tunes the model, while the outer loop estimates performance. Answer 15 . It is useful because high-dimensional workflows involve many choices, such as feature selection, preprocessing, and tuning. Nested validation helps avoid optimistic performance estimates. Answer 16 . The validation folds influenced the PCA components because PCA was performed using the full dataset. This creates preprocessing leakage. Answer 17 . PCA or preprocessing should be learned inside each training fold and then applied to the validation fold. Answer 18 . Examples include PCA, normalisation, scaling, feature filtering, batch correction, imputation, and outcome-based feature selection. Answer 19 . A batch effect is technical variation unrelated to biology. Answer 20 . Examples include sequencing run, methylation plate, lab technician, processing date, scanner, hospital site, or assay platform. Answer 21 . With many features, the model may find technical patterns that are associated with outcome in the development data but do not generalise externally. Answer 22 . AUC measures discrimination but not calibration or clinical usefulness. A model may rank patients well but give inaccurate risk probabilities. Answer 23 . Additional assessments include calibration plot, calibration slope, calibration intercept, Brier score, decision curve analysis, external validation, and feature stability. Answer 24 . No. Selected genes may be causal, but they may also be proxies, correlated features, batch artefacts, or selected by chance. Prediction selection is not causal proof. Answer 25 . The AUC may be optimistic, especially if preprocessing, tuning, or feature selection were not properly nested. Calibration and external validation are missing. Answer 26 . They should report preprocessing details, feature selection procedure, tuning method, validation design, calibration, Brier score, selected features, feature stability, batch handling, and external validation if available. Answer 27 . A better plan: . 1. Define prediction question and time point. 2. Split data or define outer cross-validation folds. 3. Within each training fold, perform preprocessing and feature filtering. 4. Tune elastic net using inner cross-validation. 5. Evaluate predictions in the outer validation fold. 6. Assess AUC, calibration, Brier score, and clinical usefulness. 7. Check feature stability. 8. Validate externally if possible. 9. Interpret selected genes cautiously.

942.title: About
942.content: Machine Learning in Biostatistics is a structured R-based course on machine learning for medical statistics, clinical prediction modelling, survival analysis, and biomedical data science. The course is designed for learners who want to understand machine learning through a medical statistics and biostatistics lens, rather than only as a general data science topic.

943.title: Course aim
943.content: The aim of this course is to help learners understand how machine learning methods should be used, interpreted, validated, and reported in medical and biomedical research. The course focuses on questions such as: . | What is the difference between prediction and inference? | Why is validation important? | How does overfitting happen? | What is data leakage? | How should sensitivity, specificity, ROC AUC, and calibration be interpreted? | When is a prediction model clinically useful? | Why is external validation needed? | How can R be used to build reproducible medical prediction workflows? | .

944.title: Course style
944.content: This is a written, applied, R-based learning resource. The course includes: . | story-based lecture pages; | detailed theory notes; | practice exercises; | reproducible R scripts; | applied clinical case studies; | figures and interpretation. | . The lectures use a conversational style with recurring teaching characters, doubts, examples, and clinical discussions. The notes remain more detailed and academic. The goal is to connect statistical concepts with realistic medical prediction problems.

945.title: Who this course is for
945.content: This course may be useful for: . | students in biostatistics, medical statistics, epidemiology, or health data science; | researchers working with biomedical or clinical data; | learners who know basic statistics and want to understand medical machine learning; | students preparing for projects involving prediction modelling, survival analysis, or biomedical data science; | analysts who want to improve their understanding of model validation and interpretation. | .

946.title: About the author
946.content: This course is developed by Rahul. Rahul has an MSc in Statistics from the Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur and is completing an MSc in Medical Statistics and Health Data Science at the University of Bristol. His interests include: . | medical statistics; | clinical prediction modelling; | survival analysis; | epidemiology; | high-dimensional biomedical data; | reproducible R workflows; | machine learning in biostatistics. | . Rahul is also the founder of My Academic Tutor, focused on: . My Academic Tutor is an academic tutoring team supporting learners across statistics, biostatistics, programming, data science, bioinformatics and quantitative research methods.

947.title: Contact
947.content: For errors, suggestions, or feedback: . rahulbalwan1910@gmail.com . LinkedIn: . Rahul Rahul .

948.title: R Code
948.content: This section contains reproducible R scripts for the course. The aim is not only to run models, but to understand the full medical prediction workflow: . | defining the outcome and predictors; | preparing the data; | splitting data into training and test sets; | fitting prediction models; | evaluating discrimination and calibration; | interpreting results clinically; | understanding feature selection and regularisation; | producing reproducible figures and outputs. | . Most scripts use the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset so that the examples connect naturally across the course.

949.title: How to use the scripts
949.content: You can run any script from the terminal using: . Rscript path/to/script.R . For example: . Rscript r/06_cross_validation.R . or: . Rscript r/15_penalised_regression_high_dimensional_data.R . or: . Rscript r/C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R . You can also open the scripts in RStudio and run them section by section.

950.title: Required R packages
950.content: Install the required packages once before running the scripts: . install.packages(c( "tidyverse", "mlbench", "pROC", "broom", "glmnet" )) . These packages are used for: . | Package | Purpose | . | tidyverse | Data manipulation and plotting | . | mlbench | Example datasets | . | pROC | ROC curves and AUC | . | broom | Tidy model summaries | . | glmnet | Ridge, lasso, and elastic net models | .

951.title: Available R scripts
951.content: | Topic | Script | Related material | . | R setup | r/01_setup.R | General setup | . | Validation, overfitting and data leakage | r/02_validation_overfitting_leakage.R | Module 1 | . | Supervised learning basics | r/03_supervised_learning_basics.R | Module 2 | . | Logistic regression for clinical prediction | r/04_logistic_regression_clinical_prediction.R | Module 2 | . | Classification metrics in medicine | r/05_classification_metrics_in_medicine.R | Module 2 | . | Cross-validation | r/06_cross_validation.R | Module 3 | . | Bootstrap validation | r/07_bootstrap_validation.R | Module 3 | . | Calibration and Brier score | r/08_calibration_brier_score.R | Module 3 | . | Decision curve analysis | r/09_decision_curve_analysis.R | Module 3 | . | Regularisation and feature selection | r/10_regularisation_feature_selection.R | Module 4 | . | Ridge regression | r/11_ridge_regression.R | Module 4 | . | Lasso regression | r/12_lasso_regression.R | Module 4 | . | Elastic net | r/13_elastic_net.R | Module 4 | . | Feature selection in biomedical data | r/14_feature_selection_biomedical_data.R | Module 4 | . | Penalised regression for high-dimensional data | r/15_penalised_regression_high_dimensional_data.R | Module 4 | . | Diabetes risk prediction case study | r/C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R | Case Study 1 | .

952.title: Suggested running order
952.content: For the smoothest learning experience, run the scripts in this order: . Rscript r/01_setup.R Rscript r/02_validation_overfitting_leakage.R Rscript r/03_supervised_learning_basics.R Rscript r/04_logistic_regression_clinical_prediction.R Rscript r/05_classification_metrics_in_medicine.R Rscript r/06_cross_validation.R Rscript r/07_bootstrap_validation.R Rscript r/08_calibration_brier_score.R Rscript r/09_decision_curve_analysis.R Rscript r/10_regularisation_feature_selection.R Rscript r/11_ridge_regression.R Rscript r/12_lasso_regression.R Rscript r/13_elastic_net.R Rscript r/14_feature_selection_biomedical_data.R Rscript r/15_penalised_regression_high_dimensional_data.R Rscript r/C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R . This sequence moves from basic prediction modelling to validation, calibration, clinical usefulness, regularisation, feature selection, high-dimensional prediction, and finally a complete applied case study.

953.title: What each script teaches
953.content: 01_setup.R . Introduces the basic R setup for the course. Use this script to check that R is working and that required packages can be loaded. 02_validation_overfitting_leakage.R . Shows why training performance can be misleading. This script compares training and test performance, then demonstrates how an artificial leakage variable can make a model look unrealistically strong. Main ideas: . | training performance; | test performance; | overfitting; | artificial data leakage; | why predictors must be available at the prediction time. | . 03_supervised_learning_basics.R . Introduces the basic supervised learning workflow. Main ideas: . | outcome and predictors; | binary classification; | train/test split; | logistic regression as a first model; | predicted probabilities; | ROC curve and AUC. | . 04_logistic_regression_clinical_prediction.R . Uses logistic regression as a clinical prediction model. Main ideas: . | logistic regression for binary outcomes; | predicted probabilities; | odds ratios; | clinical interpretation; | test-set performance; | why statistical significance is not the same as prediction performance. | . 05_classification_metrics_in_medicine.R . Shows why accuracy alone is not enough in medical prediction. Main ideas: . | confusion matrix; | accuracy; | sensitivity; | specificity; | positive predictive value; | negative predictive value; | threshold choice; | ROC curve and AUC. | . 06_cross_validation.R . Introduces 5-fold cross-validation. Main ideas: . | internal validation; | fold-based model evaluation; | variation in AUC across folds; | why cross-validation is more stable than one train/test split; | why cross-validation does not replace external validation. | . 07_bootstrap_validation.R . Introduces bootstrap validation and optimism correction. Main ideas: . | apparent performance; | bootstrap sampling; | optimism; | optimism-corrected AUC; | internal validation. | . 08_calibration_brier_score.R . Assesses calibration and probability prediction error. Main ideas: . | predicted probabilities; | calibration plot; | calibration intercept; | calibration slope; | Brier score; | difference between discrimination and calibration. | . 09_decision_curve_analysis.R . Introduces decision curve analysis using a simple manual implementation. Main ideas: . | clinical usefulness; | threshold probability; | net benefit; | treat-all strategy; | treat-none strategy; | model-based decision strategy. | . 10_regularisation_feature_selection.R . Compares ordinary logistic regression with regularised models. Main ideas: . | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net; | coefficient shrinkage; | feature selection; | why selected predictors are not automatically causal. | . 11_ridge_regression.R . Focuses specifically on ridge regression. This script compares ordinary logistic regression with ridge regression for diabetes prediction. Main ideas: . | coefficient shrinkage; | ridge penalty; | alpha = 0; | lambda.min; | ordinary logistic regression versus ridge regression; | why ridge usually keeps predictors; | why ridge may help with correlated predictors. | . 12_lasso_regression.R . Focuses specifically on lasso regression. This script fits a lasso model and prints the predictors retained by the model. Main ideas: . | lasso penalty; | alpha = 1; | coefficient shrinkage; | coefficients shrunk to zero; | feature selection; | sparse prediction models; | why selected predictors are not automatically causal. | . 13_elastic_net.R . Focuses on elastic net regression. This script fits elastic net models using different alpha values and compares their test AUC. Main ideas: . | elastic net; | alpha as the ridge-lasso mixing parameter; | alpha = 0 as ridge; | alpha = 1 as lasso; | intermediate alpha values; | comparing alpha values; | selecting features from the best elastic net model. | . 14_feature_selection_biomedical_data.R . Demonstrates feature selection in biomedical data. This script compares a leakage-prone feature selection workflow with a more honest workflow where feature selection is performed using training data only. Main ideas: . | feature ranking; | univariable AUC; | feature selection; | data leakage; | training-only feature selection; | why feature selection must be part of validation; | selected features as prediction features, not causal findings. | . 15_penalised_regression_high_dimensional_data.R . Simulates a high-dimensional biomedical prediction problem. This script creates a dataset with many more predictors than true signal features and compares ridge, lasso, and elastic net. Main ideas: . | high-dimensional data; | p much larger than n; | simulated biomedical predictors; | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net; | selected features; | why high-dimensional models need careful validation; | why selected features are not automatically biologically confirmed. | .

954.title: Case study script
954.content: C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R . This script fits a logistic regression model for diabetes prediction and generates figures for the case study page. It creates: . figures/diabetes_outcome_distribution.png figures/diabetes_roc_curve.png figures/diabetes_calibration_plot.png . Related page: . | Case Study 1: Diabetes Risk Prediction | .

955.title: Current practical focus
955.content: The current R examples cover: . | supervised learning basics; | binary clinical prediction; | logistic regression; | validation and overfitting; | data leakage; | confusion matrix; | sensitivity and specificity; | ROC curve and AUC; | calibration plot; | Brier score; | bootstrap validation; | cross-validation; | decision curve analysis; | regularisation; | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net; | feature selection; | high-dimensional biomedical prediction; | clinical interpretation of prediction models. | .

956.title: Planned R scripts
956.content: Future scripts may include: . | decision trees; | random forests; | gradient boosting; | XGBoost; | variable importance; | partial dependence; | survival prediction; | Cox proportional hazards models; | penalised Cox models; | random survival forests; | C-index for survival data; | time-dependent AUC; | survival calibration; | nested cross-validation; | batch effects and leakage in omics workflows; | EWAS-style prediction examples; | TRIPOD+AI reporting examples; | PROBAST+AI bias assessment examples. | .

957.title: Reproducibility note
957.content: The scripts are written to support reproducible learning. Where appropriate, scripts should: . | set a random seed; | clearly define the outcome; | clearly define the positive class; | clearly define predictors; | avoid data leakage; | separate training and test data; | keep validation honest; | keep feature selection inside validation where appropriate; | avoid using the final test set repeatedly for tuning; | save figures or outputs to the correct folders where relevant; | include comments explaining the clinical interpretation. | . The goal is not only to run R code, but to understand how each modelling step supports reliable medical prediction.

958.title: Exercises
958.content: This section contains practice exercises for Machine Learning in Biostatistics. Exercises are designed to test both conceptual understanding and applied reasoning. Many exercises include answer sections so learners can first attempt the question and then check their reasoning.

959.content: For each topic: . | Try the exercise before checking the answer. | Write your answer in your own words. | Compare your answer with the explanation. | Revisit the lecture or note if needed. | Apply the idea in R where possible. | .

962.title: Machine Learning in Biostatistics
962.content: A structured R-based course on machine learning for medical statistics, clinical prediction modelling, survival analysis, and biomedical data science.

963.title: Course overview
963.content: Machine Learning in Biostatistics is designed for learners who want to understand machine learning through a medical statistics and biostatistics lens. The course focuses on: . | prediction versus inference; | validation and generalisation; | overfitting and data leakage; | discrimination and calibration; | clinical usefulness; | regularisation and feature selection; | transparent reporting; | reproducible R workflows; | applied clinical case studies. | . In medical machine learning, high accuracy is not enough. A useful model should be valid, calibrated, clinically interpretable, reproducible, clinically useful, and generalisable to new patients or populations.

969.title: Learning pathway
969.content: A recommended way to use this course: . | Read the lecture page Start with the story-based lecture to understand the intuition, common doubts, and clinical motivation. | Read the detailed note Use the notes for deeper theory, statistical explanation, and examples. | Complete the exercises Test your conceptual understanding and applied reasoning. | Run the R code Implement the method and interpret the results. | Work through the case study Connect the method to a realistic medical prediction problem with figures and clinical interpretation. | .

970.title: Course format
970.content: This website is designed as a structured written learning resource. The lectures use a conversational teaching style to make the ideas more intuitive and memorable. The notes remain more detailed and academic. The exercises, R scripts, and case studies help connect the concepts to practical medical machine learning.

975.title: Case Studies
975.content: This section contains applied medical machine learning case studies. The aim is to connect the course concepts with realistic modelling workflows, clinical interpretation, figures, and common problems that occur in biomedical data analysis. Each case study follows a practical structure: . | clinical question; | dataset description; | outcome and predictors; | prediction time point; | model development in R; | performance evaluation; | figures; | clinical interpretation; | limitations; | reflection questions. | .

976.title: Available case studies
976.content: . | Case Study 1: Diabetes Risk Prediction | Case Study 2: Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction | .

977.title: Current case study progression
977.content: Case Study 1: Diabetes Risk Prediction . This case study introduces a complete clinical prediction workflow using logistic regression. It focuses on: . | binary outcome definition; | train/test split; | logistic regression; | predicted probabilities; | confusion matrix; | sensitivity and specificity; | ROC curve and AUC; | calibration plot; | Brier score; | threshold interpretation; | clinical interpretation. | . Case Study 2: Regularised Diabetes Risk Prediction . This case study extends Case Study 1 by comparing ordinary logistic regression with regularised regression methods. It focuses on: . | ridge regression; | lasso regression; | elastic net; | coefficient shrinkage; | feature selection; | AUC comparison; | Brier score comparison; | calibration comparison; | selected predictor interpretation; | why selected features are not automatically causal. | .

978.title: Planned case studies
978.content: Tree-based methods . | Decision tree diabetes prediction | Random forest diabetes prediction | Gradient boosting diabetes prediction | XGBoost for clinical prediction | Variable importance and partial dependence case study | . Survival machine learning . | Survival prediction in oncology | Cox proportional hazards prediction model | Penalised Cox model case study | Random survival forest case study | Time-dependent AUC and survival calibration case study | . High-dimensional biomedical prediction . | Methylation-based exposure prediction | Omics feature selection workflow | Batch effects and leakage in biomedical prediction | EWAS-style prediction workflow | High-dimensional penalised regression case study | . Reporting and reproducibility . | TRIPOD+AI reporting case study | PROBAST+AI risk-of-bias case study | Model card for a clinical prediction model | Reproducible GitHub workflow case study | .

979.title: How to use the case studies
979.content: A good way to use each case study is: . | Read the clinical question. | Understand the outcome and predictors. | Run the related R script. | Inspect the generated figures. | Read the clinical interpretation. | Answer the reflection questions. | Revisit the related lecture, note, or exercise if needed. | .

980.title: Related R scripts
980.content: The current case study scripts are: . r/C1_diabetes_risk_prediction_case_study.R r/C2_regularised_diabetes_prediction_case_study.R . The figures are saved in: . figures/ .

981.title: Key message
981.content: The case studies are designed to show that medical machine learning is not only about fitting models. A useful clinical prediction workflow must define the prediction question clearly, avoid leakage, evaluate discrimination and calibration, interpret thresholds carefully, and explain limitations honestly.
